宣室志 - Records from the Proclamation Chamber

Translation created with model: Qwen/Qwen3-14B / step-by-step-3step-fewshot

    Home
    1

    宣室志 [唐]张读

    ○尹君
    　　唐故尚书李公诜镇北门时，有道士尹君者。隐晋山，不食粟，常饵柏叶，虽发尽白，而容状若童子，往往独游城市。里中有老父年八十余者，顾谓人曰：“吾孩提时尝见李翁言，李翁，吾外祖也。且曰：‘我年七岁，已识尹君矣，迨今七十余年，而尹君容状如旧，得非神仙乎吾且老，自度能几何为人间人，汝方壮，当志尹君之容状。’自是及今，七十余岁矣，而尹君曾无老色，岂非以千百岁为瞬息耶！

    Explanation

    1

    The Records of the Xuan Room [Tang] Zhang Du

    ○ Yin Jun
    During the time when the former Tang Minister Li Gong Shen was stationed at the northern gate, there was a Daoist named Yin Jun. He lived in seclusion in Jin Mountain, did not eat grain, and often ate pine leaves. Although his hair was completely white, his appearance was like that of a child, and he often wandered alone in the city. In the village, there was an old man over eighty years old who turned to the people and said, "When I was a child, I saw Li Gong and he said, 'Li Gong was my maternal grandfather. And he said, "When I was seven years old, I had already known Yin Jun. Until now, over seventy years have passed, and Yin Jun's appearance has remained unchanged. Is he not a deity? I am old, and I can only estimate how much longer I can remain in the human world. You are still strong, so you should remember Yin Jun's appearance." From then until now, over seventy years have passed, and Yin Jun never showed any signs of aging. Is it not as if a thousand years is just a moment?"

    2

    北门従事冯翊严公绶，好奇者。慕尹之得道，每旬休，即驱驾而诣焉。其后严公自军司马为北门帅，遂迎尹君至府庭，馆于公署，终日与同席。常有异香自肌中发，公益重之。公有女弟学浮图氏，尝曰；“佛氏与黄老固殊致。”且怒其兄与道士游。后一日，密以堇斟致汤中，命尹君饮之。尹君既饮，惊而起曰：“吾其死乎？”俄吐出一物，甚坚，有异香发其中。公命剖而视之，真麝脐也。自是尹君貌衰齿堕，其夕，卒于馆中。严公既知女弟之所为也，怒且甚。即命部将治其丧。后二日，葬尹君于汾水西二十里。

    Explanation

    2

    A North Gate Attendant from Fengyi, Yan Gong Shou, was a man fond of the unusual. He admired Yin's attainment of the Dao and would visit him every ten days during his rest periods. Later, Yan Gong Shou rose from the position of military司马 to become the commander of the northern gate, and he welcomed Yin to his office, lodging him in the government residence and sitting with him all day. Yin often emitted an unusual fragrance from his flesh, which further increased Yan Gong Shou's respect for him. Yan Gong Shou had a younger sister who studied Buddhism. She once said, "Buddhism and Daoism have different goals," and she was angry that her brother associated with Daoists. One day, she secretly mixed a poisonous substance into the soup and ordered Yin to drink it. After drinking it, Yin was shocked and exclaimed, "I am going to die, I suppose." Soon after, he vomited out a solid object with an unusual fragrance. Yan Gong Shou ordered it to be cut open and found it to be a musk pod. From then on, Yin's appearance declined and his teeth fell out. That night, he died in the government residence. Yan Gong Shou, upon learning what his sister had done, was extremely angry. He immediately ordered his subordinates to handle the funeral arrangements. Two days later, Yin was buried twenty li west of the Fen River.

    3

    明年秋，有照圣观道士朱太虚，因投龙至晋山，忽遇尹君在山中。太虚惊而问曰：“师何为至此耶！”尹君笑曰：“吾去岁在北门，有人以堇斟饮我者，我故示之以死。然则堇斟安能败吾真耶！”言讫，忽亡所见。太虚窃异其事。及归，具白严公，曰：“吾闻仙人不死，脱有死者，乃尸解也。不然，何变异之如是耶！”将命发其墓以验之，然虑惑于人，遂止其事
    ○僧契虚
    　　有僧契虚者，本姑臧李氏子，其父为御史于玄宗时。契虚自孩提好浮图氏法，年二十，髡发衣褐，居长安佛寺中。及禄山破潼关，玄宗西幸蜀门，契虚遁入太白山，采柏叶而食之，自是绝粒

    Explanation

    3

    The following autumn, a Daoist named Zhu Taixu from Zhaosheng Temple performed a ritual and arrived at Jin Mountain, where he unexpectedly met Mr. Yin in the mountains. Zhu Taixu was surprised and asked, "Why are you here, Master?" Mr. Yin smiled and said, "Last year, while I was at the Northern Gate, someone gave me a poisonous drink. I showed them death. However, how could such a poisonous drink harm my true nature?" After saying this, he suddenly disappeared. Zhu Taixu privately found the event strange. Upon returning, he reported to Governor Yan, saying, "I have heard that immortals do not die. If there are any who die, it is through the process of '尸解.' Otherwise, how could such a transformation occur?" Governor Yan initially planned to investigate further, but he stopped out of concern for public reaction.

    There was a monk named Qixu, originally the son of a Li family from Gouzang. His father served as an imperial censor during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong. Qixu developed an interest in Buddhism from a young age. At the age of twenty, he shaved his head, dressed in brown robes, and lived in a Buddhist temple in Chang'an. When An Lushan broke through the Tongguan Pass, Emperor Xuanzong fled west to Shu Gate. Qixu fled to Mount Taibai, where he gathered and ate pine leaves. From then on, he stopped eating.

    4

    尝一日，有道士乔君，貌清瘦，须鬓尽白，来诣契虚。谓契虚曰：“师神骨甚孤秀，后当邀游仙都中矣。”契虚曰：“吾尘俗之人，安能诣仙都乎？”乔君曰：“仙都甚近，师可力去也。”契虚因请乔君导其径。乔君曰：“师可备食于商山逆旅中，遇捀即犒于商山而馈焉。或有问师所诣者，但言原游稚川，当有捀子导师而去矣。”契虚闻其言，喜且甚
    　　及禄山败，上自蜀门还长安，天下无事。契虚即往商山，舍逆旅中，备甘洁以伺捀子馈焉。仅数月，遇捀子百余，俱食毕而去。契虚意稍怠，且谓乔君见欺，将归长安。既治装，是夕，一捀子年甚少，谓契虚曰：“吾师安所诣乎？”契虚曰：“吾愿游稚川有年矣。”捀子惊曰：“稚川，仙府也。吾师安得而至乎？”契虚对曰：“吾始自孩提好神仙，常遇至人，劝我游稚川。路几何耳？”捀子曰：“稚川甚近。师真能偕我而去乎？”契虚曰：“诚能游稚川，死不悔。

    Explanation

    4

    Once upon a time, a Daoist priest named Qiao Jun arrived at the abode of Qixu. Qiao Jun had a gaunt appearance, with completely white beard and hair. He greeted Qixu and said, "Your spiritual bone structure is very solitary and exquisite; you will surely wander in the fairy realm in the future." Qixu replied, "I am an ordinary, worldly person. How could I possibly reach the fairy realm?" Qiao Jun said, "The fairy realm is very close. You can make an effort to go there." Qixu then asked Qiao Jun to guide him on the path. Qiao Jun said, "You can prepare food at an inn in Shangshan. When you meet a traveler, reward and feed them in Shangshan. If anyone asks where you are going, just say you originally intended to visit Zhi Chuan, and there will be a traveler who will guide you away." Upon hearing these words, Qixu was very happy.

    After the defeat of An Lushan, the emperor returned from Shu to Chang'an, and the world was at peace. Qixu immediately went to Shangshan, stayed at an inn, and prepared delicious food to await the traveler's arrival. After only a few months, he encountered over a hundred travelers, all of whom were fed and then departed. Qixu's intention began to wane, and he thought Qiao Jun had deceived him, so he planned to return to Chang'an. After preparing for the journey, that night, a very young traveler approached Qixu and asked, "Where is your master going?" Qixu replied, "I have long wished to visit Zhi Chuan." The young traveler was astonished and said, "Zhi Chuan is a divine realm. How could your master possibly reach it?" Qixu responded, "I have been fond of immortals since childhood. I often met enlightened beings who encouraged me to visit Zhi Chuan. It is not far from here." The young traveler said, "Zhi Chuan is very close. Can your master truly accompany me there?" Qixu said, "If I can truly travel to Zhi Chuan, I will not regret it, even if it means death."

    5

    于是捀子与契虚俱至蓝田上，治具。其夕，即登玉山，涉危险，逾岩巘，且八十里。至一洞，水出洞中，捀子与契虚共挈石填洞口，以壅其流。三日，洞水方绝。二人俱入洞中，昏晦不可辨，见一门在数十里外，遂望门而去。既出洞外，风日恬煦，山水清丽，真神仙都也。又行百余里，登一高山，其山攒峰迥拔，石径危，契虚眩惑不敢登，捀子曰：“仙都且近，何为彷徨耶！”即挈手而去。既至山顶，其上坦平，下视川原，邈然不可见矣。又行百余里，入一洞中。及出，见积水无穷，水中有石径，横尺余，纵且百里余。捀子引契虚蹑石迳而去。至山下，前有巨木，烟影繁茂，高数千寻。捀子登木长啸久之，忽有秋风起于林杪，俄见巨绳系一行橐，自山顶而缒，捀子命契虚暝目坐橐中。仅半日，捀子曰：“师可寤而视矣。”契虚既望，已在山顶

    Explanation

    5

    Zhaizi and Qixu both arrived at Lantian, where they prepared their supplies. That evening, they immediately ascended Yushan, crossing dangerous terrain and climbing over rocks and peaks for about eighty miles. They reached a cave where water flowed out. Zhaizi and Qixu carried stones together to block the cave entrance, stopping the flow of water. After three days, the water in the cave finally stopped. Both entered the cave, but it was so dark and dim that they could not see anything. They saw a gate several dozen miles away and proceeded toward it. Once they emerged from the cave, the wind was gentle, the sun was warm, and the mountains and waters were clear and beautiful, a true land of immortals. They continued for over a hundred miles and climbed a high mountain. The mountain had clustered peaks that rose sharply, and the stone path was perilous. Qixu was dizzy and hesitant to climb, but Zhaizi said, "The land of immortals is already near; why hesitate?" He took Qixu's hand and they continued. When they reached the summit, the top was flat and level, and looking down, the rivers and plains were so vast that they could not be seen. They then traveled another hundred miles and entered another cave. When they emerged, they saw an endless accumulation of water with a stone path about one foot wide and over a hundred miles long. Zhaizi led Qixu to step on the stone path and they went away. At the foot of the mountain, they saw a large tree with dense shadows, towering thousands of feet high. Zhaizi climbed the tree and sang loudly for a long time. Suddenly, a cool autumn wind arose from the treetops, and soon they saw a large rope tied to a row of bags descending from the top of the mountain. Zhaizi ordered Qixu to close his eyes and sit in the bag. After only half a day, Zhaizi said, "The master can now wake up and see." Qixu looked around and found they were already at the top of the mountain.

    6

    见有城邑宫阙，玑玉交映在云物之外。捀子指语：“此稚川也！”于是相与诣其所，见仙童百辈，罗列前后。有一仙人谓捀子曰：“此僧何为者，岂非人间人乎？”捀子曰：“此僧常愿游稚川，故挈而至此。”已而至一殿，上有具簪冕者，貌甚伟，凭玉几而坐，侍卫环列，呵禁极严。捀子命契虚谒拜，且曰：“此稚川真君也。”契虚拜。真君召契虚上，讯曰：“尔绝三彭之仇乎？”不能对。真君曰：“真不可留于此！”因命捀子登翠霞亭。

    Explanation

    6

    Seeing cities and palaces, interwoven with pearls and jade beyond the clouds, the guide pointed and said, "This is Zhi Chuan!" They then went together to the place, where they saw a hundred young immortals lined up in front and behind. One immortal said to the guide, "What is this monk doing here? Is he not a mortal?" The guide replied, "This monk often wished to visit Zhi Chuan, so I brought him here." They then arrived at a hall, where a dignified figure with a headdress and crown sat on a jade table, surrounded by strict guards. The guide commanded the monk to pay respects and said, "This is the True Lord of Zhi Chuan." The monk bowed. The True Lord summoned the monk and asked, "Have you defeated the enemies of the Three Kings of Peng?" The monk could not answer. The True Lord said, "You cannot remain here!" He then ordered the guide to ascend the Cui Xia Pavilion.

    7

    其亭亘空，居槛云矗，见一人袒而瞬目，发长数十尺，凝腻黯黑，洞莹心目。捀子谓契虚曰：“尔可谒而拜。”契虚既拜，且问：“此人为谁何瞬目乎？”捀子曰：“此人杨外郎也。外郎，隋氏宗室，为外郎于南宫。属隋末，天下分磔，兵甲大扰，因避地居山，今已得道。此非瞬目，乃彻视也。夫彻视者，寓目于人世耳。”契虚曰：“请寤其目，可乎？”捀子即面请，外郎忽寤而四视，其光益著若日月之照。契虚悸背汗，毛发尽劲。又见一人卧石壁之下，捀子曰：“此人姓乙，支润其名，亦人间之人，得道而至此。”已而捀子引契虚归。其道途皆前时之涉历

    Explanation

    7

    The pavilion stretches into the sky, and the building rises like clouds. I saw a person who was naked and blinking, with hair several feet long, thick and glossy, and dark. The hair shone brightly, capturing my attention and filling my eyes with light. The guide said to the visitor, "You should bow and pay respects." The visitor asked, "Who is this person and why are they blinking?" The guide replied, "This person is Yang Wailang, a member of the royal family of the Sui dynasty. He served as an official in the southern palace. During the end of the Sui dynasty, the world was divided and in chaos, and he retreated to the mountains. Now he has attained enlightenment. This is not physical blinking but a form of spiritual vision. Spiritual vision allows one to see beyond the physical world." The visitor asked, "Can you open his spiritual vision?" The guide then spoke to Yang Wailang, who suddenly opened his spiritual vision and looked around. The light became even more intense, like the sun and moon. The visitor was filled with awe and fear, his back drenched in sweat, and his hair stood on end. Then he saw another person lying under a stone wall. The guide said, "This person is Yi Zhirun." The guide explained, "He is also from the human world but has attained enlightenment and arrived here." Afterward, the guide led the visitor back. The path they took was the same as before.

    8

    契虚因问捀子曰：“吾向者谒见真君，真君问我三彭之仇，我不能对。”曰：“彭者，三尸之姓，常居人中，伺察其罪，每至庚申日，籍于上帝。故学仙者，当先绝其三尸，如是则神仙可得，不然，虽苦其心，无补也。”契虚悟其事。知
    　　自是而归。因庐于太白山，绝粒啄气，未尝以稚川之事语于人。贞元中，徙居华山下。有荥阳郑绅与吴兴沈聿俱自长安东出关，行至华山下，会天暮大雨，二人遂止。契虚以绝粒，故不致庖爨。郑君异其不食，而骨状丰秀，因徵其实。契虚乃以稚川之事告于郑。郑好奇者，既闻其事，且欢且惊。及自关东回，重至契虚舍，其契虚已遁去，竟不知所在。郑君常传其事，谓之《稚川记》

    Explanation

    8

    Che Xu then asked his disciple, "Earlier, I paid respects to the True Lord, who asked me about the enmity of the Three Rebels, but I could not answer." The disciple replied, "The Three Rebels are the surnames of the Three Corpses, who always reside within people, spying on their sins. Every Gengshen day, they record them to the Supreme Deity. Therefore, those who seek immortality must first eliminate the Three Corpses. Only then can one attain immortality; otherwise, even if one suffers, it will be of no use." Che Xu understood this matter.

    From then on, he returned home and lived in Taibai Mountain, abstaining from food and breathing air. He never spoke of the affairs of Zhiyuan to others. During the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang, he moved to live at the foot of Mount Hua. There, a man from Yongyang named Zheng Sen and a man from Wuxing named Shen Yu both came east of the pass from Chang'an and arrived at the foot of Mount Hua. It happened to be evening and raining heavily, so the two of them stopped. Che Xu, abstaining from food, did not prepare any cooking. Zheng was surprised by his fasting and noticed that his bones were well-formed and handsome. He then asked Che Xu for the truth. Che Xu told Zheng about the affairs of Zhiyuan. Zheng, being a curious man, was both happy and surprised upon hearing the story. After returning from the east of the pass, Zheng returned to Che Xu's dwelling, but Che Xu had already fled, and no one knew where he was. Zheng often told the story, calling it The Record of Zhiyuan.

    9

    ○十仙子
    　　唐玄宗常梦仙子十余辈，御卿云而下，列于庭，各执乐器而奏之。其度曲清越，真仙府之音也。及乐阕，有一仙人前而言曰：“陛下知此乐乎此神仙《紫云曲》也。今愿传授陛下，为圣唐正始音，与夫《咸池》、《大夏》固不同矣。”玄宗喜甚，即传受焉。俄而寤，其余响犹若在听。玄宗遽命玉笛吹而习之，尽得其节奏，然嘿不泄。及晓，听政于紫宸殿，宰臣姚崇、宋璟入，奏事于御前，玄宗俛若不闻。二相惧，又奏之。玄宗即起，卒不顾二相。二相益恐，趋出。时高力士侍于玄宗，即奏曰：“宰相请事，陛下宜面决可否。向者崇、璟所言，皆军国大政，而陛下卒不顾，岂二相有罪乎？”玄宗笑曰：“我昨夕梦仙人奏乐曰《紫云曲》，因以授我，我失其节奏，由是嘿而习之，故不暇听二相奏事。”即于衣中出玉笛，以示力士。是日，力士至中书，以事语于二相。二相惧少解。曲后传于乐府

    Explanation

    9

    The Ten Fairy Sons
    Emperor Xuanzong of Tang often dreamed of more than ten fairy sons riding on clouds and descending, arranging themselves in the courtyard, each holding an instrument and playing it. Their tunes were clear and ethereal, truly the music of the celestial realm. When the music ended, one fairy stepped forward and said, "Do you, O Emperor, know this music? It is the divine Ziyun Qu. Now I wish to teach it to Your Majesty, to be the correct beginning sound for the Holy Tang, fundamentally different from the Xianchi and Daxia." Emperor Xuanzong was very pleased and immediately accepted the transmission. Soon he woke up, and the lingering echoes still seemed to be heard. Emperor Xuanzong hastily ordered a jade flute to be played and practiced it, fully grasping its rhythm, but kept silent and did not reveal it. When morning came, he listened to government affairs in the Purple宸 Hall, and the prime ministers Yao Chong and Song Jing entered, presenting matters before the emperor. Emperor Xuanzong seemed not to hear them. The two prime ministers were afraid and presented it again. Emperor Xuanzong immediately stood up and finally did not look at the two prime ministers. The two prime ministers became even more afraid and hurried out. At that time, Gao Lishi was serving Emperor Xuanzong, and he immediately reported, "The prime ministers requested matters, and Your Majesty should personally decide whether to approve or not. What Yao and Song said earlier were all major state affairs, but Your Majesty finally did not look at them, could the two prime ministers be guilty?" Emperor Xuanzong laughed and said, "I dreamed last night that a fairy played music called Ziyun Qu, so he taught it to me. I lost its rhythm, so I kept silent and practiced it, so I had no time to listen to the two prime ministers' reports." He immediately took out a jade flute from his clothes and showed it to Gao Lishi. That day, Gao Lishi went to the Secretariat and told the two prime ministers about the matter. The two prime ministers were a little relieved. The tune was later passed on to the Music Bureau.

    10

    ○章全素
    　　吴郡蒋生，好神仙，弱岁弃家，隐四明山下。尝従道士学炼丹，遂葺炉鼎，爨薪鼓鞴，积十年，而炼丹卒不成。其后寓游荆门，见有行乞于市者，肤甚顇，裸然而病，且寒噤不能语。生怜其穷困，解裘衣之，因命执侍左右。徵其家，对曰：“楚人，章氏子，全素其名。家于南昌，有沃田数百亩，属年饥，流徒荆江间，且十年矣。田归于官，身病不能自振。幸君子怜而容焉。”于是与蒋生同归四明山下。而全素甚惰，常旦寐自逸。蒋生恶骂而捶者不可计。生有石砚在几上，忽一日，全素白蒋生曰：“先生好神仙者，学炼丹且久矣。夫仙丹，食之则骨化为金，如是，安有不长生耶今先生神丹能化石砚为金乎若然者，吾为先生有道术士。”生自度不果，心甚惭。

    Explanation

    10

    Chapter on Zhang Quansu

    Jiang Sheng from Wu County was fond of immortals and, in his youth, abandoned his family to seek seclusion in the foothills of the Four Bright Mountains. He once studied alchemy with a Taoist priest and set up his furnace and cauldrons, heating the fire and pumping air into it, for ten years. Yet, his attempts at creating an elixir of immortality ultimately failed. Later, he traveled to Jingmen, where he saw a beggar in the market, emaciated and bare, sick and shivering, unable to speak. Jiang Sheng pitied the beggar's poverty and hardship, took off his coat to clothe him, and ordered him to serve as his attendant. When Jiang Sheng inquired about his family, the beggar replied, "I am a man from Chu, a son of the Zhang clan, my name is Quansu. My family lived in Nanchang, where we had hundreds of acres of fertile land. Due to famine, we were displaced to the Jingjiang region for nearly ten years. Our land was reclaimed by the government, and I am too ill to support myself. Fortunately, you have shown kindness and allowed me to stay." Thus, Zhang Quansu accompanied Jiang Sheng back to the foothills of the Four Bright Mountains. However, Zhang Quansu was extremely lazy, often sleeping and resting during the day. Jiang Sheng scolded and beat him countless times. One day, Jiang Sheng noticed a stone inkstone on the table and Zhang Quansu said to him, "Master, you have been studying alchemy for a long time. The elixir of immortality, when consumed, transforms bones into gold. If that is true, how could one not live forever? Can your divine elixir turn this stone inkstone into gold? If so, I would be a true master of the Dao." Jiang Sheng realized that his efforts had been in vain and felt deeply ashamed.

    11

    而以他词拒之曰：“汝，佣者，岂能知神仙事乎若妄言，自速笞骂之辱。”全素笑而去。后月余，全素于衣中出一瓢甚小，顾谓蒋生曰：“此瓢中有仙丹，能化石为金。愿得先生石砚，以一刀圭传其上，可乎？”蒋生性轻果，且以为诞妄，诟骂曰：“吾学炼丹十年矣，尚未能穷其妙。佣者何敢与吾喋喋议语耶！”全素佯惧不对。明日，蒋生独行山水间，命全素守舍，于是键其门而去。至晚归，则见全素已卒矣。生乃以箦蔽其尸，将命棺而瘗于野。及彻其箦，而全素尸已亡去，徒有冠带衣履存焉。生大异，且以为神仙得道者。即于几上视石砚，亦亡矣。生益异之。后一日，蒋生见药鼎下有光，生曰：“岂非吾仙丹乎？”即于烬中探之，得石砚，其上寸余化为紫金，光甚莹彻，盖全素仙丹之所化也。生始悟全素果仙人，独恨不能识，益自惭恚。其后蒋生学炼丹卒不成，竟死于四明山

    Explanation

    11

    He dismissed him with other words, saying, “You are but a hired worker—how could you possibly know anything of divine matters? If you speak falsely, you will bring upon yourself the punishment of beatings and scolding.” Quanshu smiled and left. A month later, Quanshu took from his clothes a very small gourd and turned to Jiang Sheng, saying, “This gourd contains immortal elixirs that can turn stone into gold. Might I ask if it would be acceptable for me to take a small portion of your stone inkstone and apply it with a bit of the elixir?” Jiang Sheng, being light-minded and hasty, regarded the claim as nonsense and scolded, “I have studied alchemy for ten years now, yet I have not yet mastered its subtleties. How dare a laborer discuss such matters with me at length?” Quanshu feigned fear and said nothing. The next day, Jiang Sheng went alone to wander among the mountains and streams, ordering Quanshu to guard the house, and then locked the door and left. When he returned in the evening, he found that Quanshu had already died. Jiang Sheng covered the body with bamboo mats and prepared a coffin, burying it in the wilderness. When he removed the mats, Quanshu’s body had vanished, leaving only his cap, sash, clothes, and shoes behind. Jiang Sheng was greatly astonished and believed Quanshu to be a divine being who had attained spiritual cultivation. He looked upon the stone inkstone on the desk, and it too was gone. He was even more astonished. A day later, Jiang Sheng saw a light beneath the alchemy furnace and said, “Could this be my immortal elixir?” He reached into the ashes and retrieved the stone inkstone, which had transformed into purple gold for a few inches, its light brilliant and clear. This was the result of Quanshu’s immortal elixir. At this, Jiang Sheng finally realized that Quanshu was indeed a true immortal, and he regretted not having recognized it, feeling deep shame and anger. Afterwards, Jiang Sheng continued studying alchemy but ultimately failed, and he eventually died in the Four Bright Mountains.

    12

    ○尹真人
    　　犍为郡东十余里，有道观在深岩中，石壁四壅，有颜道士居之。观殿有石函，长三尺余，其上錾出鸟兽花卉，文理纤妙，邻于鬼工，而缄锁极固，泯然无毫缕之隙。里人相传，云是尹喜石函。真人事迹，显于纪传详矣。真人将上升，以石函付门弟子，约之曰：“此函中有符箓，慎不得启之，必有大祸。”于是郡人尽敬之

    Explanation

    12

    True Person of Yin

    In the eastern several miles of Jianwei Prefecture, there is a Taoist temple nestled in a deep cave. The temple is surrounded on all sides by stone walls, and a Taoist named Yan lived there. In the temple hall, there is a stone box, more than three feet long. On top of the box, birds, beasts, and flowers are intricately carved, their patterns delicate and exquisite, nearly divine in quality. The box is sealed and locked with extreme security, without the slightest gap. The villagers passed down the story, saying it is the stone box of Yin Xi. The True Person’s deeds are well-documented in the annals. As he was about to ascend to immortality, he entrusted the stone box to his disciples, warning them, "This box contains magic talismans. Be cautious and do not open it, or there will surely be great disaster." Thus, all the people of the region held it in deep reverence.

    13

    大历中，有青河崔君为犍为守。崔君素以刚果自恃。既至郡，闻有尹真人函，笑谓属官曰：“新垣平之诈见矣。”即诣之，且命破锁。颜道士曰：“此尹真人石函。真人有遣教曰：‘启吾函者有大祸。’幸君侯无犯仙官之约。”崔君怒曰：“尹真人死千岁，安得独有石函在乎吾不信。”颜道士确其词，而崔君固不従
    　　于是命破其锁，久之，而坚然不可动。崔君怒，又以巨絙系函鼻，用数十牛拽其絙，鞭而驱之，仅半日，石函遂开。中有符箓数十轴，以黄缣为幅，丹书其文，皆炳然如新。崔君既观毕，顾谓颜道士曰：“吾向者意函中有奇宝，故开而阅之，今徒有符箓而已。”于是令缄锁如旧

    Explanation

    13

    In the Da Li era, there was a man named Cui Jun from Qinghe who served as the governor of Jianwei. Cui Jun had always relied on his own strength and determination. Upon arriving in the prefecture, he heard about a box belonging to Master Yin, a Daoist master, and laughed at his subordinates, saying, "This is just like the deceit of Xin Yuanping." He immediately went to the box and ordered it to be opened. Yan, a Daoist priest, said, "This is Master Yin's stone box. The master left a message saying, 'Those who open my box will suffer great misfortune.' I hope Your Excellency does not violate the agreement with the immortal officials." Cui Jun became angry and said, "Master Yin has been dead for a thousand years. How can he alone have a stone box? I don't believe it." Despite Yan's insistence, Cui Jun refused to listen.

    Cui Jun then ordered the lock to be broken, but after a long time, the box remained firmly closed. Angered, he attached a large rope to the box and used dozens of oxen to pull it. After half a day, the stone box finally opened. Inside were dozens of scrolls of talismans, written on yellow silk with red ink, all looking fresh and vibrant. After viewing them, Cui Jun turned to Yan and said, "I thought there might be a treasure inside the box, so I opened it to see. Now, it turns out there are only talismans." He then ordered the box to be sealed and locked as before.

    14

    既归郡，是夕，令忽暴卒。后三日而寤。其官属将吏辈，悉诣崔君问之，且讯焉。崔君曰：“吾甚憨，未尝闻神仙事。前者偶开尹真人石函，果为冥官追摄。初见一人，衣紫衣，至寝，谓吾曰：‘我吏于冥司者也。今奉命召君，固不可拒。拒则祸益大矣，宜疾去。’吾始闻忧，欲以辞免，然不觉与使者俱出郡城，仅行五十里，至冥司，其官即故相吕公也。谓吾曰：‘子何为开尹真人石函乎奉上帝命，且削君之禄寿，果如何哉’已而召椽吏至，令按吾禄寿之籍。掾吏白吕公曰：‘崔君有官五任，有寿十七年。今奉上帝符，尽夺五任官，又削十五年寿。今独有二年寿矣。’”于是听崔君还。后二年果卒

    Explanation

    14

    After returning to the prefecture, that night, the magistrate suddenly died. Three days later, he woke up. All officials and subordinates went to ask Mr. Cui and interrogated him. Mr. Cui said, "I am very foolish and have never heard of any immortal matters. Earlier, I accidentally opened the stone box of the true person Yin, and was indeed chased and captured by the officials of the underworld. Initially, I saw a person wearing purple clothes, who came to my bed and said to me, 'I am an official of the underworld. Now I am sent to summon you, and certainly cannot be resisted. If resisted, the disaster will be even greater, so you should leave quickly.' I initially felt worried and wanted to refuse with excuses, but unconsciously went out of the prefecture city with the messenger, only traveled fifty miles, and reached the underworld. The official there was the former prime minister Lü Gong. He said to me, 'Why did you open the stone box of the true person Yin? By the command of the Supreme Deity, and will reduce your salary and lifespan, what will happen?' Then he summoned the clerks and ordered to check my salary and lifespan records. The clerk reported to Lü Gong, 'Mr. Cui has served in five positions and has a lifespan of seventeen years. Now by the command of the Supreme Deity, will take away all five positions and reduce fifteen years of lifespan. Now only have two years left.' Then allowed Mr. Cui to return. Two years later, he indeed died.

    15

    ○房建
    　　清河公房建，居于含山郡，性尚奇，好玄元之教。常従道士授六甲符及《九章真箓》，积二十年。后南游衡山，遇一道士，风骨明秀。与建语，述上清仙都及蓬莱方丈灵异之事，一一皆若涉历。建奇之。后旬余，建自衡山适南海，道士谓建曰：“吾尝客于南海，迨今十年矣。将有寺官李侯者护其军。李侯以玉簪遗我，我以簪赐君，君宜宝之。”建得其簪，喜且甚，因而别去。是岁秋，建至南海。尝一日独游开元观，观之北轩，有砖涂为真人状者二焉，其位于东者左玄真人，及视左玄之状，果衡山所遇道士也。奇而叹者且久。及睹左玄之冠，已亡簪矣。时有观居道士数辈在焉，建具以事言，次出玉簪示之。道士惊曰：“往岁有寺官李侯，护兵于南海，尝以二玉簪饰左右真人，迨今且十年，其左玄之簪，亡之十年。今君所获果是焉。”建奇之，因以玉簪归道士

    Explanation

    15

    Fang Jian, the Duke of Qinghe, resided in Huan Mountain Prefecture. He had a nature that favored the extraordinary and enjoyed the teachings of the Tao. He often received talismans of the Six Days and the Nine Chapters of True Talismans from Taoists, accumulating twenty years of practice. Later, he traveled south to Mount Heng, where he met a Taoist with a clear and refined demeanor. The Taoist spoke with Jian about the marvelous events of the Upper Clear Palace and the Isles of Penglai, describing each in detail as if he had experienced them firsthand. Jian found this extraordinary. After about ten days, Jian left Mount Heng for the South Sea. The Taoist told Jian, “I once stayed in the South Sea for ten years. There was an official named Li who commanded his troops. Li gave me a jade hairpin, which I gave to you as a gift. You should treasure it.” Jian received the hairpin, greatly pleased, and then parted ways. That autumn, Jian arrived at the South Sea. One day, he visited the Kaiyuan Temple alone. In the northern hall of the temple, there were two brick statues in the likeness of immortals. The one to the east was the Left Xuan True Person. Upon seeing the statue, Jian realized it was the Taoist he had met at Mount Heng. He marveled and remained in awe for a long time. When he noticed the Left Xuan’s crown, he saw that the hairpin was missing. At that time, several Taoists were staying at the temple. Jian explained the entire story to them and then showed them the jade hairpin. The Taoists were astonished and said, “Ten years ago, there was an official named Li who commanded troops in the South Sea. He once used two jade hairpins to adorn the statues of the Left and Right True Persons. It has been nearly ten years since the hairpin on the Left Xuan was lost. The hairpin you have found is indeed the one we lost.” Jian was amazed and returned the jade hairpin to the Taoist.

    16

    ○李贺
    　　陕西李贺，字长吉，唐郑王之孙。稚而能文，尤善乐府词句，意新语丽，当时工于词者，莫敢与贺齿，由是名闻天下。以父名晋肃，子故不得举进士。卒于太常官，年二十四。其先夫人郑氏，念其子深，及贺卒，夫人哀不自解。一夕梦贺来，如平生时，白夫人曰：“某幸得为夫人子，而夫人念某且深，故従小奉亲命，能诗书，为文章。所以然者，非止求一位而自饰也，且欲大门族，上报夫人恩。岂期一日死，不得奉晨夕之养，得非恨哉！然某虽死，非死也，乃上帝命。”夫人讯其事，贺曰：“上帝，神人仙之君也。近者迁都于月圃，构新宫，命曰‘白瑶’，以某业于词，故召某与文士数辈，共为《新宫记》。帝又作凝虚殿，使某辈纂乐章。今为神仙中人，甚乐。愿夫人无以为念。”既而告去。夫人寤，甚异其梦，自是哀少解

    Explanation

    16

    Li He, whose courtesy name was Changji, was a descendant of the Prince of Zheng of the Tang Dynasty. From a young age, he was skilled in writing, particularly excelling in yuefu poetry, known for its novel ideas and beautiful language. At the time, no one dared to compare themselves with him, and thus his fame spread throughout the world. However, because his father's name was Jinsu, he could not take the imperial exams for the position of Jinshi. He died at the age of twenty-four while serving as an official in the Taichang office. His mother, Lady Zheng, loved her son deeply. After Li He's death, she was unable to relieve her grief. One night, she dreamed that Li He came to her, as if he were alive, and said to her, "I am fortunate to be your son, and you love me deeply. Therefore, I followed my parents' wishes from a young age, learning poetry and writing. The reason for this is not just to seek a position for self-decoration, but also to bring honor to our family and repay your kindness. Who would have thought that one day I would die, unable to provide daily care, which is indeed a source of regret! However, I did not die; it was the command of the Heavenly Emperor." Lady Zheng asked him about the matter, and Li He replied, "The Heavenly Emperor is the ruler of gods, immortals, and deities. Recently, he moved the capital to the Moon Garden, constructed a new palace, and named it 'White Jade.' Because I am skilled in literature, he summoned me and several other scholars to write the New Palace Record together. The emperor also built the Hall of Ethereal Tranquility and ordered us to compose musical pieces. Now, I am among the immortals, very happy. I hope you have no worries." Afterward, he departed. Lady Zheng awoke, finding the dream very strange, and her grief somewhat eased.

    17

    ○侯道华
    　　河中永乐县道净院，居蒲中之胜境，道士寓居，有以十数。唐文宗时，道士邓太玄炼丹于药院中，药成，疑功未究，留贮院内，人共掌之。太玄死，门徒周悟仙主院事。时有蒲人侯道华事悟仙以供给使。诸道士皆奴畜之，洒扫隶役，无所不为，而道华愈欣然。又常好子史，手不释卷，一览必诵之于口。众或问之：“要此何为“答曰：“天上无愚懵仙人。”咸大笑之。蒲中多大枣，天下人传，岁中不过一二无核者，道华比三年辄得啖之。一旦，道华执斧斫古松枝垂，且尽如削，院中人无喻其意。明日昧爽，众晨起，道华房中亡所见，古松下施案，致一杯水，仍脱双履案前，道华衣挂松上。院中视之，中留一道诗云：斋

    Explanation

    17

    Hou Dao Hua

    In Yongle County of the Hezhong region, there was a Daoist temple called Daojing, situated in a beautiful location within the area of Pu. There were several Daoists living there. During the reign of Emperor Wenzong of Tang, a Daoist named Deng Tai Xuan was refining elixirs in the medicine hall. When the elixir was completed, he suspected that the work was not yet fully realized, so he left it stored in the courtyard for everyone to watch over. After Deng Tai Xuan's death, his disciple Zhou Wuxian took charge of the temple. At that time, there was a young man from Pu named Hou Dao Hua who served as an attendant to Zhou Wuxian. The other Daoists treated him like a slave, making him perform menial tasks such as sweeping and cleaning, and he did everything they asked without complaint, but he remained cheerful. He also loved history and literature, always reading books and reciting them by heart. Sometimes others would ask him, "Why do you need this?" He would reply, "There are no foolish or ignorant immortals in heaven." Everyone laughed at him. The region of Pu was known for its large dates, and it was said that in a year, there were only one or two seedless ones. Hou Dao Hua, however, managed to eat seedless dates every three years. One day, he held an axe and cut the drooping branches of an ancient pine tree, and they fell as if they had been shaved off. No one in the temple understood the meaning of his actions. The next morning, before dawn, when everyone got up, Hou Dao Hua's room was empty. Under the ancient pine tree, a table had been placed, with a cup of water on it. He had taken off his shoes and placed them in front of the table, and his clothes were hung on the pine tree. The monks looked around and found a poem left inside the tree, which read:

    18

    “帖裹大还丹，多年色不移
    　　前宵盗吃却，今日碧空飞。知
    　　惭愧深珍重，珍重邓天师
    　　他年炼得药，留着与内芝
    　　吾师知此术，速炼莫为迟
    　　三清专相待，大罗的有期。”
    　　下列细字，称：“去年七月一日，蒙韩君赐姓李，名内芝，配住上清善进院。”以次十数言。时唐大中五年五月二十一日。院中人方验道华窃太玄药仙去，因相率白节度使尚书郑公光。按视踪迹不诬，即以其事闻奏。诏斋绢五百匹，并赐御衣，修饰廊殿，赐名“升仙院。”

    Explanation

    18

    Wrapped in a small cloth, the great elixir of return,
    Its color remains unchanged for many years.
    Last night, it was stolen and consumed,
    Today, it flies in the azure sky.
    Ashamed and deeply cherishing,
    Highly value the Taoist master Deng.
    In the future, when the elixir is refined,
    Keep it for Neizhi.
    My teacher knows this technique,
    Quickly refine, do not be late.
    The Three Pure Ones especially wait for you,
    The highest celestial realm has a fixed time.

    Below are the small characters, which read: "Last July 1st, I was granted the surname Li and the given name Neizhi by Lord Han, and was assigned to reside in the Shangqing Shanjin Hall." Following these words, there are several sentences. It was on the 21st day of the fifth month in the fifth year of the Dazhong era of the Tang Dynasty. The people in the hall verified that Dao Hua had stolen the Supreme Mystery Elixir and ascended to immortality. They then collectively reported this to the Military Governor and Grand Secretary Zheng Gongguang. After inspecting the traces and finding them to be true, they reported the matter to the emperor. The emperor then ordered the distribution of 500 bolts of silk, bestowed imperial robes, renovated the halls and pavilions, and named the place "The Hall of Ascending Immortality."

    19

    ○闾丘子
    　　有荥阳郑又玄，名家子也。居长安中，自小与邻舍闾丘氏子偕读书于师氏。又玄性骄，率以门望清贵，而闾丘氏寒贱者，往往戏而骂之曰：“闾丘氏，非吾类也，而我偕学于师氏，我虽不语，汝宁不愧于心乎？”闾丘子嘿然有惭色。后数岁，闾丘子病死。
    　　及十年，又玄以明经上第，其后调补参军于唐安郡。既至官，郡守命假尉唐兴。有同舍仇生者，大贾之子，年始冠，其家资产万计，日与又玄会。又玄累受其金钱赂遗，常与燕游。然仇生非士族，未尝以礼貌接之。尝一日，又玄置酒高会，而仇生不得预。及酒阑，有谓又玄者曰：“仇生与子同舍，会燕而仇生不得预，岂非有罪乎？”又玄惭，即召仇生至。生至，又玄以卮饮之，生辞不能引满。固谢。又玄怒骂曰：“汝市井之民，徒知锥刀尔，何为僭居官秩邪且吾与汝为伍，实汝之幸，又何敢辞酒乎？”因振衣起。仇生羞且甚，挽而退。遂弃官闭门，不与人往来。经数月，病卒

    Explanation

    19

    The Story of Lüqiu Zi

    There was Zheng Youxuan from Xingyang, a son of a distinguished family. He lived in Chang'an and, from a young age, studied alongside the son of a neighbor from the Lüqiu family at the school of a teacher. Zheng Youxuan was arrogant, often boasting about his family's high status. He would mock and insult the Lüqiu family, saying, "The Lüqiu family is not of my class, and yet I study with them. Even if I do not speak, how can you not feel ashamed in your heart?" Lüqiu Zi, silent and with a look of shame, remained silent. Several years later, Lüqiu Zi died of illness.

    Ten years later, Zheng Youxuan passed the Mingjing examination with top honors and was subsequently appointed as a military advisor in Tang'an County. Upon arriving at his post, the county governor appointed him as a temporary military officer in Tangxing. There was a fellow student named Chousheng, the son of a wealthy merchant, who was just turning twenty and whose family had assets amounting to tens of thousands. Chousheng often met with Zheng Youxuan. Zheng Youxuan repeatedly received his money and gifts and frequently went out with him. However, Chousheng was not of the scholar-gentry class, and Zheng Youxuan never treated him with courtesy. One day, Zheng Youxuan held a grand gathering and invited many people, but Chousheng was not invited. When the wine was about to run out, someone said to Zheng Youxuan, "Chousheng is your fellow student, and yet he is not invited to this gathering. Is he not at fault?" Zheng Youxuan felt ashamed and immediately summoned Chousheng. When Chousheng arrived, Zheng Youxuan offered him a cup of wine, but Chousheng refused to drink it all. He firmly declined. Zheng Youxuan then angrily shouted, "You commoner from the marketplace, you only know about small gains. How dare you inappropriately hold an official position? It is your good fortune that I am with you, yet how dare you refuse to drink?" He then ripped his clothes and stood up. Chousheng felt deeply ashamed and was pulled away. He then resigned his position, closed his doors, and ceased to interact with others. After several months, he died of illness.

    20

    明年，郑罢官，侨居濛阳郡佛寺。郑常好黄老之道，时有吴道士者，以道艺闻，庐于蜀门山。又玄高其风，即驱而就谒，愿为门弟子。吴道士曰：“子既慕神仙，当且居山林，无为汲汲于尘俗间。”又玄喜谢曰：“先生真有道者。某愿为隶于左右，其可乎？”道士许而留之。凡十五年，又玄志稍惰。吴道士曰：“子不能固其心，徒为居山林中，无补矣。”又玄即辞去。燕游濛阳郡久之

    Explanation

    20

    The following year, Zheng was dismissed from office and temporarily lived in a Buddhist temple in Mengyang Commandery. Zheng often admired the teachings of Huang-Lao, and at that time, there was a Daoist priest from Wu who was renowned for his Daoist arts and had built a hut on Shumenshan Mountain. Xuan admired his style and was driven to pay homage, wishing to become his disciple. The Daoist priest said, "Since you admire immortals, you should live in the mountains and forests, and do not be eager and busy in worldly affairs." Xuan happily thanked him, saying, "Sir, you are indeed a truly enlightened person. I wish to serve as a subordinate to you. Is it possible?" The Daoist priest agreed and kept him. For fifteen years, Xuan's resolve gradually weakened. The Daoist priest said, "You cannot maintain a firm resolve, and merely living in the mountains and forests is of no benefit." Xuan then left and leisurely wandered in Mengyang Commandery for a long time.

    21

    其后东入长安，次褒城，舍逆旅氏。遇一童儿，十余岁，貌甚秀，又玄与之语，其辩慧千转万化，又玄自谓不能及。已而谓又玄曰：“我与君故人有年矣，君省之乎？”又玄曰：“忘矣。”童儿曰：“吾尝生闾丘氏之门，居长安中，与子偕学于师氏，子以我寒贱，且曰：‘非吾类也。’后又为仇氏子，尉于唐兴，与子同舍，子受我金钱赂遗甚多，然子未尝以礼貌遇我，骂我市井之民。何吾子骄傲之甚邪”又玄惊，因再拜谢曰：“诚吾之罪也。然子非圣人，安得知三生事乎？”童儿曰：“我太清真人。上帝以汝有道气，故生我于人间，与汝为友，将授真仙之诀。而汝以性骄傲，终不能得其道。吁，可悲乎！”言讫，忽亡所见。又玄既寤其事，甚惭恚，竟以忧卒

    Explanation

    21

    Later, Yuanxuan traveled eastward into Chang'an, stopping at Baocheng and lodging at an inn. There, he met a child who was about ten years old and had a very beautiful appearance. Yuanxuan conversed with the child, who displayed extraordinary eloquence and wisdom, changing topics effortlessly. Yuanxuan felt that he could not match the child's intelligence. The child then said to Yuanxuan, "I have been friends with you for many years, do you remember?" Yuanxuan replied, "I have forgotten." The child continued, "I once lived in the house of Lüqiu and resided in Chang'an, where we studied together under a teacher. You considered me poor and lowly, and said, 'not of my kind.' Later, I became a son of the Qiu family and served as a magistrate in Tangxing, living in the same quarters with you. You received a lot of money from me, but you never treated me with courtesy, calling me a market person. How arrogant you are!" Yuanxuan was surprised and then bowed twice, apologizing, "It is indeed my fault. But you are not a sage, how can you know about three lives?" The child replied, "I am a true person of the Supreme Clarity. The Heavenly Lord has given you the breath of the Dao, so he sent me to live among humans, to be your friend, and to teach you the secrets of true immortality. But you are proud by nature, and you will never achieve the Dao. Alas, it is sad!" After speaking, he suddenly disappeared. Yuanxuan then understood the matter, feeling very ashamed and angry, and eventually died of worry.

    22

    ○袁隐居
    　　贞元中，有袁隐居者，家于湘楚间，善《阴阳占诀歌》一百二十章。时故相国李公吉甫，自尚书郎谪官东南。一日，隐居来谒公。公久闻其名，即延与语。公命算己之禄仕，隐居曰：“公之禄真将相也。公之寿九十三矣。”李公曰：“吾之先未尝有及七十者，吾何敢望九十三乎？”隐居曰：“运算举数，乃九十三耳。”其后李公果相宪宗皇帝，节制淮南，再入相而薨，年五十六，时元和九年十月三日也。校其年月日，亦符九十三之数。岂非悬解之妙乎隐居著《阴阳占诀歌》，李公序其首

    Explanation

    22

    The Hermit Yuan
    During the reign of Emperor Dezong of the Tang Dynasty, there was a hermit named Yuan who lived in the region of Xiang and Chu. He was skilled in the "Yin-Yang Divination Song" consisting of one hundred and twenty chapters. At that time, the former chancellor Li Jifu, who had been demoted to the southeast from his position as a high-ranking official, received a visit from the hermit. Li Jifu had long heard of the hermit's reputation and invited him to speak. Li Jifu asked the hermit to calculate his own fortune and career. The hermit said, "Your fortune truly leads to the position of chancellor. Your lifespan will be ninety-three years." Li Jifu replied, "My ancestors have never lived past seventy, how could I dare to hope for ninety-three years?" The hermit said, "When calculating the numbers and figures, it is indeed ninety-three." Later, Li Jifu indeed became the chancellor of Emperor Xianzong, governed the region of Huainan, and then returned to the capital to serve as chancellor again before passing away at the age of fifty-six on October 3, 814. When comparing the year, month, and day of his death, it also corresponded to the number ninety-three. Is this not a profound insight into the mysteries of divination? The hermit wrote the "Yin-Yang Divination Song," and Li Jifu wrote a preface for it.

    23

    ○程逸人
    　　上党有程逸人者，有符术。刘悟为泽潞节度，临沼县民萧季平，家甚富，忽一日无疾暴卒。逸人尝受平厚惠。闻其死，即驰往视之，语其子云：“尔父未当死，盖为山神所召，治之尚可活。”于是朱书一符，向空掷之，仅食顷，季平果苏。其子问父：“向安适乎？”季平曰：“我今日方起，忽见一绿衣人云：霍山神召我。由是与使者俱行，约五十余里，适遇丈夫朱衣，仗剑怒目，従空而至，谓我曰：‘程斩邪召汝，汝可即去。’于是绿衣者驰走，若有惧。朱衣人牵我复偕来，有顷忽觉醒然。”其家惊异，因质问逸人曰：“所谓程斩邪者，谁邪？”逸人曰：“吾学于师氏归氏龙虎斩邪符箓。”因解所佩箓囊以示之，人方信其不诬。逸人后游闽越，竟不知所在

    Explanation

    23

    程逸人
    In Shangdang, there lived a man known as Cheng Yiren, who was skilled in the use of talismans. Liu Wu served as the military governor of the Ze-Lu region. A wealthy commoner named Xiao Jiping from Linzhao County died suddenly without any illness. Cheng Yiren had once received great kindness from Xiao Jiping. Upon hearing of his death, Cheng Yiren immediately rushed to see him and told Xiao Jiping's son, "Your father was not meant to die; he was summoned by the mountain god. If we treat him, he can still be saved." Cheng Yiren then wrote a talisman in red ink, threw it into the air, and within a short while, Xiao Jiping indeed revived. His son asked his father, "How were you feeling just now?" Xiao Jiping replied, "I just woke up today and suddenly saw a man in green clothing who said, 'The god of Huoshan Mountain has summoned you.' I then accompanied the messenger on a journey of about fifty miles when we encountered a man in red clothing, wielding a sword and glaring fiercely, who came down from the sky and said to me, 'Cheng Zhanxie has summoned you; you may go now.' The man in green clothing then fled in fear. The man in red clothing took me back with him, and after a short while, I suddenly woke up." The family was amazed and questioned Cheng Yiren, "Who is this Cheng Zhanxie you mentioned?" Cheng Yiren replied, "I studied the dragon and tiger talismans of exorcism from the masters of Shi and Gui." He then showed the talisman bag he was wearing, and the people finally believed him. Later, Cheng Yiren traveled to Minyue, but eventually disappeared without a trace.

    24

    ○骆玄素
    　　赵州昭庆民骆玄素者，为小吏，得罪于县令，遂遁迹而去。令怒，分捕甚急，遂匿身山谷中。忽遇老翁，衣褐衣，质状凡陋，策杖立于长松之下，召玄素讯之曰：“尔安得至此耶！”玄素对：“得罪于县令，遁逃至此，幸翁见容。”翁引玄素入深山，仅行十余里，至一岩穴。见二茅斋东西相向，前临积水，珍木奇花，罗列左右。有侍童一人，年甚少，总角，衣短褐，白衣纬带革舄，居于西斋。

    Explanation

    24

    ○ Luoxuansu
    A man from Zhaoqing, Zhaozhou, named Luoxuansu, served as a minor official but incurred the anger of the county magistrate, and so fled and hid away. The magistrate, enraged, ordered an urgent search, and so Luoxuansu concealed himself in the mountains. Suddenly, he encountered an old man wearing a coarse robe, of plain and unremarkable appearance, standing with a staff beneath a tall pine tree. The old man called Luoxuansu over and questioned him, saying, “How have you come to be here?” Luoxuansu replied, “I incurred the magistrate’s anger and fled here; I am fortunate that you would accept me.” The old man led Luoxuansu into the deep mountains, traveling only ten or so miles before arriving at a rocky cave. There, he saw two thatched huts facing each other, with a pool of water in front of them, and rare trees and unusual flowers arranged on either side. A servant boy, very young, with the childhood hairstyle of a boy, wearing a short coarse robe, a white robe with a woven belt and leather shoes, resided in the western hut.

    25

    其东斋有药灶，命玄素候火，老翁自称东真君，命玄素以东真呼之。东真以药十余粒，令玄素饵之，且曰：“可以治饥矣。”自是玄素绝粒。仅岁余，授符术及吸气之法，尽得其妙。一日，又谓玄素曰：“子可归矣。”既而送玄素至县南数十里，执手而别。自此以符术行里中。常有孕妇，过期不产，玄素以符一道，令饵之，其夕即产，于儿手中得所吞之符。其他神效，不可具述。其后玄素犯法，刺史杖杀之。凡月余，其尸如生，曾无委坏之色，盖饵灵药所致。于是里人收瘗之。时宝历元年夏月也

    Explanation

    25

    In his eastern study, there was a medicine stove, and he ordered Xuan Su to watch the fire. An old man introduced himself as the Eastern True Lord and instructed Xuan Su to address him as such. The Eastern True Lord gave Xuan Su more than ten pills of medicine and told him, "These can cure hunger." From then on, Xuan Su abstained from eating solid food. After barely a year, he was taught talismanic techniques and breathing methods, mastering them all. One day, the Eastern True Lord said to Xuan Su, "You may return now." He then escorted Xuan Su to several miles south of the county, where they parted ways with a handshake. From then on, Xuan Su practiced talismanic magic in the village. There was once a pregnant woman who did not give birth on time. Xuan Su gave her a talisman to eat, and that night she gave birth, with the child holding the talisman in his hand. Other miraculous effects were too numerous to describe. Later, Xuan Su broke the law and was beaten to death by the governor. For more than a month, his body remained as if alive, showing no signs of decay, due to the elixir he had consumed. The villagers then buried him. It was the summer of the first year of the Baoli era.

    26

    ○俞叟
    　　尚书王公潜节度荆南时，有吕氏子，衣敝举策，有饥寒之色，投刺来谒。公不为礼，甚怏怏。因寓于逆旅。月余，穷乏益甚，遂鬻所乘驴于荆州市。有市门监俞叟者，召吕生而语，且问其所由。吕生曰：“吾家于渭北。家贫，亲老无以给旨甘之养。府帅公，吾之重表丈也。吾不远而来，冀哀吾贫而周之。入谒，而公不一顾。岂非命也！”叟曰：“某虽贫，无资食以周吾子之急，然向者见吾子有饥寒色，甚不平。今夕为吾子具食，幸宿我宇下，生无以辞焉。”吕生许诺，于是延入一室。湫隘卑陋，摧檐坏垣，无床榻茵褥。致敝席于地，与吕生坐。语久命食，以陶器进脱栗饭而已。食讫，夜既深，谓吕生曰：“吾早年好道，常隐居四明山，従道士学却老之术。有志未就，自晦迹于此，仅十年，而荆人未有知者。以吾子困于羁旅，得无动于心耶令夕为吾子设一小术，以致归路裹粮之资，不亦可乎？”吕生虽疑诞妄，然甚觉其异。

    Explanation

    26

    Yushou
    During the time when Shangshu Wang Gong Qian was the governor of Jingnan, there was a man surnamed Lü who was wearing worn-out clothes and carrying a staff, showing signs of hunger and cold. He presented a card and came to pay respects. The governor did not show him courtesy, leaving him very displeased. Consequently, he stayed at an inn. After a month, his poverty and destitution worsened, and he eventually sold his donkey at the Jingzhou market. There was an elderly gatekeeper named Yu who summoned Lü and spoke to him, asking about his circumstances. Lü said, "I come from the north of Wei River. My family is poor, and my parents are old and unable to provide them with the nourishment they deserve. The governor is my great uncle. I traveled far to seek his compassion and assistance. I came to pay respects, but he did not even glance at me. Is this not fate?" Yu replied, "Although I am poor and have no means to provide food to help you in your dire situation, I felt very indignant when I saw you showing signs of hunger and cold earlier. Tonight, I will prepare food for you, and I hope you will stay under my roof. You cannot refuse." Lü agreed, and Yu invited him into a room. The room was small, low, and dilapidated, with a collapsed roof and broken walls, lacking beds, cushions, and mats. Yu placed a worn-out mat on the ground and sat down with Lü. After talking for a long time, he ordered food to be served, which consisted of only a simple meal of millet porridge in a pottery dish. After finishing the meal, it was already late at night. Yu then said to Lü, "In my early years, I was fond of the Dao and often lived in seclusion on Sima Mountain, learning techniques to reverse aging from Daoist masters. Despite my aspirations, I have not succeeded, and I have hidden myself here for nearly ten years, and no one in Jingnan knows about me. Seeing that you are suffering in your travels, I wonder if it has moved your heart. Tonight, I will perform a small trick for you to provide the means for your journey home. Would that not be acceptable?" Although Lü was skeptical and found it strange, he was also intrigued by Yu's words.

    27

    叟因取一缶合于地。仅食顷，举而视之，见一人长五寸计，紫绶金腰带，挽而拱焉。俞叟指曰：“此乃尚书王公之魂也。”吕生熟视其状貌，果类王公，心默而异之。因戒曰：“吕乃汝之表侄也。家苦贫，无以给旦夕之赡，故自渭北不远而来。汝宜厚给馆谷，尽亲亲之道。汝何自矜，曾不一顾，岂人心哉今不罪汝，宜厚赀之，无使为留滞之客。”紫衣偻而揖，若受教之状。叟又曰：“吕生无仆马，可致一匹一仆，缣二百匹，以遗之。”紫衣又偻而揖。于是却以缶合于上，有顷再启之，已无见矣。明旦，天将晓，叟谓吕生曰：“子司疾去，王公旦夕召子矣。”及归逆旅，王公果使召之。方见且谢曰：“吾子不远见访，属军府务殷，未果一日接言，深用为愧，幸吾子察之。”是日始馆吕生驿亭，与宴游累日。吕生告去，王公赠仆马及缣二百。吕生益奇之，然不敢言。及归渭北，后数年，因与友人数辈会宿，语及灵怪，始以其事说于人也

    Explanation

    27

    The old man then took a jar and placed it on the ground. After a meal's time, he lifted it and looked inside, seeing a figure about five inches tall, wearing a purple sash and a golden belt, who bowed respectfully. The old man pointed and said, "This is the spirit of the official, Wáng Gōng, the Grand Secretary." Lǚ, upon closely examining the figure's appearance, found it indeed resembled Wáng Gōng, and he secretly found it strange. The old man then warned, "Lǚ is your cousin. His family is very poor, unable to provide daily sustenance, so he has come from the north of Wèi not far away. You should provide generous lodging and food, fully fulfilling the duties of kinship. Why are you proud and not even giving him a glance? Is this human nature? I will not punish you, but you should provide generously, so that he does not become a lingering guest." The figure in purple bowed and seemed to accept the instruction. The old man added, "Lǚ has no horse or servant; you can provide one horse, one servant, and two hundred bolts of silk for him." The figure in purple bowed again. The old man then placed the jar on top and after a while lifted it again, but there was nothing to be seen inside. The next morning, as dawn was approaching, the old man told Lǚ, "You will depart soon; the official will summon you shortly." Upon returning to his inn, the official indeed sent for Lǚ. When they met, the official apologized, saying, "You have come all the way to visit me, but due to the busy affairs of the military office, I failed to have a conversation with you for a day, and I deeply feel ashamed. Please understand." That day, the official provided lodging for Lǚ at the驿站亭 (yì zhàn tíng), and they enjoyed feasts and outings for several days. When Lǚ was about to leave, the official gave him a servant, a horse, and two hundred bolts of silk. Lǚ found the whole experience even more strange, but he dared not speak about it. After returning to the north of Wèi, a few years later, because he met with several friends for a night's stay, and spoke about spirits and strange occurrences, he began to tell others about the matter.

    28

    ○石旻
    　　有石旻者，不知何许人也。浪迹江湖之间，有年数矣。道术玄妙，殆不可测。长庆中，客于宛陵郡。有雷氏子，常为宣城部将，一日与友人数辈会饮于郡南别墅，旻亦在座。其家僮网得一鱼，长数尺，致于舍。是日，雷生与客俱深醉，诸客尽云，独旻宿雷氏别墅。时夏暑方甚，及明日视其鱼，已败烂不可食矣。家童将弃之，旻谓文曰：“此鱼虽败，吾有良药，尚可活之，安可弃耶！”雷生笑曰：“先生妄矣，诚有良剂，安能活此鱼耶！”曰：“吾子幸观之。”于是衣中出一小囊，囊有药数粒，投于败鱼之上。

    Explanation

    28

    A Man Named Shi Min

    There was a man named Shi Min, whose origins were unknown. He had been wandering aimlessly for many years. His mystical arts were profound and almost impossible to comprehend. During the Longqing era, he resided as a guest in Wanzhou. A son of the Lei family, who was a military commander in Xuanzhou, once held a drinking party at a villa in the south of the county, and Shi Min was also present. One of his servants caught a fish with a net, which was several feet long, and brought it to the house. On that day, Lei and his guests were all deeply drunk, and all the guests left, leaving only Shi Min to stay at the Lei family's villa. It was the height of summer, and the next day, when they looked at the fish, it had already spoiled and was inedible. The servant was about to discard it, but Shi Min said to Lei, "This fish may be spoiled, but I have a good medicine that can still revive it; how can we throw it away?" Lei laughed and said, "Master, you are mistaken. If you truly have a good medicine, how could you revive this fish?" Shi Min replied, "My dear, please observe." He then took out a small bag from his clothes, which contained several pills, and threw them onto the spoiled fish.

    29

    仅食顷，其鱼鲜润如初，俄而摇鬣振鳞，若在洪流中。雷生惊异，再拜谢曰：“先生之术，可谓神矣。某辈尘俗聋瞽，望先生高踪，若井鲋之与云禽，焉得而为伍乎。”先是雷生有微疾积年，既而求旻衣中之丹饵，欲冀廖其久苦。旻不可，且曰：“吾之丹至清至廉，尔曹俗人，嗜好无节，脏腑之内，腥膻委集，设使以吾丹饵求置其中，则脏腑之气，与药力相攻，若水火之交战，宁有全人乎慎不可食。”旻又言曰：“神仙不难得，但尘俗多累，若槛猿笼鸟，徒有骞翔超腾之心，安可致乎？”会昌中，卒于吴郡也

    Explanation

    29

    In a short while, the fish was as fresh and moist as when it was first caught, and soon it began to shake its fins and scales, as if it were in a great river. Reborn was amazed and bowed twice in gratitude, saying, "Your artistry, Sir, can truly be called divine. We common folk, deaf and blind to the world, look up to your lofty conduct, as if a frog in a well compared to a bird in the sky, how could we possibly be in the same league?" Previously, Reborn had suffered from a long-standing minor illness. He then sought the elixir in Min's clothing, hoping to alleviate his long-term suffering. Min refused, saying, "My elixir is extremely pure and clear. You common people have no restraint in your desires, and within your bodies, filth and stench accumulate. If I were to place my elixir inside you, the impurities in your body would clash with the power of the medicine, like water and fire battling each other. Could anyone survive such a conflict? Do not eat it." Min also said, "It is not difficult to attain immortality, but the world is full of entanglements. If you are like a monkey in a cage or a bird in a cage, with only the heart of soaring and leaping, how can you achieve it?" In the reign period of Emperor Xuanzong, during the years 841-846, Min passed away in Wu County.

    30

    ○杨居士
    　　南海郡有杨居士，亡其名，以居士自目。往往游南海枝郡，常寄食于人，亦不知其所止。谓人曰：“我有奇术，汝辈庸人，固不得而识矣。”后常至郡，会太守好奇者，闻居士来，甚喜，且厚其礼，命饮之。每宴游，未尝不首召居士。居士亦以此自负。一日，使酒忤太守，太守不能容。后又会宴于郡室，阅妓乐，而居士不得预。时有数客，亦不在太守召中，因谓居士曰：“先生尝自负有奇术，某向者仰望之不暇，一日遇先生于此，诚幸矣。虽然，今闻太守大宴客于郡斋，而先生不得预其间，即不能设一奇术以动之乎必先生果无奇术耶！”居士笑曰：“此末术耳。”君试观我，我为君召其妓，可以佐酒。

    Explanation

    30

    There was once a recluse named Yang Jushi in Nanhai Prefecture, whose name has been lost to history, but who referred to himself as a scholar-retreatee. He often traveled to the outlying districts of Nanhai, lodging with people for food and never knowing where he would stay. He would say to others, "I have a special skill, one that you common folk cannot possibly understand." Later, he would frequently visit the prefecture, and when the governor, who was curious by nature, heard that Yang Jushi had arrived, he was very pleased and treated him with great respect, inviting him to drink. At every banquet and gathering, Yang Jushi was always the first to be summoned. He took pride in this. One day, he drank to excess and offended the governor, who could no longer tolerate him. Later, another banquet was held in the prefectural hall, where female entertainers performed, but Yang Jushi was not invited. At that time, there were several guests who had also not been invited by the governor. They said to Yang Jushi, "Sir, you have always boasted of having a special skill. I have long admired you from afar. It is truly fortunate to meet you here today. However, now that I hear the governor is hosting a grand banquet in the prefectural hall, and you are not invited, can you not perform a special skill to impress us? Or do you really not have any special skills at all?" Yang Jushi smiled and said, "This is just a trivial skill." Then he said, "You just watch me. I will summon the female entertainers for you, and they can help entertain us with their performances."

    31

    ”皆曰：“愿为之。”居士因命具酒，使诸客环席而坐，又命小童闭西庑空室。久之乃启之，有三四美人自庑下来，装饰华焕，携乐而至。居士曰：“某之术何如？”诸客人大异之，殆不可测，乃命列坐，奏乐且歌。客或讯其术，居士但笑而不答。时昏晦，至夜分，居士谓诸妓曰：“可归矣。”于是皆起，入西庑下空室中。客相目骇叹，然尚疑其鬼物妖惑。明日，有郡中吏曰：“太守昨夕宴郡阁，妓乐列坐，无何皆仆地。瞬息暴风起，飘其乐器而去。迨至夜分，诸妓方寤，乐器亦归于旧所。太守质问众妓，皆云黑无所见，竟不穷其由。”诸客皆大惊，因尽以事对，或告于太守，太守叹异，即谢而遣之，不敢留于郡中。时开成初也。知

    Explanation

    31

    Everyone said, "We are willing to do it." The recluse then ordered wine to be prepared, and had all the guests sit in a circle around the seating area. He also ordered a young boy to close the empty room in the western annex. After a long time, the door was opened, and there were three or four beautiful women who came down from the wing, adorned with magnificent decorations, and carrying musical instruments. The recluse said, "What do you think of my skill?" The guests were greatly surprised and found it almost impossible to comprehend. He then had them sit in rows, and they began to play music and sing. Some guests asked about his skill, but the recluse only smiled and did not answer. It was dark, and by midnight, the recluse told the women, "It is time to return." They all stood up and entered the empty room in the western annex. The guests looked at each other in amazement and exclaimed, but they still doubted that it might be the work of ghosts or bewitching spirits. The next day, an official from the county said, "Last night, the governor held a feast at the county hall, and the entertainers sat in rows. Soon, they all fell to the ground. In an instant, a strong wind arose, carrying their musical instruments away. By midnight, the entertainers awoke, and the instruments had returned to their original place. The governor questioned the entertainers, and they all said they saw nothing in the darkness. He could not fully understand the reason behind it." The guests were greatly surprised and recounted the events to the governor, and some reported it to him. The governor exclaimed at the strangeness of it and immediately apologized and sent them away, not daring to keep them in the county. This was during the early years of the Kai Cheng reign.

    32

    ○冯渐
    　　河东冯渐，名家子，以明经入仕。性与俗背，后弃官隐居伊水上。有道士李君以道术闻，尤善视鬼。朝士皆慕其能。李君后退归汝颖，适遇渐于伊洛间，知渐有奇术，甚重之。大历中，有博陵崔公者，与李君为僚，甚善。李君寓书于崔曰：“当今制鬼，无过渐耳。”是时朝士咸知渐有神术数，往往道其名。别后长安中人率以“渐”字题其门者，盖用此也
    ○王先生
    　　有王先生者，家于乌江上，隐其迹，由是里人不能辨，或以为妖妄。一日，里中火起，延烧庐舍，生即往视之，厉声呼曰：“火且止，火且止。”于是火灭。里人始奇之。长庆中，有弘农杨晦之，自长安东游吴楚，行至乌江，闻先生高躅，就门往谒。先生戴玄绡巾，衣褐衣，隐几而坐，风骨清美。晦之再拜备礼，先生拱揖而已，命晦之坐其侧。其议论玄畅，迥出意表。晦之愈健慕，于是留宿。是日乃八月十二日也。

    Explanation

    32

    ○冯渐
    　　Feng Jian, a son of a prominent family from Hedong, entered officialdom through his knowledge of Confucian classics. His nature was contrary to the common ways, and he later gave up his official position to live in seclusion on the Yi River. There was a Daoist adept named Li Jun who was renowned for his Daoist skills, especially skilled in seeing ghosts. Officials all admired his abilities. Li Jun later retired to the regions of Ru and Ying, where he met Feng Jian in the area between the Yi and Luo rivers. Knowing that Feng Jian possessed extraordinary skills, he held him in high regard. During the Dali era, there was a prominent official from Boling named Cui Gong, who was a colleague of Li Jun and had a good relationship with him. Li Jun wrote to Cui Gong, saying, "In controlling ghosts today, no one surpasses Feng Jian." At that time, officials throughout the capital knew of Feng Jian's divine skills and often mentioned his name. After their parting, people in Chang'an often wrote the character "渐" on their doors, using it for this reason.

    ○王先生
    　　There was a man named Mr. Wang who lived by the U River. He hid his traces, so the villagers could not recognize him, and some thought he was a sorcerer or a charlatan. One day, a fire broke out in the village, spreading to the houses. Mr. Wang went to see it and shouted loudly, "The fire will stop, the fire will stop." The fire then ceased. The villagers began to marvel at him. During the Changqing era, there was a scholar from Hongnong named Yang Hui Zhi, who traveled east from Chang'an to Wu and Chu. When he reached the U River, he heard of Mr. Wang's esteemed character and went to visit him. Mr. Wang wore a black silk cap and a brown robe, leaning on a table and sitting. He had a clear and beautiful demeanor. Yang Hui Zhi performed the proper courtesies, bowing twice, and Mr. Wang only bowed and gestured in return, inviting Yang Hui Zhi to sit beside him. His discourse was profound and smooth, far exceeding expectations. Yang Hui Zhi became even more admiring and stayed overnight. It was the twelfth day of the eighth month.

    33

    先生召其女七娘者，乃一老妪也，年七十余，发尽白，扶杖而来，先生谓晦之曰：“此我女也，惰而不好道，今且老矣。”既而谓七娘曰：“汝为吾刻纸，状今夕之月，置于室东垣上。”有顷，七娘以纸月施于垣上。夕有奇光自发，洞照一室，纤毫尽辨。晦之惊叹不测。及晓将去，先生以杖画地，俄有尘起，天地尽晦，久之尘敛，视其庭，则悬崖峻险，山谷重叠，前有积石尽目。晦之悸然背汗，毛发竖立。先生曰：“陵谷速迁，吾子安所归乎？”晦之益恐，洒泣言曰：“诚不知一旦有桑田之变，岂仙都瞬息，而尘世已千岁乎？”先生笑曰：“子无惧也。所以为娱耳。”于是持帚扫其庭，又有尘起。有顷，尘敛，门庭如旧。晦之喜，即驰马而去

    Explanation

    33

    The master summoned his daughter, Miss Qi Niang, who was an elderly woman, over seventy years old, with completely white hair, and she came leaning on a staff. The master said to Hui Zhi, "This is my daughter, who is lazy and not fond of the Dao, and now she is old." Afterward, he told Miss Qi Niang, "You should carve paper to depict the moon of this night and place it on the eastern wall of the room." After a while, Miss Qi Niang placed the paper moon on the wall. In the evening, a strange light spontaneously appeared, illuminating the entire room so clearly that everything was visible. Hui Zhi was amazed and could not fathom it. When dawn came, he was about to leave, and the master drew a line on the ground with his staff. Suddenly, dust arose, and the sky and earth became completely dark. After a while, the dust settled, and when he looked at the courtyard, it was steep and dangerous cliffs, with overlapping valleys, and in front of him were mountains of stones stretching as far as the eye could see. Hui Zhi was so frightened that his heart was pounding, his back was drenched in sweat, and his hair stood on end. The master said, "Mountains and valleys have quickly transformed. Where will you go now, my son?" Hui Zhi was even more afraid and wept, saying, "I truly did not know that there would be such a sudden change in the world. Is it that the immortal realm has changed in an instant, while the mortal world has passed a thousand years?" The master smiled and said, "Do not be afraid. It is just for entertainment." Then he swept the courtyard with a broom, and dust arose again. After a while, the dust settled, and the courtyard returned to its original state. Hui Zhi was happy and rode away on his horse.

    34

    ○周生
    　　唐太和中，有周生者，庐于洞庭山，时以道术济吴楚，人多敬之。后将抵洛谷之间，途次广陵，舍佛寺中。会有三四客皆来。时方中秋，其夕霁月澄莹，且吟且望，有说开元时明皇帝游月宫事，因相与叹曰：“吾辈尘人，固不得至其所矣。奈何？”周生知曰：“某常学于师，亦得焉，且能挈月致之怀袂，子信乎？”或患其妄，或喜其奇。生曰：“吾不为明，则妄矣。”因命虚一室，翳四垣，不使有纤隙。又命以箸数百，呼其僮绳而架之。且告客曰：“我将梯此取月去。闻呼可来观。”乃闭户久之。数客步庭中，且伺焉。忽觉天地曛晦，仰而视之，即又无纤云。俄闻生呼曰：“某至矣。”因开其室，生曰：“月在某衣中耳。请客观焉。”因以举之，其衣中出月寸许，忽一室尽明，寒逼肌骨。生曰：“子不信我，今信乎？”客再拜谢之，愿收其光。因又闭户，其外尚昏晦，食顷方如初。知

    Explanation

    34

    A Tale of Zhou Sheng

    During the middle of the Tang dynasty, there was a man named Zhou Sheng who lived in Dongting Mountain. He was known for using his Daoist skills to help the people of Wu and Chu, and many people held him in high regard. Later, he was traveling through Guangling and stayed at a Buddhist temple. On this night, around the Mid-Autumn Festival, the moon was clear and bright. Zhou Sheng and several guests were reciting poetry and admiring the moon. One of them mentioned the legendary story of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (also known as Ming Huang) visiting the Moon Palace during the Kaiyuan era. They sighed together, saying, "We ordinary people cannot reach that place. What can we do?" Zhou Sheng responded, "I have often studied with my teacher and have also achieved it. Moreover, I can bring the moon into my sleeves. Do you believe me?" Some guests doubted his claim, while others were intrigued by its strangeness. Zhou Sheng said, "If I am not clear, then it is false." He then ordered a room to be emptied and covered the walls so that there was no small gap. He also arranged hundreds of chopsticks and called his servant to tie them up and arrange them. He told the guests, "I will climb this to take the moon away. If you hear the call, you can come to see." He closed the door and stayed inside for a long time. The guests walked around the courtyard, waiting. Suddenly, they felt the sky dimming. Looking up, they saw that there was no cloud. Soon, they heard Zhou Sheng call out, "I have arrived!" He opened the door, and said, "The moon is in my clothes. Please come and see." He then lifted his clothes, and a bit of the moon appeared, making the whole room bright and cold. Zhou Sheng said, "You did not believe me before, do you believe me now?" The guests bowed twice and thanked him, wishing to collect its light. He closed the door again, and outside it was still dim. After a mealtime, everything returned to its original state.

    35

    ○惠照
    　　唐元和中，武陵郡开元寺有僧惠照，貌衰体羸，好言人之休戚而皆中。性介独，不与群狎，常闭关自处，左右无侍童。每乞食于里人，里人有年八十余者云：“照师居此六十载，其容状无少异于昔时。但不知其甲子。”
    　　后有陈广者，由孝廉科为武陵官。广好浮图氏，一日，因谒寺，尽访群僧。至惠照室，惠照见广，且悲且喜曰：“陈君何来之晚耶！”广愕然，自以为平生不识照，则谓曰：“未尝与师游，何见讶来之晚乎？”照曰：“此非立可尽言，当与子一夕静语耳。”广异之。后一日，仍诣照宿，因请其事。照乃曰：“我，刘氏子，彭城人，宋孝文帝之玄孙也。曾祖鄱阳王休业，祖士弘，并详于史氏。先人以文学自负，为齐竟陵王子良所知。子良招召贤俊文学之士，而先人预焉。后仕齐梁之间，为会稽令。吾生于梁普通七年夏五月，年三十方仕于陈，至宣帝时为卑官，不为人知。

    Explanation

    35

    In the Yuanhe period of the Tang dynasty, there was a monk named Hui Zhao at Kaiyuan Temple in Wuling Prefecture. He was frail and weak in appearance, often speaking of people's joys and sorrows, always hitting the mark. By nature, he was introverted and solitary, not socializing with others, often staying in seclusion, with no attendants. He often begged food from the locals. An elderly person in his eighties among the locals said, "Master Zhao has lived here for sixty years, and his appearance is no different from before. But I do not know his age."

    Later, there was Chen Guang, who became an official in Wuling through the Xiu-lian examination. Chen Guang was fond of Buddhism. One day, he visited the temple and met all the monks. When he reached Hui Zhao's room, Hui Zhao saw Chen Guang and said with both sorrow and joy, "Why did you come so late, Mr. Chen?" Chen Guang was startled, thinking he had never met Hui Zhao before, and asked, "I have never been acquainted with the master, why are you surprised that I came late?" Hui Zhao replied, "This cannot be fully explained immediately. I will have a quiet talk with you tonight." Chen Guang found this strange. The next day, he still visited Hui Zhao to stay overnight and asked about the matter. Hui Zhao then said, "I am a descendant of the Liu family, from Pengcheng, the great-great-grandson of Emperor Xiaowen of Song. My great-grandfather was Prince of Poyang, Xiu Yi, and my grandfather was Shi Hong, both of whom are well-documented in historical records. My ancestors were proud of their literary achievements and were known to Prince Zhi Liang of Jingling of Qi. Prince Zhi Liang invited talented scholars and literary figures, and my ancestors were among them. Later, they served as officials during the Qi and Liang dynasties, with my ancestors serving as the magistrate of Kuaiji. I was born in the fifth month of the seventh year of the Tongguan era of the Liang dynasty. I began my career at the age of thirty in the Chen dynasty, and by the time of Emperor Xuan of Chen, I held a low-ranking position, not well-known."

    36

    与吴兴沈彦文为诗酒之交。后长沙王叔坚与始兴王叔陵皆广聚宾客，大为声势，各恃权宠，有不平心。吾与彦文俱在长沙之门下。及叔陵被诛，吾与彦文惧长沙之不免，则祸且相及，因偕循去，隐于山林。因食橡栗，衣一短褐，虽寒暑不更。一日，老僧至吾所居，曰：‘子骨甚奇，当无疾耳。’彦文亦拜请其药，僧曰：‘子无刘君之寿，奈何虽饵吾药，亦无补耳。’遂告去。将别，又谓我曰：‘尘俗以名利相胜，竟何有哉唯释氏可以舍此矣。’吾敬佩其语，自是不知人事，凡十五年。又与彦文俱至建业。时陈氏已亡，宫阙尽废，台城牢落，荆榛蔽路，景阳结绮，空基尚存，衣冠文物，阒无所观。

    Explanation

    36

    I formed a friendship with Shen Yanwen of Wu Prefecture based on poetry and wine. Later, Prince Shu Jian of Changsha and Prince Shu Ling of Shixing both widely gathered guests, making great shows of power and influence. Each relied on their power and favor, harboring discontent. I and Yanwen were both under the patronage of Prince Shu Jian of Changsha. When Prince Shu Ling was executed, I and Yanwen feared that Prince Shu Jian would not escape punishment, and thus our misfortune would follow. So we fled together, hiding in the mountains and forests. We lived on acorns and chestnuts, wearing a single short robe, without changing even through the seasons. One day, an old monk came to where I lived. He said, "Your bones are very unusual, you should not fall ill." Yanwen also bowed and asked for his medicine. The monk replied, "You do not have the lifespan of Liu Jun, how can you, even if you take my medicine, be any better?" So he took his leave. As he was leaving, he said to me, "In the mundane world, people compete for fame and profit, what do they ultimately gain? Only the Buddhist path can transcend this." I admired his words, and from then on, I knew nothing of worldly affairs for fifteen years. Later, I and Yanwen both went to Jianye. At that time, the Chen dynasty had already fallen, and the imperial palaces were all ruined. The Taicheng was desolate, with brambles covering the roads. The Jingyang and Jieqi palaces still stood, though empty. The scholars and cultural artifacts were gone, nothing to see.

    37

    故老相遇，捧袂而泣曰；‘后主骄淫，为隋氏所灭，良可悲乎！’吾且泣不能已。又问后主及陈氏诸王，皆入长安，即与彦文挈一囊，乞食于路，以至关中。吾，长沙之故客也，恩遇甚厚，闻其迁于瓜州，则又径往就谒。长沙少长绮纨，而又早贵，虽流放之际，尚不事生业。时方与沈妃酣饮，吾与彦文再拜于前，长沙悲恸久之，洒泣而起，乃谓吾曰：‘一日家国沦亡，骨肉播迁，岂非天耶！’吾自是留瓜州。数年而长沙殂。又数年，彦文亦亡。吾因髡发为僧，遁迹会稽山佛寺，凡二十年，时已百岁矣。虽容状枯瘠，而筋力不衰，尚日行百里。因与一僧同至长安。时唐帝有天下，建号武德，凡六年矣。吾自此，或居京洛，或游江左，至于三蜀五岭，无不往焉。

    Explanation

    37

    Old friends met, clasped their sleeves, and wept, saying, "The last emperor was luxurious and debauched, and was destroyed by the Sui dynasty, truly a sad thing!" I too could not stop weeping. They then asked about the last emperor and the various princes of the Chen dynasty, who had all entered Chang'an. I immediately went with Yanwen, carrying a bag, begging for food along the way until we reached Guanzhong. I was a former guest of Changsha, who had treated me with great favor. When I heard that he had moved to Guazhou, I went straight to pay respects. Changsha had grown up in luxury and became prominent early, but even during his exile, he still did not engage in productive work. At that time, he was drinking with Consort Shen, and I and Yanwen kneeled and bowed twice in front of him. Changsha grieved for a long time, shed tears, and stood up, then said to me, "One day our country fell, our family members were scattered, isn't it the will of heaven?" From then on, I stayed in Guazhou. Several years later, Changsha died. Another few years passed, and Yanwen also died. I then shaved my head, became a monk, and retreated to a Buddhist temple in Kuaiji Mountain, staying there for twenty years. By then, I was a hundred years old. Although my appearance was withered and thin, my strength did not decline, and I still walked a hundred miles a day. I then went to Chang'an with a monk. At that time, the Tang emperor ruled, established the era name Wu德, and had been reigning for six years. From then on, I sometimes lived in the capital, sometimes traveled to the east of the river, and even went to the three Shus and five ranges, visiting everywhere.

    38

    迨今二百九十年矣，虽烈寒盛暑未尝有微恙。贞元末，于此寺尝梦一丈夫，衣冠甚伟，视之，乃长沙王也。吾迎延坐话旧，伤感如平生。而谓吾曰：‘后十年，我之六世孙广当官于此郡。师其念之。’吾因问曰：‘王今何为’曰：‘冥官甚尊。’既而泣曰：‘师存，而我已六世矣。悲夫！’吾既觉，因纪君之名于经笥中。至去岁，凡十年，乃以君之名氏访于郡人，尚讶君之未至。昨因乞食里中，遇邑吏，访之，果得焉。及君之来，又依然长沙之貌。然自梦及今，十一年矣，故讶君之晚也。”已而悲惋，泣下数行。因出经笥示之。广乃再拜，原执履锡为门弟子。照曰：“君且去，翌日当再来。”广受教而还。明日，至其居，而照已遁去，莫知其适。时元和十一年。知

    Explanation

    38

    Until now, two hundred and ninety years have passed, and despite extreme cold and heat, he has never had a slight ailment. At the end of the Zhenyuan era, I once dreamed of a man whose attire was very grand. Upon looking at him, I realized he was the King of Changsha. I greeted and invited him to sit, talking about old times, and felt saddened as if it were a lifetime. He then said to me, "In ten years, my sixth-generation descendant Guang will hold office in this prefecture. Teacher, remember this." I asked him, "What is the king doing now?" He replied, "He holds a very high position in the afterlife." Afterward, he wept and said, "Teacher, you still live, but I have already been reborn six times. Alas!" I woke up and recorded your name in my collection of scriptures. By last year, ten years had passed, and I sought your name and surname among the people of the prefecture, still surprised that you had not arrived. Yesterday, while begging for food in the village, I encountered a local official, inquired, and indeed found you. When you came, you still looked like the King of Changsha. However, from the dream until now, it has been eleven years, hence the surprise at your late arrival. Afterward, I felt sad and wept several times. Therefore, I took out the collection of scriptures and showed it to you. Guang then bowed twice and wished to be a disciple under your guidance. Teacher Zhao said, "You may go now, and come again tomorrow." Guang received the instruction and returned. The next day, when he arrived at his residence, Teacher Zhao had already disappeared, and no one knew where he had gone. It was the eleventh year of the Yuanhe era.

    39

    至大和初，广为巴州掾，于蜀道忽逢照，惊喜再拜曰：“愿弃官従吾师，为物外之游。”照许之。其夕偕舍于逆旅氏。天未晓，广起，而照已去矣。自是竟不知所往。然照自梁普通七年生，按《梁史》，普通七年，岁在丙午，至唐元和十年乙未，凡二百九十年，则与照言果符矣。愚常以梁、陈二史，校其所说，颇有同者，由是益信其不诬矣。

    Explanation

    39

    At the beginning of the Dahe era, Guang served as an assistant official in Bashu, and on the Shu Road he suddenly met Zao. Overjoyed, he bowed twice and said, "I wish to resign from my post and follow you, my teacher, on a journey beyond the mundane world." Zao agreed. That night, they both stayed at an inn. Before dawn, Guang awoke, but Zao had already departed. From then on, Guang never knew where Zao had gone. However, Zao was born in the seventh year of the Pingtong era of the Liang dynasty. According to the Liang Shi, that year was the year of Bing Wu, and from that time until the tenth year of the Yuanhe era of the Tang dynasty, the year of Yi Wei, a total of 290 years passed—thus confirming Zao’s words as accurate. I have often compared the Liang Shi and Chen Shi, and found many similarities in their accounts, and so I have come to believe even more firmly that this is not a falsehood.

    40

    ○唐休璟门僧
    　　唐中宗时，唐公休璟为相。尝有一僧，发言多中，好为厌胜之术。休璟甚敬之。一日僧来，谓休璟曰：“相国将有大祸，且不远数月，然可以禳去。”休璟惧甚，即拜之。僧曰；“某无他术，但奉一计耳，愿听之。”休璟曰：“幸吾师教焉。”僧曰：“且天下郡守，非相国命之乎？”曰：“然。”僧曰：“相国当于卑冗官中访一孤寒家贫有才干者，使为曹州刺史，其深感相国恩，而可以指踪也。既得之，原以报某。”休璟且喜且谢，遂访于亲友。张君者，家甚贫，为京卑官，即日拜替善大夫。又旬日，用为曹州刺史。既而召僧谓曰：“已従师之计，得张某矣。然则可教乎？”僧曰：“张君赴郡之时，当令求二犬，高数尺而神俊者。”休璟唯之。已而张君荷唐公特达之恩，然莫喻其旨，及将赴郡，告辞于休璟。既而谢之曰：“某名迹幽昧，才识疏浅。

    Explanation

    40

    During the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang, Tang Xiujing served as Premier. There was once a monk who often spoke accurately and was skilled in charms and spells. Tang Xiujing held him in high regard. One day, the monk came and said to Tang Xiujing, "The Premier will face a great disaster within a few months, but it can be averted." Tang Xiujing was greatly alarmed and immediately bowed to him. The monk said, "I have no other skills, but I offer one plan, which I hope you will consider." Tang Xiujing replied, "I am fortunate to have my teacher's guidance." The monk asked, "Isn't it true that the Premier has the authority to appoint the local governors?" Tang Xiujing answered, "Yes." The monk then said, "The Premier should seek out a lonely, poor, and humble man of talent among the low-ranking officials and appoint him as the Governor of Cao Prefecture. This man will deeply appreciate the Premier's favor and can be the cause of the disaster. Once found, he should be willing to repay me." Tang Xiujing was both pleased and grateful, and so he sought out such a person among his friends and relatives. Zhang Jun, a man from a very poor family and a low-ranking official in the capital, was immediately promoted to the title of "a good official." Ten days later, he was appointed as the Governor of Cao Prefecture. Later, Tang Xiujing summoned the monk and said, "I have followed your plan and found Zhang Jun. Can I now instruct him?" The monk replied, "When Zhang Jun goes to his new post, he should be instructed to find two large and spirited dogs." Tang Xiujing agreed. Zhang Jun, grateful for Tang Xiujing's special favor, did not understand the purpose, and when he was about to leave for his new post, he bid farewell to Tang Xiujing. He then said, "My reputation is obscure, and my talent and knowledge are limited."

    41

    相国拔此沈滞，牧守大郡，由担石之储，获二千石之禄，自涸辍而泛东溟，出穷谷而陟层霄，德固厚矣。然而感恩之外，窃所忧惕者，未知相国之旨何哉？”休璟曰：“用君之才耳，非他也。然常闻贵郡多善犬，愿得神俊非常者二焉。”张君曰：“谨奉教。”既至郡数日，乃悉召郡吏，告之曰：“吾受丞相唐公深恩，拔于不次，得守大郡。今唐公求二良犬，可致之乎”。有一吏前曰：“某家育一犬，质状异常，愿献之。”张大喜，即献焉。既至，其犬高数尺而肥，其臆广尺余，神俊异常，而又驯扰。张君曰：“相国所求者二也，如何？”吏白曰：“郡内唯有此，他皆常也。然郡南十里某村某民家，其亦有一焉。民极惜之，非君侯亲往，不可取之。”张君即命驾。赍厚直而访之，果得焉。其状与吏所献者无异，而神彩过之。张君甚喜，即召亲吏以二犬献休璟。休璟大悦，且奇其状，以为未常见。遂召僧视之。僧曰：“善育之。脱相君之祸者，二犬耳。”主

    Explanation

    41

    The Chancellor has promoted me, who was previously overlooked, and appointed me as the governor of a major region. From a humble savings of a few stones, I have received a salary equivalent to 2000 stones, a high official rank. I have ventured from a dry pond into the East Sea, and ascended from the depths of a valley to the heights of the sky. The kindness is indeed profound. However, beyond gratitude, I privately feel concern and fear, unsure of the Chancellor's intentions. Chancellor Xiu Jing said, "It is solely due to your talent, nothing else. Yet, I often hear that your county has many fine dogs. I wish to obtain two exceptionally fine ones." Zhang Jun replied, "I will follow your instructions." After arriving in the county for several days, he summoned all the county officials and told them, "I have received deep favor from Chancellor Tang Gong, who promoted me beyond my station, allowing me to govern a major region. Now, Chancellor Tang Gong seeks two fine dogs. Can they be obtained?" One official stepped forward and said, "My family has raised a dog with an unusual appearance. I am willing to offer it." Zhang Jun was delighted and immediately accepted the offer. When the dog arrived, it was several feet tall and well-fed, with a broad chest over a foot wide, and an exceptionally fine and spirited demeanor, yet it was also well-behaved. Zhang Jun said, "The Chancellor seeks two, how can this be?" The official replied, "There is only this one in the county, the others are ordinary. However, in a village ten miles south of the county, there is another one. The owner is very fond of it and will not part with it unless you personally go to fetch it." Zhang Jun immediately prepared to travel. He carried a generous gift to visit the village and indeed obtained the dog. The appearance of this dog was the same as the one offered by the official, but its spirit and beauty surpassed the first. Zhang Jun was very pleased and summoned the officials to present the two dogs to Chancellor Xiu Jing. Chancellor Xiu Jing was greatly pleased and found the dogs to be extraordinary, something he had never seen before. He then summoned a monk to examine the dogs. The monk said, "Take good care of them. These two dogs will save you from disaster."

    42

    后旬日，其僧又至，谓休璟曰：“事在今夕，愿相君严为之备。”休璟即留僧宿。是夜，休璟坐于堂之前轩，命左右十余人执弧矢立于榻之隅，其僧与休璟共处一榻。至夜分，僧笑曰：“相君之祸免矣。可以就寝。”休璟大喜，且谢之。遂彻左右，与僧寝焉。迨晓，僧呼休璟：“可起矣。”休璟即起，谓僧曰：“祸诚免矣，然二犬安所用乎？”僧曰：“俱往观焉。”乃与休璟偕寻其迹，至后园中，见一人仆地而卒矣，视其颈有血，盖为物所噬者。

    Explanation

    42

    After ten days, the monk returned, telling Xiu Jing, "The matter will occur tonight. I hope you will take serious measures." Xiu Jing immediately invited the monk to stay overnight.
