That night, Xiu Jing sat in the front hall, ordering more than ten people to hold bows and arrows and stand at the corner of the bed. The monk and Xiu Jing shared a bed. By midnight, the monk smiled and said, "Your disaster is truly averted. You may go to sleep." Xiu Jing was greatly relieved and thanked him. He then dismissed the attendants and went to sleep with the monk. Until dawn, the monk called Xiu Jing, "You may get up now." Xiu Jing got up and said to the monk, "The disaster is truly averted, but what use are the two dogs?" The monk said, "Let's both go and see." They went together to investigate the scene and found a person lying on the ground, dead. Upon closer inspection, they saw blood on the person's neck, indicating that he had been bitten by something.

    43

    又见二犬在大木下，仰视之，见一人袒而匿其上。休璟惊，且诘曰：“汝为谁？”其人泣而指死者曰：“某与彼俱贼也，昨夕偕来，且将致害相国。盖遇此二犬，环而且吠，彼遂为噬而死。某惧，因匿身于此，伺其他去，将逃焉。迨晓终不去。今即甘死于是矣。”休璟即召左右，令缚之。曰：“此罪固当死，然非其心也，盖受制于人耳。愿释之。”休璟命解缚，其贼拜泣而去。休璟谢其僧曰：“赖吾师，不然，死于二人之手。”僧曰：“此盖相国之福也，岂所能为哉。”主
    　　休璟有表弟卢轸，在荆门，有术士告之：“君将有灾戾，当求一善禳厌者为，庶可矣。”轸素知其僧，因致书于休璟，请求之。僧即以书付休璟曰：“事在其中耳。”及书达荆州，而轸已卒。其家开视其书，徒见一幅之纸，并无有文字焉。休璟益奇之。后数年，其僧遁去，竟不知其所适

    Explanation

    43

    Xiu Jing saw two dogs under a large tree and looked up, seeing a man baring his body and hiding on top of the tree. Xiu Jing was surprised and interrogated the man, asking, "Who are you?" The man cried and pointed to the dead body, saying, "I and that person were both thieves. We came together last night with the intention to harm the chancellor. We encountered these two dogs, which barked around us, and he was bitten to death. I was afraid, so I hid here, waiting for him to leave so I could escape. By morning, he had not left. Now I am willing to die here." Xiu Jing immediately called his attendants to bind the man. He said, "This crime deserves death, but it was not his will; he was forced by others. I wish to release him." Xiu Jing ordered the man to be unbound, and the thief bowed and cried as he left. Xiu Jing thanked the monk, saying, "Thanks to my teacher, otherwise, I would have died at the hands of these two people." The monk replied, "This is the chancellor's good fortune; it was not my doing."

    Xiu Jing had a cousin named Lu Zhen in Jingmen. A diviner told him, "You will have misfortune; you should seek someone skilled in warding off evil to help you, perhaps it will work." Lu Zhen knew the monk and wrote a letter to Xiu Jing, requesting his help. The monk gave the letter to Xiu Jing, saying, "The matter is in the letter." When the letter reached Jingmen, Lu Zhen had already died. His family opened the letter and found only a sheet of paper with no writing on it. Xiu Jing was even more amazed. Several years later, the monk disappeared, and no one knew where he went.

    44

    ○韦皋
    　　唐故剑南节度使太尉兼中书令韦皋，既生一月，其家召群僧会食。有一胡僧，貌甚陋，不召而至。韦氏家童咸怒之，以弊席坐于庭中。既食，韦氏命乳母出婴儿，请群僧祝其寿。胡僧所自升阶，谓婴儿曰，“别久无恙乎？”婴儿若有喜色。众皆异之。韦氏先君曰：“此子生才一月，吾师何故言别久耶！”胡僧曰：“此非檀越之所知也。”韦氏固问之，胡僧曰：“此子乃诸葛武侯之后身耳。武侯当东汉之季，为蜀丞相，蜀人受其赐且久。今降生于世，将为蜀门帅，且受蜀人之福。吾往岁在剑门，与此子友善。今闻降于韦氏，吾固不远而来。”韦氏异其言，因以武侯字之。后韦氏自少金吾节制剑南军，累迁太尉兼中书令，在蜀十八年，果契胡僧之语也

    Explanation

    44

    ○ Wei Gao
    The late Tang general and administrator Wei Gao, former military governor of Jiannan, Taivie, and concurrently Zhongshu Ling, was born one month after his family summoned a group of monks to dine together. Among them was a foreign monk, who appeared quite unattractive, yet arrived uninvited. The servants of the Wei family were all angry at him and made him sit on a tattered mat in the courtyard. After the meal, Wei ordered the nursemaid to bring out the infant and asked the monks to bless him for longevity. The foreign monk ascended the steps on his own and said to the infant, “Have you been well since we last met?” The infant seemed to show a look of joy. The gathered monks were all amazed. Wei’s father said, “This child is only one month old. Why does my teacher speak of being apart for so long?” The monk replied, “This is not something you, the benefactor, can know.” Wei pressed him further, and the monk said, “This child is the reincarnation of Zhuge Liang, the Lord of the Southern Gate. Zhuge Liang, during the later years of the Eastern Han, served as the chancellor of Shu, and the people of Shu have long benefited from his deeds. Now he has been reborn into this world and will become the commander of Shu’s western gate, receiving blessings from the people of Shu. Last year I was at Jianmen Pass, where I was friends with this child. Now I have heard that he has been born into the Wei family, so I have traveled far to come.” Wei found the monk’s words astonishing and thus gave the child the courtesy name of Zhuge Liang. Later, when Wei was still young, he was appointed to command the Jiannan army under the Jinwu guard, was repeatedly promoted to Taivie and Zhongshu Ling, and spent eighteen years in Shu, ultimately fulfilling the monk’s words.

    45

    ○辛七师
    　　辛七师，陕人，辛其姓也。始为儿时，甚谨肃，未尝以狎弄为事，其父母异而怜之。十岁好浮图氏法，日阅佛书，自能辨梵音，不由师教。其后父为陕郡守。先是，郡南有瓦窑七所，及父卒，辛七哀毁甚，一日发狂遁去。其家僮迹其所往，至郡南，见辛七在一瓦窑中端坐，身有奇光，粲然若炼金色。家僮惊异。次至一窑，又见一辛七在焉。历七窑，俱有一辛七在中。由是呼为“辛七师”

    Explanation

    45

    Xīn Qī Shī, a man from Shaanxi whose surname is Xīn, was very cautious and solemn when he was a child, never engaging in playful activities. His parents found him unusual and pitied him. At the age of ten, he became fond of Buddhist teachings, read Buddhist scriptures daily, and could naturally distinguish Sanskrit sounds without teacher's instruction. Later, his father became the governor of Shaanxi. Previously, there were seven kilns in the south of the county. After his father's death, Xīn Qī suffered greatly from grief and went mad and fled one day. His family servants followed his path and found him sitting calmly in one of the kilns in the south of the county, with a strange light shining on him, bright like refined gold. They were astonished. Visiting another kiln, they found another Xīn Qī there. Visiting all seven kilns, they found one Xīn Qī in each. Thus he was called 'Xīn Qī Shī.'

    46

    ○广陵大师
    　　唐贞元中，有一僧客于广陵，亡其名，自号大师，广陵人因以“大师”呼之。大师质甚陋，好以酒肉为食。日衣弊袭，盛暑不脱，由是蚤虮聚其上。侨居孝感寺，独止一室。每夕阖扉而寝，率为常矣。性狂悖，好屠犬彘，日与广陵少年斗殴，或醉卧道傍。广陵人俱以此恶之。有一少年以力闻，常一日，少年与人对博，大师怒，以手击其博局，尽碎。少年曰：騃儿，何敢逆壮士耶！”大师且骂而唾其面，于是与少年斗击，而观者千数，少年卒不胜，竟遁去。自是，广陵人谓大师有神力。大师亦自负其力，往往剽夺市中金钱衣物，市人皆惮其勇，莫敢拒。

    Explanation

    46

    A Master of Yangzhou

    During the reign of Emperor Dezong in the Tang Dynasty, there was a monk who stayed in Yangzhou. His name is unknown, but he called himself "Master," and the people of Yangzhou thus referred to him as "Master." The Master was physically unattractive and had a fondness for alcohol and meat. He wore tattered clothes and did not change them even in the heat of summer, which led to lice and fleas gathering on his garments. He lived temporarily at Xinggan Temple, residing alone in a room. Every night, he would close the door and sleep, which became his routine. He had a wild and rebellious nature, enjoyed slaughtering dogs and pigs, and daily fought with young men from Yangzhou, sometimes lying drunk on the roadside. The people of Yangzhou all disliked him for these reasons. One young man was known for his strength. On a particular day, the young man was playing a game of chance with someone, and the Master became angry, breaking the gaming board with his hand. The young man called the Master a fool and challenged his courage. The Master then cursed and spat in the young man's face. The two fought, and a large crowd gathered to watch. The young man eventually lost and fled. From then on, the people of Yangzhou believed the Master had supernatural strength. The Master also took pride in his strength, frequently stealing money and clothes from the market. The people of the market were all afraid of his courage and dared not resist.

    47

    后有老僧召大师，至，曰：“僧当死心奉戒，奈何食酒食，杀大彘，剽夺市人钱物，又与少年同殴击，岂僧人之道耶一旦吏执以闻官，汝不羞人耶！”大师怒骂曰：“蝇蚋徒嗜膻腥耳，安能知龙鹤之心哉然则吾道亦非汝所知也。且我清其中而混其外者，岂汝龊龊无大度乎？”老僧卒不能屈其词。后一日，大师自外来归，既入室，闭户。有于门隙视者，大师坐于席，有奇光自眉端发，晃然照一室。观者奇之，具告群僧。群僧来，见大师眉端之光，相指语曰：“吾闻佛之眉有白毫相光，今大师有之，果佛矣。”遂相率而拜。至明日清旦，群僧俱集于庭，候谒广陵大师，比及开户，而广陵大师已亡去矣。群僧益异其事，因号大师为“大师佛”焉。知

    Explanation

    47

    Later, an old monk summoned the Master. Upon arriving, the monk said, "Monks should wholeheartedly adhere to the precepts. How can you consume alcohol, kill pigs, and steal money and goods from market people? And how can you also fight with young men? Is this the way of a monk? If officials arrest you and report to the authorities, are you not ashamed?" The Master angrily retorted, "Flies and mosquitoes only crave stench and filth; how can they know the heart of dragons and cranes? Then my path is also not something you can understand. Moreover, I am clear within but mixed without; are you petty and lacking in great virtue?" The old monk could not ultimately convince him with his words.

    The next day, the Master returned from outside. After entering the room, he closed the door. Someone was peeping through the crack in the door. The Master sat on the mat, and a strange light emanated from his brow, illuminating the entire room brightly. The observer found it strange and fully informed the group of monks. The group of monks came, and saw the light from the Master's brow. They pointed at each other and said, "I have heard that the Buddha's brow has the white hair-like light. Now the Master has it; he is truly a Buddha." They then proceeded to worship him.

    By the next morning at dawn, the group of monks all gathered in the courtyard, waiting to pay homage to the Master of Guangling. By the time they opened the door, the Master of Guangling had already passed away. The monks were even more amazed by this event and thus began to call the Master "Master Buddha."

    48

    ○鉴师
    　　唐元和初，有长乐冯生者，家于吴，以明经调选于天官氏，是岁，见黜于有司，因侨居长安中。有老僧鉴其名者，一日来诣生，谓生：“汝，吾姓也。”因相与往来，近岁余。及冯尉于东越，既治装，鉴师负笈来，告去。冯问曰：“师去安所诣乎？”鉴师曰：“我庐于灵岩寺之西庑下且久，其后游长安中，至今十年矣，幸得与子相遇。今将归故居，故来告别。然吾子尉于东越，道出灵岩寺下，当宜一访我也。”生诺曰：“谨受教。

    Explanation

    48

    In the early years of the Yuanhe era of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Feng Sheng from Changle who lived in Wu. He was selected for an official position through the Ministry of Personnel based on his knowledge of the classics, but in that year, he was dismissed by the officials. Consequently, he temporarily resided in Chang'an. One day, an old monk who recognized his name came to visit Feng Sheng and said to him, "You bear my surname." From then on, they met frequently for nearly a year. When Feng Sheng was appointed as an official in East Yue, he began to prepare for his journey, and the monk came with his belongings to say goodbye. Feng Sheng asked, "Where are you going, Master?" The monk replied, "I have been living in the west wing of Lingyan Temple for a long time. Afterward, I traveled around Chang'an, and it has been ten years since then. It is fortunate to have met you. Now I am returning to my hometown, so I come to bid farewell. However, since you are being appointed as an official in East Yue, and your route will pass by Lingyan Temple, you should visit me once." Feng Sheng agreed, saying, "I will follow your advice."

    49

    ”后数月，冯生自长安之任，至灵岩寺门，立马望曰：“岂非鉴师所居寺乎？”即入而诣焉。时有一僧在庭，生问曰：“不知鉴师庐安在吾将诣之。”僧曰：“吾曹数辈，独无鉴其名者。”生始疑异，默而计曰：“鉴师信士，岂欺我耶！”于是独游寺庭，行至西庑下，忽见有群僧画像，其一人，状同鉴师，生大惊曰：“鉴师果异人也，且能神降于我。”因慨然泣下者久之。视其题曰：“冯氏子，吴郡人也。年十岁学浮图法，以道行闻。卒年七十八。”冯阅其题，益异之

    Explanation

    49

    After several months, Feng, having taken up his post from Chang'an, arrived at the gate of Lingyan Temple. He stood his horse and looked, saying, "Is this not the temple where Master Jian resides?" He then entered and went to visit. At the time, there was a monk in the courtyard. Feng asked, "Do you not know where Master Jian's abode is? I am about to go there." The monk replied, "Among us, none of us have heard of someone named Jian." Feng began to doubt and feel strange, silently thinking and saying, "Master Jian is a trustworthy person; could he be deceiving me?" He then walked alone in the temple courtyard, reaching the western wing, when suddenly he saw a group of monks' portraits. One of them looked like Master Jian. Feng exclaimed in great surprise, "Master Jian is truly a strange person, and even a spirit descended upon me." He cried for a long time in a moved manner. Looking at the inscription, it read, "A son of the Feng family, from Wu County. At ten years old, he learned the Buddha's teachings, becoming known for his virtuous conduct. He died at the age of seventy-eight." Feng, reading the inscription, found it even more strange.

    50

    ○李德裕
    　　唐相国李德裕为太子少保，分司东都。尝召一僧问己之休咎，僧曰：“非立可知，愿结坛设佛像。”僧居其中，凡三日。谓公曰：“公灾戾未已，当万里南去耳。”公大怒，叱之。明日，又召其僧问焉。”虑所见未子细，请更观之。”即又结坛三日，告公曰：“南行之期，不旬月矣。不可逃。”公益不乐，且曰：“然则吾师何以明其不妄耶！”僧曰：“愿陈目前事为验，庶表某之不诬也。”公曰：“果有说也”即指其地曰：“此下有石函，请发之。”即命穷其下数尺，果得石函，启之，亦无睹焉，公异而稍信之。因问：“南去诚不免矣，然乃遂不还乎？”僧曰：“当还耳。

    Explanation

    50

    Li Deyu, the Tang Dynasty Chancellor, was appointed as the Junior Advisor to the Crown Prince and stationed in the Eastern Capital (Luoyang). He once summoned a monk to inquire about his future fortune. The monk said, "It is not immediately knowable, I wish to set up an altar and place a Buddha statue." The monk stayed in the temple for three days. Then he told Li Deyu, "Your misfortunes have not ceased, you will be exiled to the south." Li Deyu became very angry and scolded the monk. The next day, he summoned the monk again and asked him. The monk said, "I wish to observe more carefully, please allow me to set up the altar for another three days." After three more days, the monk told Li Deyu, "The time of going south will not be far off, it cannot be avoided." Li Deyu was even less happy and said, "Then how can your master prove that he is not lying?" The monk said, "I wish to prove it with what is happening now, to show that I am not lying." Li Deyu said, "If you really have something to say, please go ahead." The monk pointed to the ground and said, "There is a stone box under here, please dig it up." Li Deyu ordered his people to dig down several feet, and indeed, they found a stone box. When they opened it, there was nothing inside. Li Deyu was surprised and began to believe the monk. He then asked, "Going south is indeed unavoidable, but will I never return?" The monk said, "You will return."

    51

    ”公讯其事，对曰：“相国平生当食万羊，今食九千五百矣。所以当还者，未尽五百羊耳。”公惨然而叹曰：“吾师果至人。且我元和十三年为巫相张公従事，于北都，尝梦行于晋山，见山上尽目皆羊，有牧者十数迎拜我。我因问牧者，牧者曰：‘此侍御平生所食羊。’吾尝记此梦，不泄于人。今者果如师之说耶。乃知阴骘固不诬也。”后旬日，振武节度使米暨遣使致书于公，且馈五百羊。公大惊，召告其事。僧叹曰：“万羊将满，公其不还乎？”公曰：“吾不食之，亦可免耶！”曰：“羊至此，已为相国所有。”公戚然。旬日，贬潮州司马，连贬崖州司户，竟没于荒裔也

    Explanation

    51

    The official inquired about the matter, and the monk replied, "The chancellor's lifetime allotment of sheep was ten thousand, and he has now consumed nine thousand five hundred. The reason for his return is that there are still five hundred sheep remaining." The official sighed sadly and said, "My teacher is indeed a sage. Moreover, during the thirteenth year of the Yuanhe era, I served as an assistant to Chancellor Zhang Gong in the Northern Capital. Once, I dreamed of walking on Jishan Mountain and saw that the entire mountain was filled with sheep. There were several dozen shepherds who welcomed and bowed to me. I then asked the shepherds, and they said, 'These are the sheep that the chancellor has consumed in his lifetime.' I once remembered this dream and never revealed it to anyone. Now, is it true as my teacher said? I now understand that hidden virtue is indeed not false." A few days later, the military governor of Zhenwu, Mi Ji, sent an envoy to deliver a letter to the official and also presented him with five hundred sheep. The official was greatly surprised and summoned the monk to inform him of the matter. The monk sighed and said, "With ten thousand sheep approaching, will the official not return?" The official said, "If I do not consume them, can I still avoid the consequences?" The monk replied, "The sheep are now the property of the chancellor." The official was deeply saddened. A few days later, he was demoted to the position of Military Governor of Chaozhou, and was subsequently demoted to the position of Household Registrar of Yazhou, eventually dying in a remote and desolate place.

    52

    ○抱玉师
    　　抱五师以道行闻，居长安中，师而事者千数。每夕独处一室，阖户撤烛。尝有僧于门隙视之，见有庆云自口中出。后年九十卒，时方大暑，而其尸无萎败。唐宰相第五琦与师善，及卒，来治丧，将以香乳灌其口，已而有祥光自口出，晃然四照。公甚奇之。或曰：“佛有庆祥光，今抱玉师有之，真佛矣。
    ○佛陀萨
    　　有佛陀萨者，其籍编于岐阳法门寺，自言姓佛氏，陀萨其名也。常独行岐陇间，衣黄持锡。年虽老，然其貌类者童騃。好扬言于衢中，或诟辱群僧，僧皆怒焉。其资膳裘纻，俱乞于里人。里人怜其愚，厚与衣食，以故资用独饶于群僧。陀萨亦转均于里中穷饿者焉。里人益怜其心。开成五年夏六月，陀萨召里中民告曰：“我今夕死矣。汝为吾塔瘗其尸。”果而卒。于是里中之人建塔于岐阳之西冈上。漆其尸而瘗焉。后月余，或视其首，发仅寸余，弟子即剃去。已而又生。里人大异，遂扃其户，竟不开焉

    Explanation

    52

    抱玉师

    The Master Embracing Jade was renowned for his spiritual practice, residing in Chang'an. Thousands followed him as their teacher. Each evening, he would sit alone in a room, closing the door and extinguishing the candle. Once, a monk peered through a crack in the door and saw auspicious clouds emerging from his mouth. He lived to be ninety years old and passed away during a hot summer. His body did not decay. The Tang Chancellor Wang Qi, who was on good terms with the Master, came to attend his funeral. They intended to pour fragrant milk into his mouth as a ritual, but then a brilliant light emerged from his mouth, illuminating the surroundings. The Chancellor was greatly amazed. Some said, "The Buddha emits auspicious light, and now the Master Embracing Jade has it—truly a Buddha."

    佛陀萨

    There was a monk named Buddha Sa, whose name was listed in the records of the Temple of the Dharma Gate in Qishan. He claimed to be of the Buddha clan, with "Buddha" as his surname and "Sa" as his given name. He often walked alone through the regions of Qishan, wearing yellow robes and carrying a staff. Although he was old, his appearance was childlike and innocent. He enjoyed speaking loudly in the marketplace, sometimes insulting and humiliating the monks, who all grew angry at him. His food, clothing, and other necessities were all obtained by begging from the villagers. The villagers pitied his foolishness and generously provided him with food and clothing, so that his resources were more abundant than those of the other monks. Buddha Sa also shared his resources with the poor and hungry in the village. The villagers came to admire his heart even more. In the sixth month of the fifth year of the Kai Cheng era, Buddha Sa summoned the villagers and said, "I will die tonight. You must build a stupa to bury my body." He passed away as he had said. The villagers then built a stupa on the western hill of Qishan, lacquered his body, and buried it. After a month, someone noticed that his hair was only a few inches long, so the disciples shaved it off. Soon after, his hair began to grow again. The villagers were greatly astonished and locked the door of the stupa, never opening it again.

    53

    ○赵蕃
    　　唐国子祭酒赵蕃，大和七年为南宫郎。忽一日，有僧乞食于门，且谓其家僮曰：“吾愿见赵公，可乎？”家僮告蕃，善即命延入与坐，僧乃曰：“君将有忧。然亦可禳去。”蕃即拜而祈之。僧曰：“遗我裁刀一千五百，庶可脱君之祸，不然，未旬日，当为东南一郡耳。”蕃许之，约来日就送焉，且访其名暨所居。僧曰：“吾居青龙寺，法安其名也。”言已遂去。明日，蕃即办送之。使者至寺，以物色访群僧，僧皆不类，且询法安师所止，周遍院宇，无影响踪迹。后数日，蕃出为袁州刺史

    Explanation

    53

    ○ Zhao Fan

    Zhao Fan, the highest-ranking official of the Imperial Academy during the Tang Dynasty, served as an official of the Ministry of Personnel in the seventh year of the Dàhé era. One day, a monk came to the door to beg for food and told the household servant, "I wish to see Zhao Gong, may I?" The servant informed Zhao Fan, who, upon hearing this, invited the monk in and had him sit down. The monk then said, "You are about to have worries, but it can also be averted." Zhao Fan immediately bowed and prayed to him. The monk replied, "Give me 1,500 knives, and perhaps it can save you from disaster; otherwise, within ten days, you will become the governor of a southeastern prefecture." Zhao Fan agreed and promised to send the knives the next day, while also asking for the monk's name and where he lived. The monk said, "I live at Qinglong Temple, and my name is Fayan." After saying this, the monk left. The next day, Zhao Fan prepared to send the knives. The messenger went to the temple and searched for the monk among the monks, but none of them looked like him. They also inquired about the whereabouts of Master Fayan, searched the entire temple grounds but found no trace or sign. Several days later, Zhao Fan was sent to serve as the governor of Yuzhou.

    54

    ○十光佛
    　　兴福寺西北隅有隋朝佛堂，其壁有画十光佛者，笔势甚妙，为天下之标冠。有识者云，此国手蔡生之迹也。蔡生，隋朝以善画闻。初建堂宇既成，有僧以百金募善画者，得蔡生。既画，谓人曰：“吾平生所画多矣。独今日下笔，若有鬼神翼而成者。”由是长安中尽传其名。贞观初，寺僧以此堂年月稍久，虑一旦有摧圮，遂召数工及土木之费，且欲新其制。忽一日，群僧斋于寺庭，既坐，有僧十人，俱白皙清瘦，貌甚古，相次而来，列于席。食毕偕起，入佛堂中，群僧亦继其后。俄而十人忽亡所见。群僧相顾惊叹者久之。因视北壁十光佛，见其风度与向者十人果同。自是，僧不敢毁其堂，且用旌十光之易也

    Explanation

    54

    In the northwest corner of Xingfu Temple, there is a Buddhist hall from the Sui Dynasty. On its walls, there is a painting of the Ten-Light Buddhas, whose brushwork is extremely skillful and stands as the pinnacle of its kind. An informed person said that this is the work of the national master painter, Cai Sheng. Cai Sheng was renowned in the Sui Dynasty for his exceptional painting skills. When the hall was first built and completed, a monk offered a hundred gold pieces to solicit skilled painters, and Cai Sheng was hired. After completing the painting, he told others, "I have painted many things in my lifetime. But today, as I set brush to paper, it feels as if I were guided by divine assistance." Because of this, his name spread throughout Chang'an. In the early years of the Zhenguan era, the temple monks, concerned about the hall's age and potential collapse, hired several craftsmen and spent money on construction, intending to renovate its structure. One day, the monks held a meal in the temple courtyard. After they sat down, ten monks arrived one after another, all pale and slender, with ancient-looking appearances, and arranged themselves at the table. After finishing their meal, they all rose together and entered the Buddhist hall, followed by the other monks. Soon, the ten monks vanished without a trace. The monks looked at each other and marveled for a long time. Upon examining the Ten-Light Buddhas painted on the north wall, they found that their demeanor matched that of the ten monks who had arrived earlier. From then on, the monks did not dare to destroy the hall, and they used it to honor the Ten-Light Buddhas.

    55

    ○道严
    　　有严师者，居于成都实历寺。唐开元十四年五月二十一日，于佛殿前轩燃长明灯，忽见一巨手在殿西轩，道严悸且甚，俯而不动。久之，忽闻空中语云：“无惧，无惧。吾善神也。且不敢害师之一毫，何俯而不动耶！”道严既闻，惧少解。因问曰：“檀越为何人匿其躯而见其手乎？”已而闻空中对曰：“天命我护佛寺之地，以世人好唾佛祠地，我即以背接之，受其唾。由是背有疮，渍吾肌且甚。愿以膏油傅其上，可乎？”道严遂以清油置巨手中，其手即引去。道严乃请曰：“吾今愿见檀越之形，使画工写于屋壁，且书其事以表之，冀世人无敢唾佛词之地者。”神曰：“吾貌甚陋，师见之，无得慓然耶！”道严曰：“檀越但见其身，勿我阻也。”见西轩下有一神，质甚异，丰首巨准，严目呀口，体状魁硕，长数丈。道严一见，背汗如沃。其神即隐去。于是具以神状告画工，命图于西轩之壁

    Explanation

    55

    Master Dao Yan, residing at the Shili Temple in Chengdu, experienced a remarkable event on the 21st day of the fifth month in the 14th year of the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty (726 AD). While lighting the eternal lamp in front of the Buddha hall, he suddenly saw a giant hand in the west pavilion of the hall. Dao Yan was greatly frightened and bowed down without moving. After a while, he suddenly heard a voice from the air saying, "Do not be afraid, do not be afraid. I am a good spirit. I dare not harm even the slightest part of the teacher. Why bow down and not move?" Dao Yan, after hearing this, was slightly relieved of his fear. He then asked, "Master, who are you, hiding your body and showing only your hand?" Afterwards, he heard the voice from the air reply, "Heaven has appointed me to protect the land of the Buddha temple because people like to spit on the ground of the Buddha shrine. I immediately receive it with my back, taking the spittle. Therefore, my back has sores, and the sores are very deep. May I apply ointment on it, is it acceptable?" Dao Yan then placed clear oil in the giant hand, and the hand withdrew. Dao Yan then requested, "I now wish to see your form. To have a painter depict it on the wall and write the story to record it, hoping that people will not dare to spit on the ground of the Buddha shrine." The spirit said, "My appearance is very ugly. Teacher, when you see it, do not be startled." Dao Yan replied, "Master, just see my body, do not hinder me." Under the west pavilion, he saw a spirit with an appearance very strange, broad head and large nose, sharp eyes and wide mouth, a tall and robust body shape, several zhang long. Upon seeing it, his back sweat like a flood. The spirit then disappeared. Dao Yan then described the spirit's appearance to the painter and ordered him to depict it on the wall of the west pavilion.

    56

    ○鸡卵
    　　唐敬宗皇帝御历，以天下无事，视政之余，因广浮屠教。由是，长安中缁徒益多。及文宗嗣位，亲阅万机，思除其害于人者，尝顾左右曰：“自吾为天子，未能有补于人。今天下幸无兵革，吾将尽除害物者，使亿兆之民，指今日为尧舜之世，足矣。有不能补化而蠹于物者，但言之。”左右或对曰：“独浮屠氏不能有补于大化，而蠹于物亦甚，可以斥去。”于是文宗病之，始命有司诏中外，罢缁徒说佛书义。又有请斥其不修教者。诏命将行，会尚食厨吏修御膳，以鼎烹鸡卵，方燃火于其下，忽闻鼎中有声极微，如人言者。迫而听之，乃群卵呼“观世音菩萨”也。声甚凄咽，似有所诉。尚食吏异之，具其事上闻。文宗命左右验之，如尚食所奏。文帝叹曰：“吾不知浮屠氏之力乃如是耶！”翌日，敕尚食吏无以鸡卵为膳。因颁诏郡国，各于精舍塑观世音菩萨像

    Explanation

    56

    ○ Chicken Eggs

    Emperor Jingzong of the Tang Dynasty ruled the empire. With the realm at peace, during his leisure from state affairs, he extended the practice of Buddhism. As a result, the number of Buddhist monks in Chang’an increased greatly. When Emperor Wenzong ascended the throne, he personally attended to all state matters and sought to eliminate those who harmed the people. He once turned to his attendants and said, “Since I became emperor, I have not yet been able to benefit the people. Now that the realm is fortunately free from war, I shall completely remove all things that cause harm, so that the myriad millions of people may regard this day as the age of Yao and Shun. If there are those who cannot contribute to the transformation of the world and instead harm it, just speak up.” One of his attendants replied, “It is only the followers of Buddhism who cannot contribute to the great transformation of the world, and they harm it greatly. They may be expelled.” Thus, Emperor Wenzong was troubled by this, and he first ordered the relevant officials to issue a decree throughout the empire, prohibiting Buddhist monks from expounding the meanings of Buddhist scriptures. Some also requested that those who failed to observe religious teachings be expelled. The imperial decree was about to be carried out when, coincidentally, the official in charge of the emperor’s meals was preparing a banquet. He placed chicken eggs in a cauldron and was just lighting a fire beneath it when he suddenly heard a very faint sound from inside the cauldron, as if someone were speaking. He leaned closer to listen, and it turned out to be the eggs calling out, “Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva!” The sound was very sorrowful, as though they were making a plea. The official in charge of the imperial kitchen was astonished and reported the incident to the emperor. Emperor Wenzong ordered his attendants to investigate, and they confirmed what the official had reported. Emperor Wenzong sighed and said, “I did not know that the power of the Buddha could be so great!” The next day, he issued an order to the official in charge of the imperial kitchen, instructing him not to serve chicken eggs as part of the imperial meals. Consequently, he issued a decree to the provinces and commanderies, ordering that images of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara be erected in each Buddhist monastery.

    57

    ○许文度
    　　高阳许文度，唐太和中，侨居岐阳郡。后以病热近月余，一日卧于榻，若沉醉状。后数日始寤。初，文度梦有衣黄袍数辈，与俱行田野，四望间，迥然无鸡犬声，且不知几百里。其时天景曛晦，愁思如结。有黄袍者谓文度曰：“子无苦。夫寿之与夭，固有涯矣。虽圣人，安能逃其数？”文度忽悟身已死，恐甚。又行十余里，至一水，尽目无际，波若黑色，杳不知其深浅。黄衣人俱履水而去，独文度惧不敢涉。已而有二金人，皆长五寸余，奇光皎然，自水上来。黄衣者望见金人，沮色震怵，即辟易驰去，不敢偷视。

    Explanation

    57

    Xu Wendu
    During the Tahe period of the Tang Dynasty, Xu Wendu from Gaoyang was temporarily residing in Qiyang Prefecture. After suffering from a fever for nearly a month, one day he lay down on his bed, as if drunk. Several days later, he awoke. At first, Wendu dreamed of several figures dressed in yellow robes, walking with him through the fields. Looking around, there was no sound of chickens or dogs, and he could not tell how far they had traveled. The sky was dim and overcast, and his worries were as if tied up. One of the yellow-robed figures said to Wendu, "Do not be distressed. Life and death are finite. Even sages cannot escape their fate." Wendu suddenly realized that he had died, and he was very afraid. They then walked for more than ten li, reaching a body of water that seemed boundless. The waves were dark, and the depth was unknown. The yellow-robed figures all walked on the water and left, but Wendu was too afraid to cross. Soon after, two golden figures appeared, each about five inches tall, with a brilliant light shining around them, coming from the water. The yellow-robed figures saw the golden figures and turned pale with fear, retreating quickly and not daring to look back.

    58

    二金人谓文度曰：“汝何为来地府中我今挈汝归生途，慎无恐。”文度惧稍解，因再拜谢之。于是金人与文度偕行数十里，俄望见里门，喜不胜。忽闻有厉声呼文度者，文度悸而醒。见妻子方泣于前，且奇且叹，而羸惫不能运支体，故未暇语其事。后旬日，疾少间，策而步于庭，忽见二金人，皆长五寸余，在佛舍下，即昔时梦中所见者，视其仪状，无毫缕之异。心益奇之，始以其事告于妻。妻曰：“昨者以君病且亟，妾忧不解。然常闻释氏有救苦之力，由是弃资玩，铸二金人之像，每清旦，常具食祭之。自是，君之苦亦瘳除，盖其力也。”文度感二金人报效之速，不食生牢，常阅佛书，因尽穷其指归焉

    Explanation

    58

    The two golden figures said to Wendu, "Why have you come to the underworld? I will now take you back to the path of life, be careful not to be afraid." Wendu's fear gradually subsided, and he bowed twice and thanked them. The golden figures then traveled with Wendu for dozens of miles, and soon they saw the gate of the village, overjoyed. Suddenly, they heard a harsh voice calling Wendu, and he woke up in a panic. He saw his wife and children crying in front of him, finding it both strange and admirable, yet exhausted and unable to move his limbs, so he did not have time to talk about the matter. After ten days, his illness slightly improved, and he leaned on a cane and walked in the courtyard. Suddenly, he saw the two golden figures again, both over five inches tall, at the Buddhist temple, the same ones he had seen in his dream before, and their appearance was not different in the slightest. Wendu found it even more extraordinary, and began telling his wife about the matter. His wife said, "Yesterday, because your illness was severe, I was worried and unable to resolve my anxiety. However, I often heard that Buddhism has the power to relieve suffering, so I gave up wealth and possessions, cast images of the two golden figures, and every morning, prepared food and offered it as a sacrifice. Since then, your suffering also disappeared, it was their power." Wendu was grateful for the quick reward of the two golden figures, abstained from eating meat, often read Buddhist scriptures, and thus thoroughly explored their ultimate meaning.

    59

    ○商居士
    　　有商居士者，三河县人。年七岁，能通佛氏书。里人异之。后庐于三河县西田中，有佛书数百编，手卷目阅，未尝废一日。従而师者百辈。往往独游城邑，偕其行者，闻居士每运支体，垅然若戛玉之音，听者奇之。或曰：“居士之骨，真锁骨也。夫锁骨运络如蔓，故动摇之，体则有清越之声，固其然矣。昔闻佛氏书言，佛身有舍利骨，菩萨之身有锁骨。今商居士者，岂非菩萨乎然荤俗之人，固不可辨也。”居士后年九十余，一日，汤沐，具冠带，悉召门弟子会食，因告之曰：“吾年九十矣，今旦暮且死，汝当以火烬吾尸。慎无逆吾旨。”门弟子泣曰：“谨听命。”是夕，坐而卒。后三日，门弟子焚居士于野。及视其骨，果锁骨也。支体连贯，若纫缀之状，风一拂，则纤韵徐引。于是里人竟施金钱，建一塔，以居士锁骨瘗于塔中

    Explanation

    59

    Mr. Shang, the Recluse

    There was a recluse named Mr. Shang from Sanhe County. At the age of seven, he was able to understand Buddhist scriptures, which amazed the villagers. Later, he lived in a hut in the fields west of Sanhe County, where he had hundreds of Buddhist texts. He would read and review these books every day without missing a single day. Hundreds of people followed him as their teacher. He often traveled alone to the city, and those who accompanied him heard that whenever Mr. Shang moved his body, it sounded like the sound of jade being struck. Listeners found this remarkable.

    Some people said, "Mr. Shang's bones are truly lock bones. Lock bones are like vines, spreading throughout the body, so when they move, the body produces a clear and resonant sound. It is indeed so. I have heard that according to Buddhist scriptures, the Buddha has relics in his body, and Bodhisattvas have lock bones. Now Mr. Shang, is he not a Bodhisattva? However, it is difficult for ordinary people to discern this."

    Mr. Shang lived to be over ninety years old. One day, he bathed and dressed formally, then summoned all his disciples to a meal. He told them, "I am ninety years old, and I am about to die soon. You should cremate my body. Be careful not to go against my wishes." The disciples wept and said, "We will obey your command." That night, he died sitting up. Three days later, the disciples cremated Mr. Shang in the wilderness. When they examined his bones, they found them to be lock bones. The bones were connected in a continuous manner, as if sewn together. When the wind blew, a delicate and resonant sound was produced.

    Thus, the villagers contributed money to build a pagoda to bury Mr. Shang's lock bones in the pagoda.

    60

    ○宁勉
    　　宁勉者，云中人，年少，有勇气，善骑射，能以力格猛兽，不用兵仗。北都守健其勇，署为衙将。后以兵四千军于飞狐城。时蓟门帅骄悍，弃天子法，反书闻阙下。唐文宗皇帝诏北都守攻其南。诏未至，而蓟门兵夜伐飞狐。钲鼓震地，飞狐人汹然不自安，谓宁勉曰：“蓟兵豪健不可敌，今且至矣，其势甚急，愿空其邑以遁去。不然，旦暮拔吾城，吾不忍父子兄弟尽血贼刃下，悔宁可及！虽天子神武，安能雪吾冤乎幸熟计之。

    Explanation

    60

    Ning Mian, a young man from Yunzhong, was brave, skilled in riding and archery, and able to fight wild beasts with strength, without using weapons. The governor of the northern capital admired his bravery and appointed him as a military officer. Later, with four thousand troops, he was stationed at Feixu City. At that time, the commander of Jimo Gate was arrogant and fierce, disregarding the emperor's laws, and rebellion was reported to the imperial court. Emperor Wenzong of Tang ordered the governor of the northern capital to attack the southern part. Before the edict arrived, the Jimo troops attacked Feixu City at night. The sound of drums shook the ground, and the people of Feixu were restless and uneasy. They told Ning Mian, "The Jimo troops are fierce and cannot be defeated. They are about to arrive, and the situation is very urgent. We wish to evacuate the city and flee. Otherwise, they will capture our city soon. I cannot bear to see my family and brothers die by the enemy's sword. It will be too late to regret! Even if the emperor is divine and powerful, how can he clear our injustice? Please think carefully."

    61

    ”勉自度兵少，固不能折蓟帅之锋，将听邑人语，虑得罪于天子；欲坚壁自守，又虑一邑之人悉屠于贼手。忧既甚，而策未决。忽有谍者告曰：“贼尽溃矣。有弃甲在城下，愿取之。”勉即登城垣望，时月明朗，见贼兵驰走，颠踬者不可数，若有大兵击其后。勉大喜，开邑门，纵兵逐之，生擒数十人，得遣甲甚多。先是，勉好浮图氏，常阅佛书《金刚经》，既败蓟师，擒其虏以讯焉曰：“向夕望见城上有巨人数四，长二丈余，雄俊可惧，怒目呿吻，袒裼执剑。蓟人见之，惨然汗怵，即走避，又安有斗心乎？”勉悟，巨人乃金刚也，益自奇之。勉官御史中丞，后为清塞副使

    Explanation

    61

    Mian, assessing his own situation, believed that with his small number of troops, he could not break through the sharp edge of the Jì commander's forces. He considered listening to the words of the people of the town, fearing he might offend the emperor; yet, he also feared that if he were to defend firmly and remain on the defensive, the entire town might be completely slaughtered by the hands of the bandits. The worry was very great, but his strategy remained undecided. Suddenly, there was a spy who reported, "The bandits have completely broken and fled. There are discarded armor pieces at the base of the city walls, willing to be taken." Mian immediately climbed the city walls to look, and at that time, the moon was bright and clear. He saw the bandit soldiers fleeing in chaos, with countless fallen and stumbling, as if a large army was attacking from behind. Mian was greatly pleased and opened the city gates, letting his soldiers pursue them. They captured dozens alive and acquired many discarded armor pieces. Previously, Mian had been fond of the Buddha's teachings and often read the Buddhist scripture Diamond Sutra. After defeating the Jì commander's forces, he captured the prisoners and questioned them, saying, "Late in the day, I saw several giants on the city wall, over two zhang long, majestic and fearsome, with angry eyes and open mouths, naked and holding swords. When the Jì people saw them, they were terrified and sweating coldly, immediately fleeing, and how could they have any fighting spirit?" Mian realized that the giants were actually the Vajra (Diamond) warriors, becoming even more amazed by it. Mian held the position of Inspectorate Vice-Chancellor and later became the Deputy Envoy for Clearing the Frontier.

    62

    ○悟真寺僧
    　　唐贞观中，有王顺山悟真寺僧，夜如蓝溪，忽闻有诵《法华经》者，其声纤远。时星月回临，四望数十里，阒然无睹，其僧惨然有惧。及至寺，且白其事于群僧。明夕，俱于蓝溪听之，乃闻经声自地中发，于是以标表其所。明日，穷表下，得一颅骨在积壤中。其骨槁然，独唇吻与舌鲜而且润。遂持归寺，乃以石函置于千佛殿西轩下。自是，每夕常有诵《法华经》声在石函中。长安士女观者千数。后新罗僧客于寺，仅岁余，一日，寺僧尽下山，独新罗僧在，遂窃石函而去。寺僧迹其往，已归海东矣。时开元末年也

    Explanation

    62

    During the reign of Emperor Taizong of the Tang Dynasty, there was a monk from the Wuzhen Temple in Wanshun Mountain who, one night, went to Lan Creek. Suddenly, he heard someone reciting the Lotus Sutra, the sound being delicate and distant. The stars and moon were overhead, and looking in all directions for dozens of miles, everything was silent and empty. The monk felt fear and distress. When he returned to the temple, he reported the matter to the other monks. The next night, they all went to Lan Creek to listen, and then heard the recitation coming from underground. They marked the spot with a marker. The following day, they dug down to the marker and found a skull buried in accumulated earth. The bone was dry and shriveled, but only the lips and tongue were fresh and moist. They carried it back to the temple and placed it in a stone box under the west pavilion of the Thousand Buddha Hall. From then on, every night, there was a recitation of the Lotus Sutra coming from the stone box. Thousands of people from Chang'an came to watch. Later, a monk from Silla (Korea) stayed as a guest at the temple for almost a year. One day, when the monks had all gone down the mountain, the Silla monk was alone in the temple and stole the stone box and left. The monks followed him, but he had already returned to the East Sea (Korea). This was during the late Tang Dynasty, during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong (reigned 712-756).

    63

    ○师夜光
    　　唐师夜光者，蓟门人。少聪敏好学，雅尚浮屠氏，遂为僧，居于本郡。仅十年，尽通内典之奥。又有沙门惠达者，家甚富，有金钱巨万，贪夜光之学，因与为友。是时，玄宗皇帝好神仙释氏，穷索名僧方士，而夜光迫于贫，不得西去，心常怏怏。惠达知之，因以钱七十万资其行，且谓夜光曰：“师之学艺材用，愚窃以为无出于右者。圣上拔天下英俊，吾子必将首出群辈，沐浴恩渥，自此托迹缁徒，为明天子臣，可翘足而待也。然当是时，必有拥帚子门，幸无忘半面之旧。”夜光谢曰：“幸师厚贶我，得遂西上。傥为君之五品，则以报师之惠矣。

    Explanation

    63

    Master Yèguāng of the Tang Dynasty was a native of Jìmen. From a young age, he was intelligent, studious, and had a deep inclination towards Buddhism, eventually becoming a monk and residing in his hometown. Within just ten years, he had thoroughly mastered the profound teachings of the Buddhist scriptures. There was another monk named Huìdá, who came from a very wealthy family and possessed millions of coins. He admired Master Yèguāng's learning and thus became his friend. At that time, Emperor Xuanzong favored both Taoist immortals and Buddhism, seeking out famous monks and Taoist practitioners. However, Master Yèguāng, constrained by poverty, could not travel west, and he often felt discontented. Huìdá knew this and provided him with 700,000 coins to support his journey. He also said to Master Yèguāng, "Your learning, skills, and talents, I humbly believe there is none superior to you. The Holy Emperor selects the finest talents from the world, and you will certainly lead the pack, bathing in the emperor's favor. From now on, you will be part of the monastic community, serving as a minister to the future emperor, and can wait with folded feet. However, at that time, there will certainly be someone sweeping at your door, so please do not forget our brief acquaintance." Master Yèguāng replied, "Thank you for your generous gift, allowing me to go west. If I become a fifth-rank official, then I will repay your kindness."

    64

    ”夜光至长安，因赂九仙公主左右，得以温泉，命内臣选硕学僧十辈，与方士议论，夜光在选。演畅玄奥，发挥疑义，群僧无敢比者。上奇其辩，诏赐银印朱绶，拜四门博士，日侍左右，赐甲第洎金钱缯采以千数。时号幸臣。惠达遂自蓟门入长安访之。夜光闻惠达至，以为收债于己，甚不怿。惠达悟其旨，因告去。既以北归月余，夜光虑其再来，即密书与蓟门帅张廷珪：“近者惠达师至辇下，诬毁公缮完兵革，将为逆谋。人亦颇有知者。以公之忠，天下莫不闻之。积毁销金，不可不戒。”廷珪惊怒，即召惠达鞭杀之。后数日，夜光忽见惠达来庭中，骂夜光曰：“我以七十万钱资汝西上，奈何遽相诬谤，使我冤死。何负我之深也！”言讫，遂跃而上，珪拽夜光，久之乃亡所见。师氏家僮咸见之。其后数日，夜光卒。知

    Explanation

    64

    Yeguang arrived in Chang'an, where he bribed those close to Princess Jiu Xian, thereby gaining access to the hot springs. He was then ordered by the inner court officials to select ten learned monks, who engaged in discussions with Daoist scholars, and Yeguang was among those chosen. He elaborated on profound mysteries and clarified ambiguous points, and no other monks dared to compare with him. The emperor was amazed by his eloquence and issued an edict granting him a silver seal and red sash, appointing him as a Four Gates Doctor. He served at the emperor's side daily and was bestowed a mansion and thousands of gold coins and silk. At that time, he was called a favored minister. Huidada then traveled from Jimo to Chang'an to visit him. When Yeguang heard that Huidada had arrived, he thought that Huidada had come to collect debts from him, and he was very unhappy. Huidada understood his intention and took his leave. After returning north for more than a month, Yeguang feared that Huidada would return again, so he secretly wrote a letter to Zhang Tinggui, the commander of Jimo: "Recently, Huidada came to the imperial court and accused you of repairing weapons and armor, planning to rebel. Many people also knew about it. Given your loyalty, no one in the world does not know about it. Repeated slander can erode even gold, so one must be cautious." Zhang Tinggui was shocked and angry and immediately summoned Huidada to beat him to death. A few days later, Yeguang suddenly saw Huidada appear in the courtyard and cursed him, saying, "I lent you seventy thousand coins to travel west, how could you suddenly accuse me and cause me to die unjustly. How deeply have you wronged me!" After finishing his words, he leaped up. Zhang Tinggui pulled Yeguang, and after a long time, the vision disappeared. All of Huidada's disciples saw it. A few days later, Yeguang died.

    65

    ○李生（一）
    　　唐贞元中，有李生者，家河朔间，少有膂力，恃气好侠，不拘细行，常与轻薄少年游。年二十余，方折节读书，为歌诗，人颇称之。累为河朔官，后至深州录事参军。生美风仪，善谈笑，曲晓吏事，廉谨明干。至于击鞠饮酒，皆号为能，雅为太守所知

    Explanation

    65

    In the reign of Emperor Dezong during the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Li, who came from the Huai River region. When he was young, he had great physical strength, was proud and fond of chivalry, and did not adhere to minor details, often associating with frivolous young men. By the time he was in his twenties, he began to change his ways and start reading, writing poetry, and was highly praised by people. He served multiple times as an official in the Huai River region and later became a recorder and military officer in Shen Prefecture. Li had a beautiful appearance, was good at conversation and laughter, had a thorough understanding of administrative affairs, and was honest, cautious, and capable. He was skilled in playing polo and drinking, and was highly regarded by the prefect.

    66

    时王武俊帅成德军，恃功负众，不顾法度，支郡守畏之侧目。尝遣其子士真巡属郡，至深州，太守大具牛酒，所居备声乐宴士真。太守畏武俊，而奉士真之礼甚谨。又虑有以酒忤士真者，以故僚吏宾客，一不敢召。士真大喜，以为他郡莫能及。饮酒至夜，士真乃曰：“幸使君见待之厚，欲尽欢于今夕，岂无嘉宾，愿得召之。”太守曰：“偏郡无名人，惧副大使之威，不敢以他客奉宴席。唯录事参军李某，足以侍谈笑。”士真曰：“但命之。”于是召李生，入趋拜。士真目之，色甚怒。既而命坐，貌益恭，士真愈不悦，瞪顾攘腕，无向时之欢矣。太守惧，莫知所谓。顾视生靦然而汗，不能持杯，一坐皆愕。有顷，士真叱左右，缚李某系狱，左右即牵李袂，疾去械狱中。已而士真欢饮如初

    Explanation

    66

    During the time when Wang Wujun was commanding the Chengde Army, he relied on his achievements and held the masses in contempt, disregarding the laws and regulations. The governors of the subordinate prefectures looked at him with fear and suspicion. Once, he sent his son, Prince Wujun, to tour the subordinate prefectures, and they arrived at Shen Prefecture. The governor prepared a lavish banquet with beef and wine, and arranged music and entertainment for Prince Wujun. The governor feared Wang Wujun and treated Prince Wujun with great respect. He also worried that someone might offend Prince Wujun with their drinking, so he dared not invite any other officials or guests. Prince Wujun was greatly pleased, thinking that no other prefectures could match this hospitality. They drank until night, and then Prince Wujun said, "I am fortunate to be treated so well by you. I wish to enjoy the festivities tonight. Are there no distinguished guests? I wish to invite them." The governor replied, "This remote prefecture has no famous people. I fear the power of the Deputy Commander-in-Chief and dare not invite other guests to the banquet. Only the Chief Clerk, Mr. Li, is suitable to accompany our conversation." Prince Wujun said, "Just have him come." The governor then summoned Mr. Li, who entered and bowed. Prince Wujun looked at him with a very angry expression. After a while, he ordered him to sit, and Mr. Li became even more respectful, but Prince Wujun was even less pleased, glaring and clenching his fists, losing all his earlier joy. The governor was afraid and did not know what was happening. He looked at Mr. Li, who was embarrassed and sweating, unable to hold his cup, and everyone at the table was stunned. After a while, Prince Wujun shouted at his attendants to bind Mr. Li and lock him in prison. The attendants quickly took Mr. Li's sleeves and dragged him away to be locked up. After that, Prince Wujun continued drinking and feasting as before.

    67

    迨晓宴罢，太守且惊且惧，乃潜使于狱中讯李生，曰：“君貌甚恭，且未尝言，固非忤于王君，君宁自知耶！”李生悲泣久之，乃曰：“常闻释氏有现世之报，吾知之矣。某少贫，无以自资，由是好与侠士游，往往掠夺里人财帛。常驰马腰弓，往还太行道，日百余里。一日遇一年少，鞭骏骡，负二匹囊，吾利其资，顾左右，皆岩崖万仞，而日渐曛黑，遂力排之，堕于崖下。即疾驱其骡逆旅氏，解其囊，得缯绮百余段。自此家稍赡，因折弓夭，闭门读书，遂仕而至此，及今凡二十七矣。昨夕君侯命与王公之宴，既入，而视王公之貌，乃吾曩时所杀少年也。一拜之后，中心惭惕，自知死不朝夕。今则延颈待刃，又何言哉！为我谢君侯，幸知我深，敢以身后为托。”主

    Explanation

    67

    Until dawn, after the feast, the prefect was both surprised and fearful, and secretly sent someone to interrogate Li in the prison, saying, "Your appearance is very respectful, and you never spoke, certainly not in opposition to Lord Wang, do you know yourself?" Li wept bitterly for a long time, then said, "I often heard that the Buddhist doctrine of immediate retribution exists, and now I understand. In my youth, I was poor and unable to support myself, so I associated with knights and often seized the wealth of the villagers. I would ride a horse with a bow at my waist, travel back and forth on the Taihang road, covering over a hundred li each day. One day, I encountered a young man who was whipping a fine mule, carrying two bags. I coveted his wealth, looked around, and saw cliffs a thousand fathoms high, while the day grew dim. I then forcefully pushed him, and he fell into the ravine. I immediately drove his mule to the inn keeper, opened his bags, and obtained over a hundred pieces of silk and brocade. From then on, my family became somewhat wealthy, so I broke the bow and cut the string, closed the door, and studied. I entered officialdom and reached this position, and until now, it has been twenty-seven years. Last night, Lord Jun commanded an invitation to a banquet with Lord Wang. After entering, I saw the appearance of Lord Wang, and it was the young man I killed in the past. After the first bow, I felt ashamed and fearful, and I knew I would die soon. Now I extend my neck to await the blade, what more can I say? On my behalf, thank Lord Jun, fortunately, you understand me deeply, dare to entrust my affairs after death."

    68

    有顷，士真醉悟，急召左右：“往取李某首来。”左右即于狱中斩其首以进。士真熟视而笑，既而又与太守大饮于郡斋。酒醉，太守因欢，乃起曰：“某不才，幸得守一郡，而副大使下察弊政，宽不加罪，为恩厚矣。昨日副大使命某召他客，属郡僻小无客，不足奉欢宴者。窃以李某善饮酒，故请召之。而李某愚憨，不习礼法，大忤于明公，实某之罪也。今明公既已诛之，宜矣。窃有所未晓，敢以上问。李某之罪为何愿得明数之，且用诫于将来也。”士真笑曰：“李生亦无罪，但吾一见之遂忿然激吾心，已有戮之之意。今既杀之，吾亦不知其所以然也。君无复言。”主

    Explanation

    68

    After a short while, Shi Zhen, having become enlightened while drunk, hastily summoned his attendants: "Go fetch Li's head." The attendants immediately beheaded him in the prison and presented his head. Shi Zhen looked at it carefully and laughed, and afterwards, he drank heavily with the prefect in the prefectural hall. The prefect, being drunk, became cheerful. The prefect then said: "I am unworthy, but fortunately I have been appointed to govern this prefecture, and the deputy ambassador came down to inspect my corrupt governance, but was lenient and did not punish me. Yesterday, the deputy ambassador ordered me to invite other guests, but the prefecture is remote and small, so there were no guests. I privately thought that Li was good at drinking, so I invited him. But Li was foolish and ignorant, not accustomed to etiquette, and greatly offended the honorable official. It was my fault. Now that the honorable official has already executed him, it is appropriate. I have something that I do not understand, and dare to ask above. What was Li's crime, and may I ask for a clear account of it, so that I may learn from it in the future?" Shi Zhen laughed and said: "Li had no crime, but I saw him and immediately felt a strong aversion that stirred my heart, and I had the intention to kill him. Now that he is dead, I do not know why I did it. You should not speak of it again."

    69

    及宴罢，太守密讯其年，则二十有七矣。盖李生杀少年之岁，而士真生于王氏也。太守叹异久之，因以家财厚葬李生
    ○郑生
    　　唐荥阳郑生，善骑射，以勇悍趫捷闻，家于巩雒之郊。尝一日乘醉，手弓腰矢，驰捷马，独驱田野间，去某居且数十里。会天暮，大风雨，生庇于大木下久之。及雨霁，已夕矣。迷失道，纵马行，见道傍有门宇，乃神庙也。生以马系门外，将止屋中，忽怵然心动，即匿身东庑下。闻庙左空舍中窣窣然，生疑其鬼，因引弓震弦以伺之。俄见一丈夫，身长，衣短后卓衣，负囊仗剑，自空舍中出。既而倚剑扬言曰：“我盗也。

    Explanation

    69

    After the banquet ended, the magistrate secretly inquired about his age, and it turned out he was twenty-seven years old. It was in the year when Li killed the youth, and indeed, the scholar was born into the Wang family. The magistrate was astonished for a long time, and so he used his family wealth to give Li a grand burial.

    ○ Zheng Sheng

    In the Tang dynasty, Zheng Sheng of Xingyang was skilled in riding and archery, renowned for his bravery, fierceness, and agility. He lived in the suburbs of Gong and Luo. Once, while drunk, he held a bow in his hand and an arrow on his hip, rode a swift horse, and rode alone through the fields, nearly several tens of miles away from someone’s residence. When the sky darkened and a great wind and rain came, Zheng took shelter under a large tree for a long time. When the rain stopped, it was already evening. He lost his way and let the horse run freely. He saw a gate and building by the roadside, which was a shrine. Zheng tied his horse outside the gate and was about to enter the building, but suddenly felt a sense of alarm and fear, and immediately hid himself beneath the eastern wing of the hall. He heard a rustling sound from the empty room on the temple’s left side. Zheng suspected it was a ghost, and so he drew his bow and tightened the string to wait for it. Soon, a tall man emerged from the empty room, wearing a short-sleeved robe fastened at the back, carrying a bag and holding a sword. He leaned on his sword and declared loudly, “I am a thief.”

    70

    尔岂非盗乎？”郑生曰：“吾家于巩雒之郊，向者独驱田间，适遇大风雨，迷而失道，故匿身于此。”仗剑者曰：“子既不为盗，得无害我之心乎且我遁去，道必经东庑下。愿解弓弦以授我，使我得去。不然，且死于竖子矣。”先是，生常别以一弦致袖中，既解弦投于剑客前，密以袖中弦系弓上。贼既得弦，遂至东庑下，将杀郑生以灭口。急以矢系弦，贼遂去，因曰：“吾子果智者，某罪固当死矣。”生曰：“我不为害，尔何为疑我？”贼再拜谢，生即去西庑下以避
    　　贼既去，生惧其率徒再来，于是登木自匿。久之，星月始明，忽见一妇人，貌甚冶，自空舍中出，泣于庭。问之，妇人曰：“妾家于村中，为盗见诱至此。且利妾衣装，遂杀妾空舍中，弃某尸而去。幸君子为雪其冤。”又曰：“今夕当匿于田横墓。愿急逐之，无失。”生诺之，妇人谢而去。及晓，生视之，果见尸。即驰马至洛，具白于河南尹郑叔则。尹命吏捕之，果得贼于田横墓中

    Explanation

    70

    "You are not a thief?" Zheng Sheng replied, "I live in the outskirts of Gong and Luo. Earlier, I was driving alone in the fields and encountered a sudden storm, which caused me to lose my way, so I hid here." The man with the sword said, "Since you are not a thief, could you have ill intentions against me? As I flee, I must pass under the eastern wing. Please untie the bowstring and give it to me, so I can leave. Otherwise, I will be killed by this young man." Previously, Zheng Sheng had hidden another string in his sleeve. After untying the string and throwing it before the sword-wielder, he secretly tied the string from his sleeve to the bow. The thief, having obtained the string, went to the eastern wing and intended to kill Zheng Sheng to silence him. Zheng Sheng quickly tied an arrow to the string, and the thief fled. The thief then said, "You truly are a wise one, I truly deserve to die." Zheng Sheng replied, "I do not harm you, why do you doubt me?" The thief knelt twice and thanked him, and Zheng Sheng then went to the western wing to hide.

    After the thief left, Zheng Sheng feared that he might return with his followers, so he climbed a tree to hide. After a long time, the stars and moon began to appear, and he suddenly saw a woman with a beautiful appearance emerge from an empty house and weep in the courtyard. He asked her, and she said, "I lived in a village, but was lured here by bandits. They also coveted my clothes and belongings, so they killed me in the empty house and left my body behind. I hope you can clear my name." She also said, "Tonight, I should hide in the tomb of Tian Heng. Please hurry to catch them and do not let them escape." Zheng Sheng agreed, and the woman thanked him and left. By morning, Zheng Sheng saw the body. He then rode his horse to Luoyang and reported the incident in detail to Governor Zheng Shuce of Henan. The governor ordered officials to capture them, and they were indeed found in the tomb of Tian Heng.

    71

    ○樊宗谅
    　　唐樊宗谅为密州刺史，时属邑有群盗，提兵入邑氓殷氏家，掠夺金帛，杀其父子，死者三人。刺史捕之甚急，月余不获。有钜鹿魏南华者，寓居齐鲁之间，家甚贫，宗谅命摄司法掾。一夕，南华梦数人，皆被发，列诉于南华曰，姓殷氏，父子三人，俱无罪而死，愿明公雪其冤。南华曰：“杀汝者为谁？”对曰：“某所居东十里，有姓姚者，乃贼之魁也。”南华许诺，惊寤。数日，宗谅谓南华曰：“盗杀吾氓，且一月矣，莫穷其迹，岂非吏不奉职乎尔为司法官，第往验之。”南华驰往。未至，忽见一狐起于路傍深草中，驰入里人姚氏所居，噪而逐者以数百数，其狐入一穴中，南华命以锸发之，得金帛甚多，乃群盗劫殷氏财也。即召姚氏子，讯其所自，目动词讷，即收劾之，果盗之魁也。自是尽擒其支党，且十辈。其狐虽匿于穴中，穷之卒无所见也，岂非冤魂之所假欤时大和中也

    Explanation

    71

    ○Fan Zongliang
    　　During the Tang dynasty, Fan Zongliang served as the governor of Mizhou. At that time, there were a group of bandits in the subordinate counties. They led troops into the home of the commoner Yin family, plundered gold and silk, and killed their father and sons, three people died. The governor captured them very urgently, but after more than a month, they were not caught. There was a man named Wei Nan Hua from Juxu, who lived temporarily between Qi and Lu. His family was very poor, and Zongliang ordered him to take on the role of judicial official. One night, Nan Hua dreamed of several people, all with their hair loose, who lined up and told him, saying, "We are the Yin family, father and sons three, all died without any guilt. We hope you will clear our innocence." Nan Hua asked, "Who killed you?" They replied, "In the east of where we live, there is a man surnamed Yao, who is the leader of the bandits." Nan Hua agreed and woke up in a hurry. A few days later, Zongliang said to Nan Hua, "The bandits killed my commoners, and it has been more than a month, but we cannot find their tracks. Is it not because the officials are not fulfilling their duties? You are the judicial official, just go and investigate." Nan Hua rushed to the place. Before he arrived, he suddenly saw a fox rise from the deep grass by the roadside and rush into the residence of the local man surnamed Yao. Those who were shouting and chasing numbered in the hundreds. The fox entered a hole. Nan Hua ordered to dig it up with a spade and found a lot of gold and silk. It was the bandits who robbed the wealth of the Yin family. He immediately summoned the son of the Yao family, questioned him about his origins. The man's eyes moved, and he spoke hesitantly. Nan Hua immediately arrested and impeached him. He was indeed the leader of the bandits. From then on, all his accomplices were captured, about ten in total. The fox, although hidden in the hole, was finally found. Is it not the soul of the wronged person that has taken possession of the fox? It was during the Dahan period.

    72

    ○王洞微
    　　唐汾州景云观道士王洞微者，家于孝义县。初为小胥，性喜杀，常钓弋渔猎，自弱冠至壮年，凡杀狼狐雉兔洎鱼鳖飞鸟计以万数。后为里尹，患热病月余，忽觉室内有禽兽鱼鳖万数，环其榻而噬之。疮痏被身，殆无完肤。中夕之后，其父母兄弟俱闻洞微卧内有群鸟啁啾。历然可辨。凡数年，疾益甚。或有谓洞微父曰：“汝子病且亟，宜迁居景云观。”于是卜日徙居。月余，会群道士修斋授箓，是夕洞微瘳。后十年，竟以疾卒。

    Explanation

    72

    Wang Dongwei, a Daoist priest of the Jingyun Temple in Fen Prefecture during the Tang Dynasty, lived in Xiaoyi County. Initially serving as a minor clerk, he had a nature that enjoyed killing and often engaged in fishing, hunting, and trapping. From the age of adulthood to middle age, he killed tens of thousands of wolves, foxes, pheasants, rabbits, fish, turtles, and birds. Later, he became a village magistrate and suffered from a fever for more than a month. Suddenly, he felt that there were tens of thousands of birds, beasts, fish, and turtles inside the room, surrounding his bed and gnawing at him. Scars and wounds covered his body, almost no part of his skin was intact. After midnight, his parents and brothers all heard a group of birds chirping inside his room, clearly distinguishable. For several years, his illness became increasingly severe. Someone told Wang's father, "Your son is gravely ill; it would be best to move him to the Jingyun Temple." So, they moved on a divinely chosen day. After a month, a gathering of Daoist priests performed a ritual and granted talismans, and that night Wang's fever subsided. Ten years later, he ultimately died from the illness.

    73

    ○叱金像
    　　国初有神像，用金而制。传云，周、隋间有术士熔范而成之。天后朝，因命置于宫中，扃其殿宇甚严。玄宗尝幸其殿，启而观焉。时肃宗在中宫，代宗尚稚，俱侍上。上问内臣力士曰：“此神像何所异亦有说乎？”力士曰：“此前代所制，可以占王者在位之几何年耳。其法当厉声而叱之，苟年甚永，则其像摇震亦久，不然，一撼而止。”上即严声叱之，其像若有惧，摇震移时，仆于地，上喜笑曰：“诚如说，我为天子几何时？”力士因再拜贺。上即命太子叱之，其像微震。又命皇孙叱之，亦动摇久之。上曰：“吾孙似我。”其后玄宗在位五十载，肃宗在位凡六年，代宗在位十九年，尽契其占也。斋

    Explanation

    73

    Chastising the Golden Image**

    In the early years of the Tang Dynasty, there was a divine image made of gold. According to tradition, during the period between the Zhou and Sui dynasties, a Taoist magician melted and cast it into shape. During Wu Zetian's reign, it was placed in the palace and kept very strictly locked away. Emperor Xuanzong once visited the palace and inspected the image. At the time, Emperor Suzong was in the imperial palace, and Emperor Daizong was still young; both attended the emperor. The emperor asked the eunuch Li Shi, "What is special about this divine image? Is there a story behind it?" Li Shi replied, "This image was made in ancient times and can predict how many years a ruler will reign. The method is to shout loudly at it. If the reign is long, the image will shake and tremble for a long time; otherwise, it will only shake once and stop." The emperor then shouted loudly at the image, and it seemed to be afraid, shaking and trembling for a long time before falling to the ground. The emperor laughed and said, "Just as was said, how long will I reign as emperor?" Li Shi then bowed twice in celebration. The emperor then ordered the crown prince to shout at the image, which shook slightly. He then ordered the emperor's grandson to shout at it, and it also shook for a long time. The emperor said, "My grandson resembles me." Later, Emperor Xuanzong reigned for fifty years, Emperor Suzong for six years, and Emperor Daizong for nineteen years, all matching the prediction.

    74

    ○迎光王
    　　太子宾客卢真有犹子，曾为沙门，会昌中，沙汰归俗，阴补为光王府参军。一日，梦前师至其家而问讯焉。卢则告卑官屑屑然，非其愿也，常思落发，再披缁褐。师曰：“汝诚有是志，像教兴复，非晚也。”语未竟，俄四面见日月旌旆，千乘万骑。喧言迎光王即皇帝位。未几，武帝崩，光王果即皇帝位。至是，竟符其事焉。知
    ○彭偃
    　　唐大历中，彭偃未仕时，尝有人谓曰：“君当得珠而贵，后且有祸。”寻为官得罪，谪为澧州司马。既至，以江中多蚌，偃喜，以为珠可取，即命人采之。获蚌甚多，而卒无有应。及朱泚反，召偃为伪中书舍人，偃方悟得珠，乃朱泚知

    Explanation

    74

    ○迎光王
    　　The Grand Chamberlain Lu Zhen had a nephew who once served as a Buddhist monk. During the Kai Cheng period, he was forced to return to secular life due to the government's anti-Buddhist policies and was unofficially appointed as a military officer in the Prince Guang's household. One day, he dreamed that his former teacher came to his home and greeted him. Lu Zhen told him that his lowly position was not what he desired and that he often wished to shave his head again and return to monastic life. The teacher said, "You truly have this wish, and the revival of Buddhism is not too late." Before he finished speaking, suddenly, from all sides, he saw banners with the sun and moon, thousands of chariots and horses. They loudly proclaimed that Prince Guang was ascending to the throne as emperor. Soon after, Emperor Wu died, and Prince Guang indeed became the emperor. Thus, the prophecy was fulfilled.

    ○彭偃
    　　During the Dali period of the Tang Dynasty, Peng Yan was not yet in office when someone told him, "You will gain wealth and status by finding a pearl, but you will also suffer misfortune later." Soon after, he was demoted and exiled to the position of military governor of Li Prefecture. Upon arriving, he was delighted because there were many clams in the river and believed that pearls could be obtained. He immediately ordered people to collect them. They gathered many clams, but ultimately, no pearls were found. When Zhu Ci rebelled, he summoned Peng Yan to serve as a false Secretary of the Central Secretariat. At that moment, Peng Yan realized that the "pearl" was actually Zhu Ci's rebellion.

    75

    ○李师道（一）
    　　唐李师道既以青齐叛，章武帝将讨之，凡数年而王师不胜。师道益骄。尝一日坐于堂，其榻前有银鼎，忽相鼓，其一鼎耳足尽坠。后月余，刘悟手刃师道，青齐遂平。盖银鼎相鼓之兆也。知
    ○王涯
    　　唐丞相王涯，大和九年掌邦赋，又主簿盐铁。其子仲翔尝一日避暑于山亭，忽见家僮数十皆无首，被血来仲翔前。仅食顷，方不见。仲翔惊异且甚，即具白之，愿解去权位。涯不听。是岁冬十一月，果有郑注之祸。知
    ○温造
    　　新昌里尚书温造宅，桑道茂尝居之。庭有二柏树甚高。桑生曰：“夫人之所居，木蕃茂者，皆宜去之。且木盛则土衰，由是居人有病者，乃土衰之致也。”于是以铁数十钧镇于柏树下。既而告人曰：“后有居，发吾所镇之地者，其家长当死。”唐大和九年，温造居其宅，因修建堂宇，遂发地得桑生所镇之铁。后数日造果卒

    Explanation

    75

    ○李师道（一）

    唐李师道既以青齐叛，章武帝将讨之，凡数年而王师不胜。师道益骄。尝一日坐于堂，其榻前有银鼎，忽相鼓，其一鼎耳足尽坠。后月余，刘悟手刃师道，青齐遂平。盖银鼎相鼓之兆也。

    During the Tang Dynasty, Li Shidao rebelled in Qing and Qi regions. Emperor Xianzong of Tang planned to suppress him, but the imperial army was not victorious for several years. Li Shidao grew increasingly arrogant. One day, while sitting in the hall, he noticed two silver tripods before his seat. Suddenly, they began to strike each other, and one of them fell completely apart. More than a month later, Liu Wu killed Li Shidao with his own hands, and Qing and Qi were finally pacified. It was said that the incident with the silver tripods was a sign of the impending defeat.

    ○王涯

    唐丞相王涯，大和九年掌邦赋，又主簿盐铁。其子仲翔尝一日避暑于山亭，忽见家僮数十皆无首，被血来仲翔前。仅食顷，方不见。仲翔惊异且甚，即具白之，愿解去权位。涯不听。是岁冬十一月，果有郑注之祸。

    In the ninth year of the Dazhong era, Wang Ya, a high-ranking official, oversaw the national finances and the salt and iron industries. One day, his son Zhongxiang sought refuge in a mountain pavilion to escape the heat. Suddenly, he saw dozens of servants without heads, covered in blood, approaching him. After just a meal's time, they disappeared. Zhongxiang was greatly alarmed and immediately reported what he had seen, wishing to resign from his position. However, Wang Ya did not listen. In the eleventh month of that year, Wang Ya indeed suffered the calamity brought by Zheng Zhu.

    ○温造

    新昌里尚书温造宅，桑道茂尝居之。庭有二柏树甚高。桑生曰：“夫人之所居，木蕃茂者，皆宜去之。且木盛则土衰，由是居人有病者，乃土衰之致也。”于是以铁数十钧镇于柏树下。既而告人曰：“后有居，发吾所镇之地者，其家长当死。”唐大和九年，温造居其宅，因修建堂宇，遂发地得桑生所镇之铁。后数日造果卒。

    In Xinchang Lane, there was a residence belonging to Minister Wen Zao, where Sang Daomao once lived. The courtyard had two very tall cypress trees. Sang Daomao said, "Where people live, if the trees are abundant and flourishing, they should all be removed. Moreover, when the trees are abundant, the earth becomes weak, which is why the residents may fall ill." Therefore, he placed several dozen iron hooks under the cypress trees. Later, he told others, "If anyone disturbs the place I have secured, the head of the household will die." In the ninth year of the Dazhong era, Wen Zao lived in this residence and began to renovate the hall and buildings. He dug up the ground and found the iron hooks that Sang Daomao had placed. Several days later, Wen Zao indeed died.

    76

    ○李宗闵
    　　唐丞相李宗闵，大和七年夏出镇汉中。明年冬，再入相。又明年夏中，尝退朝于靖安里第，其榻前有熨斗，忽跳掷久之。宗闵异且恶。是时李训、郑注以奸诈得幸。数言于帝。训知之，遂奏以致其罪。后旬日，有诏贬为明州刺史，连贬潮州司户，盖其兆也。主
    ○柳公济
    　　柳公济尚书，唐大和中奉诏讨李同揵。既出师，无何，麾枪忽折。客有见者，叹曰：“夫大将军出师，其门旗及麾枪折者，军必败。不然，上将死。”后数月，公济果薨。凡军出征，有乌鸢随其后者，皆败亡之徵。有曾敬云者，尝为北都裨将。李师道叛时，曾将行营兵士数千人，每出军，有乌鸢随其后，即军必败，率以为常。后舍家为僧，住于太原凝定寺。大和九年，罗立言为京兆尹，尝因入朝，既冠带，引镜自视，不见其首。遂语于季弟约言。后果为李训连坐，诛死

    Explanation

    76

    李宗闵

    The Tang chancellor Li Zongmin was dispatched to Hanzhong in the summer of the seventh year of the Dazhong era. The following winter, he re-entered the position of chancellor. In the middle of the next summer, after returning home from court duties in Jing'an Lane, he noticed that an iron used for pressing clothes on his bed suddenly jumped and moved for a long time. Li Zongmin found this strange and unpleasant. At that time, Li Xun and Zheng Zhu had gained favor through their cunning and deceit. They frequently spoke to the emperor about these matters. Li Xun learned of this and subsequently submitted a report to the emperor, leading to Li Zongmin's punishment. Ten days later, an imperial decree demoted him to the position of governor of Mingzhou, and he was further demoted to the position of household administrator in Chaozhou. This was indeed a sign of his misfortune.

    柳公济

    Shangshu Liu Gongji, during the Dazhong era, was ordered by the emperor to campaign against Li Tongjian. After setting out, shortly thereafter, his standard spear suddenly broke. A guest who saw this exclaimed, "When a great general sets out on a campaign, if his standard flag or spear breaks, the army will surely suffer defeat. Otherwise, the general himself will die." A few months later, Liu Gongji indeed passed away. Generally, if crows or kites follow a military force, it is a sign of impending disaster. There was a man named Zeng Jingyun, who once served as a deputy general in the northern capital. During the rebellion led by Li Shidao, Zeng commanded several thousand troops in the field army. Whenever he set out on a campaign, crows or kites would follow him, indicating that the army would surely suffer defeat, and this became a common occurrence. Later, he left his family to become a monk and settled in the Ninding Temple in Taiyuan. In the ninth year of the Dazhong era, Luo Liyan, who served as the governor of Jingzhao, once entered the court wearing his official robes. Looking into a mirror, he could not see his head. He then told his younger brother about this incident. Eventually, he was implicated in the same case as Li Xun and was executed.

    77

    ○刘遵
    　　故刑部尚书沛国刘遵古，大和四年节度东蜀军。先是，蜀人有富蓄群书，刘既至，尝假其数百篇，然未尽详阅。明年夏，涪江大泛，突入壁垒，溃里中庐舍。历数日，水势始平，而刘之图书器玩尽为暴水濡污。刘始命列于庭以曝之。后数日，刘于群书中得《周易正义》一轴，笔势奇妙，字体稍古，盖非近代之书也。其卷未有题云：“上元二年三月十一日，因读《周易》，著此正义，従兹易号十二三，岁至一人八千口，当有大水漂溺，因得舒转晒曝。衡阳道士李德初。”刘阅其题，叹且久。穷其所自，乃蜀人所蓄之书也。于是召宾掾以视之。所谓“易号十二三，岁至一人八千口”者，“一人八千口”盖“大和”字也。自上元，历宝应、广德、永泰、大历、建中、兴元、贞元、永贞、元和、长庆、宝历，至大和，凡更号十有三矣。与其记果相契然。不知李德初何人耳抑非假其名以示于后乎！主

    Explanation

    77

    Former Minister of Punishment from Pei State, Liu Zungu, during the fourth year of the Great and Harmonious era (830 AD), served as the military governor of the eastern part of Shu. Previously, there was a wealthy book collector in Shu who had amassed a vast collection of books. When Liu arrived, he borrowed several hundred volumes, though he did not read them all in detail. The following summer, the Fu River experienced a major flood that broke through the city's defenses and destroyed houses within the city. After several days, the water level began to recede, but Liu's books, artifacts, and treasures were all soaked and damaged by the flood. Liu then ordered the books to be arranged in the courtyard to dry them out. A few days later, Liu found a scroll titled The Commentary on the I Ching among the books. The handwriting was exquisite, and the script was slightly ancient, indicating that it was not a recent work. At the end of the scroll, it was inscribed: "On the eleventh day of the third month of the second year of the Shangyuan era (761 AD), while reading the I Ching, I wrote this commentary. From this point, there have been twelve or thirteen era names, leading to the Great and Harmonious era, when a great flood would occur, and the books would be spread out to dry. Hengyang Taoist priest Li Dechu." Liu read the inscription and was amazed for a long time. He investigated the origin of the book and discovered that it was one of the books collected by the people of Shu. He then summoned officials to see it. The phrase "twelve or thirteen era names, leading to the Great and Harmonious era" refers to the fact that "one person with eight thousand mouths" is the era name "Great and Harmonious." From the Shangyuan era, through Baoying, Guangde, Yongtai, Dali, Jianzhong, Xingyuan, Zhenyuan, Yongzhen, Yuanhe, Changqing, and Baoli, to the Great and Harmonious era, there have been thirteen era names in total. The record matches the actual situation. It is uncertain whether Li Dechu was a real person or if the name was fabricated for future reference.

    78

    ○圣画
    　　云花寺有圣画殿，长安中谓之七圣画。初，殿宇既制，寺僧召画工，将命施彩饰，会贵其直，不合寺僧祈酬，亦竟去。后数日，有二少年诣寺来谒曰：“某，善画者也。今闻此寺将命画工，某不敢利其价，愿输功，可乎？”寺僧欲先阅其迹，少年曰：“某弟兄凡七人，未尝画于长安中，宁有迹乎。”寺僧以为妄，稍难之。少年曰：“某既不纳师之直，苟不可师意，即命圬其壁，未为晚也。”寺僧利其无直，遂许之。后一日，七人果至。各挈采绘，将入其殿，且谓僧曰：“従此去七日，慎勿启吾之门，亦不劳饮食，盖以畏风日所侵铄也。可以泥锢吾门，无使有纤隙，不然，则不能施其妙矣。”僧従其语。如是凡六日，阒无有闻。僧相语曰：“此必他怪也。且不可果其约。”遂相与发其封。户既启，有七鸽翩翩望空飞去。其殿中采绘，俨若四隅，唯西北墉未尽其饰焉。后画工来见之，大惊曰：“真神妙之笔也。”于是无敢继其色者

    Explanation

    78

    Divine Painting

    Yunhua Temple has a Divine Painting Hall, known in Chang'an as the Seven Divine Paintings. Initially, the hall had already been constructed, and the temple monks summoned painters to apply colorful decorations. However, the price was considered too high, and it did not meet the expectations of the monks, so they eventually left. After several days, two young men came to the temple and said, "We are skilled painters. We have heard that this temple is about to hire painters. We do not seek profit from our work; we wish to offer our labor, may we do so?" The monks wanted to see their previous work first, but the young men replied, "My brothers and I, seven in total, have never painted in Chang'an. How could we have any previous work?" The monks thought they were lying and tried to discourage them. The young men said, "We do not accept payment from the monks. If our work does not meet your expectations, then we will simply whitewash the walls, and it will not be too late." The monks, seeing that they would not have to pay, agreed to their offer. The next day, the seven men arrived as promised. Each carried their painting tools and entered the hall, telling the monks, "For the next seven days, carefully do not open our door, nor provide us with food or drink, as we must avoid the influence of wind and sun. You may seal our door with mud, leaving no gaps, or else we cannot achieve our work's perfection." The monks followed their instructions. For six days, there was no sound. The monks whispered to each other, "This must be some kind of supernatural occurrence. We cannot keep our promise." They then broke the seal together. When the door was opened, seven pigeons flew gracefully into the sky. Inside the hall, the paintings were arranged as if in the four corners, except that the northwest wall was not fully decorated. Later, the painters who had seen the work were greatly surprised and said, "Truly the work of a divine and wondrous brush!" After that, no one dared to continue the coloring.

    79

    ○李生（二）
    　　天宝中，有陇西李生自白衣调选桂州参军，既至任，以热病旬余，觉左乳痛不可忍，及视之，隆若痈肿之状。即召医验其脉，医者曰：“脏腑无他。若臆中有物，以喙攻其乳，乳痛，而痈不可为也。”又旬余，病甚，一日痈溃，有一雉自左乳中突而飞出，不知所止。是夕，李生卒。知
    ○娄师德
    　　娄师德布衣时，常因沉疾，梦一人，衣紫，来榻前再拜曰：“君之疾且间矣，幸与某偕去。”却引公出。忽觉力甚捷，自谓疾愈。行路数里，见有廨署，左右吏卒，朱门甚高，曰：“地府院。”惊曰：“何地府院而在人间乎？”紫衣者对曰：“冥道固与人接迹。世人又安得而知之？”公入其院，吏卒辟易四退。见一空室曰“司命署”。问：“职何如？”对曰：“主世人禄命之籍也。”公因窃视之，有书数千幅在几上，傍有绿衣者，称为按掾。公命出己之籍，按取一轴以进，公阅之，书己名，载其禄位年月，周历清贯，出入台辅，寿至八十有五。

    Explanation

    79

    Li Sheng (Part Two)
    During the Tianbao era, there was a man from Longxi named Li Sheng who was selected for the position of military officer in Gui Prefecture despite not holding an official rank. After taking up his post, he fell ill with a fever for over ten days and felt severe pain in his left breast. Upon examination, it appeared to be a boil or abscess. He summoned a doctor to check his pulse, and the doctor said, "There is nothing wrong with your internal organs. If there is something inside your chest, probing the breast with a tool will cause pain, and the boil will not heal." After another ten days, his condition worsened. One day, the boil burst, and a pheasant suddenly flew out from his left breast, its destination unknown. That night, Li Sheng died.

    Liu Shide
    When Liu Shide was still an ordinary man, he often suffered from a long-term illness. One night, he dreamed of a man dressed in purple who bowed twice in front of his bed and said, "Your illness will soon subside. Please accompany me." The man then led him out. Suddenly, he felt a great surge of strength and believed his illness was cured. After walking several miles, he saw a government office with red doors and officials on either side. The man said, "This is the Underworld Court." Liu Shide was surprised and asked, "How can the Underworld Court be in the human world?" The man in purple replied, "The path of the underworld intersects with the human world. How could ordinary people know about it?" Liu Shide entered the court, and the officials retreated in awe. He saw an empty room and asked, "What is this place?" The man replied, "This is the Office of the Life and Death Registrar." Liu Shide asked, "What is your duty?" The man said, "I record the fortunes and destinies of people." Liu Shide secretly looked at the records and saw thousands of documents on the desk. Beside him was a man in green clothing who called himself a clerk. Liu Shide asked the clerk to bring his own record. The clerk took a scroll and handed it to Liu Shide, who read it and found his name listed. It recorded his official rank, position, and lifespan, showing a clear and honorable career path, serving in high-ranking government positions, and living to be eighty-five years old.

    80

    鉴之喜，谓按掾曰：“某一布衣耳，无饥冻足矣。又安敢有他望乎？”言未毕，忽有一声沿空而下，震砌檐宇。按掾惊曰：“天鼓且动，君宜疾归，不可留矣。”闻其声，遂惊悟，始为梦游耳。时天已曙，其所居东邻有佛寺，击晓钟，盖按掾所谓天鼓者也。是日，疾亦间焉。后入仕，历官咸如所载者。及为西凉帅，一日，见黄衣使者至阁前曰：“冥途小吏，奉命请公。”公曰：“吾尝见司命之籍，纪吾之位，当至上台，寿凡八十有五，何为遽见命耶！”黄衣人曰：“公任某官时，尝误杀无辜人，位与寿为主吏所降，今则穷矣。”言讫忽亡所见。自是卧疾，三日乃薨也。知

    Explanation

    80

    Jian was pleased and said to the assistant magistrate, "I am just a commoner, content with not being hungry or freezing. How could I dare to have other ambitions?" Before he finished speaking, suddenly a sound descended from the sky, shaking the walls and eaves. The assistant magistrate was startled and said, "The heavenly drum is about to sound; you should quickly return home, you cannot stay here anymore." Hearing the sound, Jian suddenly woke up from his dream, realizing it was all just a dream journey. At that time, the sky had already dawned, and there was a Buddhist temple to the east of his residence, striking the dawn bell. This was what the assistant magistrate had referred to as the "heavenly drum." That day, his illness also improved. Later, he entered government service, and the officials he held were all as recorded. When he became the commander of Xiliang, one day, he saw a messenger in yellow robes come to the front of the hall and say, "A minor official from the underworld, has been ordered to invite you." Jian replied, "I have seen the register of the God of Destiny, which records my position, which should reach the highest rank, and my lifespan is eighty-five years. Why am I summoned so soon?" The messenger in yellow robes said, "When you held a certain office, you once mistakenly killed an innocent person. Your rank and lifespan were lowered by the chief official, and now you are in dire straits." After finishing his words, he suddenly disappeared. From then on, Jian fell ill and died after three days.

    81

    ○杨炎
    　　故相国杨炎未仕时，尝梦陟高山之巅，下瞰人境，杳不可辨；仰而视之，见瑞日在咫尺，红光赫然，洞照万里。公因举左右手以捧之，炎燠之气，如热心目。久而方寤。视其手，尚沥然而汗。公异之，因语于人。有解者曰：“夫日者，人君像也。今梦登山以捧日，将非登相位而辅人君乎？”其后杨公周历清贯，遂登相位。果叶捧日之祥也。斋
    ○窦参
    　　贞元中，相国窦参为御史中丞。尝一夕梦德宗召对于便殿，问以经国之务。上喜，因以锦半臂赐之。及寤，奇其梦，默而念曰：“臂者，庇也。大邑所以庇吾身也。今梦半臂者，岂上以我叨居显位，将给半俸，俾我致政乎？”蹙然久之。因以梦话于人，客有解曰：“公之梦，祥符也。且半臂者，盖被股肱之衣也。今公梦天子赐之，岂非上将以股肱之位而委公乎？”明日，果拜中书侍郎平章事。知

    Explanation

    81

    Yang Yan

    Former Prime Minister Yang Yan, before he entered officialdom, once dreamed of ascending to the peak of a high mountain. From there, he looked down upon the human world, which was vaguely indistinguishable; looking up, he saw the auspicious sun within arm's reach, its red light bright and striking, illuminating ten thousand miles. He then raised his left and right hands to hold it, feeling a warm and radiant energy as if it were heating his heart and eyes. After a long time, he woke up. Looking at his hands, they were still sweating profusely. He found the dream strange and told it to others. Someone explained, "The sun represents the ruler. Now you dream of ascending a mountain to hold the sun, is it not a sign of ascending to the position of chancellor and assisting the ruler?" Later, Yang Yan traversed through clean and prestigious positions and eventually became chancellor. Indeed, it fulfilled the auspicious sign of holding the sun.

    Dou Can

    During the Zhenyuan period, Prime Minister Dou Can served as the Chief Censor. One night, he dreamed that Emperor Dezong summoned him to a private hall and asked him about matters of state. The emperor was pleased and bestowed a half-sleeved robe made of brocade on him. When he woke up, he found the dream strange and silently thought to himself, "The arm is a shelter. The large city shelters my body. Now I dream of a half-sleeved robe, is it not that the emperor has bestowed me with an honored position and will give me half of my salary, allowing me to retire from office?" He felt distressed for a long time. He then told his dream to others, and a guest explained, "Your dream is a sign of good fortune. And a half-sleeved robe is the garment of the limbs. Now you dream that the emperor bestows it on you, is it not that the emperor entrusts you with the position of a trusted minister?" The next day, he was indeed appointed as the Vice-Chancellor and Chancellor.

    82

    ○贞卢犹子
    　　太子宾客卢尚书贞犹子，为僧。会昌中，沙汰僧徒，斥归家，以荫补光王府参军。一夕，梦为僧时所奉师来慰，问其出处再三，告以佛氏沦破，已无所归，今为一官，徒遣旦夕，期再落顶上发，方毕志愿。且泣且诉之，良久曰：“若我志果遂，兴佛法。”语未竟，见八面屯兵，千乘万骑，旌旗日月，衣裳锦绣，仪卫四合，真天子大驾。军中人喧喧言“迎光王”。部整行列，以次前去。卢方骇愕不能测，遽惊觉。魂悸流汗，久之方能言。卒不敢泄于人。无几，宣宗自光邸践祚，录王府属吏。卢以例不拘常调格迁叙。自是，稍稍兴起释教寺宇僧尼旧制，一契梦中语。卢校梦中所谓本师，盖参军事府主近师弟子。故以为冥兆。岂神之意，以是微而显乎
    ○郑光
    　　淄青郑尚书光，会昌六年春，梦自御牛车，车中载瑞日，光烛天地，自执引，行通衢中。俄而惊寤，且奇叹。后月余，宣宗即位，以元舅之故，累拜尚书淄青节度，果契前梦
    ○张诜

    Explanation

    82

    ○贞卢犹子
    　　The loyal relative of Lu Shangshu Zhen, a guest of the Crown Prince, was a monk. During the Kai Cheng period, when the monks were purged and expelled, he was returned to his family and appointed to the position of Canjun in the Guang Wang's household through hereditary privilege. One night, he dreamed that his teacher, whom he had revered during his time as a monk, came to comfort him. The teacher asked repeatedly about his current situation, and he replied that Buddhism had been destroyed, leaving him with no place to return. Now, he held a minor official position, merely passing the days, hoping to shave his head again to finally fulfill his wishes. He wept and lamented, and after a long while, the teacher said, "If my wish is indeed fulfilled, I will revive Buddhism." Before the teacher could finish speaking, he saw eight armies encamped around him, thousands of chariots and horses, flags and banners like the sun and moon, and splendidly dressed attendants forming a grand procession, truly the imperial entourage. The soldiers were loudly proclaiming, "Welcome the Guang Wang!" The procession was orderly, moving forward in sequence.
