    Explanation

    122

    A Tale of Wu Ren Sheng

    Wu Ren Sheng, a man from Wu Prefecture, was skilled in seeing ghosts and lived in a thatched hut on Dongting Mountain. He always looked like a child, and the customs of Wu and Chu could not determine his age. During the Bao Li era, a former magistrate from Kunshan named Yang, who had settled in Wu Prefecture, was traveling with a few others by boat one day to visit Tiger Hill Temple. At the time, Wu Ren Sheng was on the boat, and they discussed matters concerning ghosts and spirits. Yang said, "Humans and ghosts have different paths, so ghosts cannot be seen." Wu Ren Sheng smiled and said, "There are many ghosts; it's just that people cannot recognize them. I alone can." Then he glanced at a woman dressed in green, holding a small child, walking along the shore. He pointed and said, "That is a ghost. The one she is holding is the living soul of an infant." Yang asked, "Then how can you tell it's a ghost?" Wu Ren Sheng replied, "Just watch me talk to her." He then shouted loudly, "You, you are a ghost, stealing the child of the living?" The woman heard and was frightened, quickly retreated, took a few steps, and suddenly disappeared. Yang was both amazed and astonished. By evening, they returned, several miles from the city, where a family had set up a feast by the riverbank. A female witch was dancing and drumming to her left, performing an offering to the gods. Yang and Wu Ren Sheng asked her about it. The witch said, "Today, someone in the village had a baby die suddenly, and now the baby has revived, so we are setting up a feast to express our gratitude." She then ordered the baby to be brought out to see, and it was indeed the true baby held by the woman. The guests were amazed, and they thanked Wu Ren Sheng, saying, "You are truly a master of the Dao; I did not know."

    123

    ○胡氵急
    　　安定胡氵急，家于河东郡，以文学知名。大和七年春登进士第。时贾餗为礼部侍郎。后二年，文宗皇帝擢餗相国事。是岁冬十月，京兆乱，餗与宰臣涯已下俱遁去。有诏捕甚急。时中贵人仇士良护左禁军，命部将执兵以穷其迹。部将谓士良曰：“胡氵急受贾工餗恩，今当匿在氵急所，愿骁健士五百，环其居以取之。”士良可其请。于是，部将拥兵至氵急门，召氵急出，厉声曰：“贾餗在君家，君宜立出。不然，与餗同罪。”氵急度其势，不可以理屈，抗辞拒之。部将怒，执氵急诣士良，戮于辕门之外。时氵急弟湘在河东郡，是日，湘及家人见一人，无首，衣绿衣，衣有血濡之迹，自门而入，步至庭。湘大恐，命家人逐之，遽不见。后三日，而氵急之凶闻至。知

    Explanation

    123

    Hu Ji, from Anding, lived in Hedong Prefecture and was renowned for his literary talent. In the spring of the seventh year of the Dazhong era (833 CE), he passed the imperial examination to become a Jinshi. At that time, Jia Fu served as the Vice Minister of Rites. Two years later, Emperor Wenzong promoted Jia Fu to the position of Chancellor. In the tenth month of that year, a disturbance erupted in the capital region, and Jia Fu, along with other ministers, fled. An edict was issued to capture them urgently. The influential eunuch Cao Shiliang, who protected the Left Guard, ordered his subordinates to take arms to track them down. The subordinate said to Cao Shiliang, "Hu Ji received favor from Jia Fu, and now he should be hiding at Hu Ji's place. I wish for five hundred brave and strong soldiers to surround his residence and capture him." Cao Shiliang approved his request. Then, the subordinate led troops to Hu Ji's door, called Hu Ji out, and spoke sternly, "Jia Fu is in your house; you should come out immediately. Otherwise, you will share the same punishment as Jia Fu." Hu Ji considered the situation and realized he could not argue logically, so he defiantly refused. The subordinate became angry and took Hu Ji to Cao Shiliang, where he was killed outside the main gate. At that time, Hu Ji's brother Xiang was in Hedong Prefecture. That day, Xiang and his family saw a person, headless, wearing green clothes with bloodstains, who entered through the door and walked into the courtyard. Xiang was greatly frightened, ordered his family to chase him, but he suddenly disappeared. Three days later, news of Hu Ji's death arrived.

    124

    ○辛神邕
    　　平卢従事御史辛神邕，太和五年冬，以前白水尉调集于京师。时有佣者刘万金与家僮自勤同室而居。自勤病数月，将死。一日，万金他出，自勤偃于榻。忽有一人，紫衣危冠广袂，貌枯形瘠，巨准修髯，自门而入。至榻前，谓自勤曰：“汝强起，疾当间矣。”于是扶自勤负壁而坐。先是，室之东垣下有食案，列数器。紫衣人探袖中，出一掬物，状若稻实而色青，即以十余粒置食器中。谓自勤曰：“吾非人间人，今奉命召万金，万金当食而死。食，尔勿泄吾语，不然，则祸及矣。”言讫遂去。是日，万金归，脸赤而喘，且曰：“我以腹虚热上，殆不可治。”即就其器而食，食且尽。自勤疾愈，万金果卒

    Explanation

    124

    Xin Shenyong, a subordinate official (従事) and censor (御史) of the Pinglu region, was assigned to the capital during the winter of the fifth year of the Tahe era (831 AD). He had previously served as the magistrate of Bai Shui County. At that time, there was a hired worker named Liu Wanjin and a household servant named Ziqin who lived in the same room. Ziqin had been ill for several months and was on the verge of death. One day, Liu Wanjin went out, and Ziqin lay down on his bed. Suddenly, a man appeared, dressed in purple, wearing a tall hat and broad sleeves, with a gaunt appearance, a large nose, and a long beard, entering through the door. He approached Ziqin's bed and said, "Rise up, your illness will soon improve." He then helped Ziqin sit against the wall. Before this, there was a food table under the eastern wall with several dishes. The purple-clad man reached into his sleeve, took out a handful of something that looked like green rice grains, and placed about ten grains into the food dish. He told Ziqin, "I am not from the human world; I have been sent to summon Liu Wanjin, who will die after eating. Do not reveal my words, or else you will suffer the same fate." After speaking, he left. That day, Liu Wanjin returned home, looking flushed and breathing heavily, and said, "I am suffering from abdominal heat, which is almost untreatable." He then ate from the dish until it was nearly finished. Ziqin recovered from his illness, and Liu Wanjin indeed died.

    125

    ○唐燕士
    　　晋昌唐燕士，好读书，隐于九华山。常日晚，天雨霁，燕士步月上山。夜既深，有群狼拥其道，不得归。惧既甚，遂匿于深林中。俄有白衣丈夫，戴纱巾，貌孤俊，年近五十，循涧而来，吟步自若，伫立且久，乃吟曰：
    　　“涧水潺潺声不绝，溪垅茫茫野花发
    　　自去自来人不知，归时唯对空山月。”燕士常好为七言诗，颇称于时人，闻此惊叹。将与之言，未及而没。明日，燕士归，以貌问里人，有识者曰：“是吴氏子，举进士，善为诗，卒数年矣。

    Explanation

    125

    ○Tang Yanshi
    Tang Yanshi, from Jinchang, loved reading and lived a reclusive life on Jiuhua Mountain. One evening, after the rain had cleared, Tang Yanshi walked up the mountain under the moonlight. As night deepened, a pack of wolves blocked his path, preventing him from returning home. Fearing greatly, he hid in the deep forest. Soon, a man in white appeared, wearing a silk scarf, with a solitary and handsome appearance, nearly fifty years old. He walked along the stream, reciting poetry calmly, and stood still for a long time before reciting:

    "Under the moon, the stream murmurs on,
    Across the fields, wildflowers bloom.
    Comes and goes, unseen by all,
    Returns alone, facing the moonlit mountain."

    Tang Yanshi, who often wrote seven-character poems and was well-regarded by his contemporaries, was amazed by this. He wanted to speak to the man but did not get the chance as the man disappeared. The next day, Tang Yanshi returned home and asked the locals about the man's appearance. Someone recognized him and said, "He was a son of the Wu family, who passed the imperial examination and was skilled in poetry. He died several years ago."

    126

    ○梁璟
    　　有梁璟者，开成中，自长沙将举孝廉，途次商山，舍于馆亭中。时八月十五夕，天雨新霁，风月高朗。璟偃而不寐。至夜半，忽见三丈夫，衣冠甚古，皆被珠绿，徐步而来。至庭中，且吟且赏，従者数人。璟心知其鬼也，然素有胆气，因降阶揖之。三人亦无惧色，自称萧中郎、王步兵、诸葛长史。即命席坐于庭中，曰：“不意良夜遇君于此。”因呼其童曰：“玉山取酒。”酒至，环席递酌。已而王步兵曰：“值此好风月，况佳宾在席，不可无诗也。”因举题联句，以咏秋物。步兵即首为之，曰：“秋月圆如镜。”萧中郎曰：“秋风利似刀。”璟曰：“秋云轻比絮。”次至诸葛长史，嘿然久之，二人促曰：“幸以拙速为事。”长史沈吟，又食顷，乃曰：“秋草细同毛。”二人皆大笑曰：“拙则拙矣，何乃迟乎？”长史曰：“此中郎过耳。为僻韵而滞捷才。”既而中郎又曰：“良会不可无侑酒佐欢。”命玉山召蕙娘来。玉山去。

    Explanation

    126

    A Tale of Liang Jing

    During the Kai Cheng period, there was a man named Liang Jing who, on his way from Changsha to take the examination for filial piety and integrity, stopped at an inn in Shangshan. It was the evening of the 15th day of the eighth month, and after a fresh rain, the wind and moonlight were clear and bright. Liang Jing lay down but did not sleep. At midnight, he suddenly saw three men with very ancient attire, all adorned with jade-green robes, walking slowly towards him. They reached the courtyard, where they paused to recite poetry and admire the scenery, accompanied by several attendants. Liang Jing knew they were spirits, but being naturally bold and courageous, he descended the steps to greet them. The three men showed no fear and introduced themselves as Xiao Zhonglang, Wang Bubing, and Zhuge Changshi. They immediately arranged seats in the courtyard and said, "We did not expect to meet you on such a fine night." They then called out to their attendant, "Yushan, bring the wine." When the wine arrived, they passed the cup around the table. Soon after, Wang Bubing said, "With such a beautiful night, and such distinguished guests present, we cannot go without composing some poetry." They began to write verses together, praising the autumn scenery. Wang Bubing started with, "The autumn moon is round like a mirror." Xiao Zhonglang followed with, "The autumn wind is sharp like a knife." Liang Jing added, "The autumn clouds are light as cotton." When it came to Zhuge Changshi, he remained silent for a long time. The other two urged him, "Please act quickly and simply." After pondering for a moment, Zhuge Changshi finally said, "The autumn grass is fine as hair." The other two burst into laughter, saying, "It may be simple, but why so slow?" Zhuge Changshi replied, "This is just Zhonglang's way. He gets stuck on obscure rhymes and delays his quick wit." Soon, Xiao Zhonglang said, "Such a gathering cannot lack wine to enhance the joy." He ordered Yushan to bring Hui Nü over. Yushan left to fetch her.

    127

    顷之，有一美人，鲜衣，自门步来，笑而拜坐客。诸葛长吏戏谓女郎曰：“自赴中郎召耳，与吾何事？”美人曰：“安知不为众人来？”步兵曰：“欲自明，无如歌以送长史酒。”蕙娘起曰：“愿歌《凤楼》之曲。”即歌之，清吟怨慕。璟听之忘倦。久而歌阕。中郎又歌。曲既终，曰：“山光渐明，愿更缀一篇以尽欢也。”即日：“山树高高影。”步兵曰：“山花寂寂香。”因指长史曰：“向者僻韵，信中郎过。今愿续此，以观捷才耳。”长史应曰：“山天遥历历。”一坐大笑：“迟不能巧速而且拙，捷才知是耶！”长史色不能平。次至璟，曰：“山水急汤汤。”中郎泛言赏之。乃问璟曰：“君非举进士者乎？”璟曰：“将举孝廉科。”中郎笑曰：“孝廉安知为诗哉！”璟因怒叱之。长史亦奋袂而起，坐客惊散，遂失所在，而杯盘亦亡见矣。璟自是被疾恍惚，往往梦中郎、步兵来，心甚恶之。后至长安，遇术士李生，辟鬼符佩之，遂绝也。知

    Explanation

    127

    After a while, a beautiful woman entered, dressed in fine clothes, and approached the guests with a smile, bowing to them. The high-ranking official, Zhuge Changli, playfully addressed the woman, saying, "You've come in response to the Zhonglang's summons; what business do you have with me?" The woman replied, "How can I be sure I'm not here for everyone?" The Bubing officer suggested, "To clarify yourself, there is no better way than to sing a song to accompany the wine of the Changshi." The woman named Huinian stood up and said, "I would like to sing the song 'The Song of the Phoenix Tower.'" She began to sing, her voice clear and melancholic. Jing listened to her without getting tired. After a while, the song ended. The Zhonglang then sang. When his song concluded, he said, "The mountain light is gradually brightening; I wish to compose another piece to prolong our enjoyment." He began, "The tall trees cast long shadows." The Bubing responded, "The quiet fragrance of mountain flowers." He then pointed to the Changshi and said, "The previous rhyme was awkward; indeed, it was a mistake by the Zhonglang. Now, I wish to continue this, to see who is more quick-witted." The Changshi replied, "The distant mountains and sky are clearly visible." Everyone laughed, saying, "Slow and unable to be quick, and also clumsy; is this the quick-witted one?" The Changshi's face could not remain calm. Next, Jing contributed, "The rushing water in the mountains." The Zhonglang generally praised the poem. He then asked Jing, "Aren't you someone who has taken the Jinshi examination?" Jing replied, "I am about to take the Xiaolian examination." The Zhonglang laughed and said, "How can a Xiaolian know about poetry?" Jing, angered, scolded him. The Changshi also stood up with raised sleeves, and the guests were startled and dispersed. They then disappeared, and the cups and plates also vanished. Jing then suffered from illness and became confused, often dreaming of the Zhonglang and the Bubing coming, and greatly disliked it. Later, he went to Chang'an and met a magician named Li, who gave him a charm to ward off ghosts, and it was finally cured.

    128

    ○崔御史
    　　广陵有官舍，地步数百，制置宏丽，里中传其中为鬼所宅，故居之者一夕则暴死，锁闭累年矣。有御史崔某，职于广陵，至，开门曰：“妖不自作。我新居之，岂能为灾耶！”即白廉使而居焉。是夕微雨，崔君命仆者尽居他室，而独寝于堂中。惕然而寤，衣尽沾湿，即起，见己之卧榻在庭中。却寝，未食顷，其榻又迁于庭。如是者三。崔曰：“我谓天下无鬼，今则果有矣。”即具簪笏，命酒，沃而祝曰：“吾闻居此者多暴死。且人神殊道，当自安其居，岂害生人耶虽苟以形见、以声闻者，是其负冤郁而将有诉者，或将求一饭以祭者，则见于人，而人自惊悸而死，固非神灵害之也。吾甚愚，且无畏惮。若真有所诉，直为我言，可以副汝托，虽汤火不避。”沃而祝者三。俄闻空中有言曰：“君，人也；我，鬼也。

    Explanation

    128

    The Imperial Censor Cui

    In Guangling, there was an official residence that spanned several hundred steps and was grandly constructed. In the neighborhood, it was said that a ghost inhabited it, and anyone who stayed there died suddenly the same night. It had been locked up for years. There was an imperial censor named Cui, who was stationed in Guangling. Upon arriving, he opened the door and said, "Evil does not arise on its own. If I move in, how could it bring disaster?" He immediately informed the governor and moved in. That night, there was a light rain. Cui ordered his servants to stay in other rooms and slept alone in the main hall. He woke up with a start, and his clothes were completely soaked. He got up and saw that his bed was in the courtyard. He went back to sleep, and before he had eaten, his bed moved again to the courtyard. This happened three times. Cui said, "I thought there were no ghosts in the world, but now I see that there are indeed." He then put on his official cap and robes, poured wine, and made a toast, saying, "I have heard that many who live here die suddenly. Moreover, humans and spirits follow different paths. They should stay in their own place and not harm living people. Even if they appear in form or make sounds, it is because they have grievances and are about to make a complaint, or they might be seeking a meal for a sacrifice. If they appear to people, people will be frightened and die. It is not that the spirits harm them. I am very foolish and have no fear. If they truly have something to complain about, just tell me. I can fulfill your trust, even if it means facing fire and water." He made this toast three times. Soon, he heard a voice from the air saying, "You are a human; I am a ghost."

    129

    诚不当以鬼干人，直将以深诚奉告。”崔曰：“但言之。”鬼曰：“我，女子也。女弟兄三人，俱未笄而殁，父母葬我于郡城之北久矣。其后府公于此峻城池，构城屋，工人伐我封内树且尽，又徙我于此堂之东北隅，羁魂不宁，无所栖托。不期今夕幸遇明君子，故我得以语其冤。傥君以仁心为我棺而葬于野，真恩之大者矣。”已而涕泣鸣咽，又曰：“我在此十年矣。前后所居者皆欲诉其事，自是居人惊悸而死。某儿女子，非有害于人也。”崔曰：“吾前言固如是矣。虽然，如何不见我耶！”鬼曰：“某鬼也，岂敢以幽晦之质而见君乎既诺我之请，虽处冥昧中，亦当感君子恩，岂可徒然而已。”言讫遂告去。明日，召工人，于堂东北隅发之，果得枯骸，葬于禅智寺隙地。里人皆祭之，谓之三女坟。自得其宅遂安。知

    Explanation

    129

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    It is not appropriate to disturb people with ghosts; I will instead sincerely inform you. Cui replied, "Just speak." The ghost said, "I am a woman. My three sisters and I, all unmarried, died young, and our parents buried us in the north of the prefectural city for a long time. Later, the prefect built high walls and city houses here, and workers cut down almost all the trees around our graves, then moved us to the northeast corner of this hall. Our restless souls had no place to rest. Unexpectedly, I met an enlightened gentleman tonight, so I can express my grievances. If you, with a benevolent heart, bury me in a coffin and lay me to rest in the wilderness, that would be a great kindness indeed." She then wept and sobbed, and added, "I have been here for ten years. Those who lived here before all wanted to report this matter, but since then, the residents have died in fright. I am just a young woman, and I do not harm anyone." Cui said, "I said before that I would do as such. However, why can't I see you?" The ghost replied, "I am a ghost, how dare I appear to you with my dark and obscure nature? Since you have promised my request, even if I am in a state of darkness, I should still be grateful for your kindness, how could I just leave it at that?" After saying this, she departed. The next day, Cui called the workers, dug up the northeast corner of the hall, and indeed found dry bones. He buried them in the vacant land of Chanzhi Temple. The villagers all worshipped there, calling it the Trio of Girls' Grave. Since then, the place became peaceful.

    130

    ○曹唐
    　　进士曹唐，以能诗名闻当世。久举不第，常寓居江陵佛寺中亭沼。境甚幽胜，每自临玩赋诗，得两句曰：“水底有天春漠漠，人间无路月茫茫。”吟之未久，自以为常制皆不及此作。一日，还坐亭沼上，方用怡咏，忽见二妇人，衣素衣，貌甚闲冶，徐步而吟，则唐前所作之二句也。唐自以制未翌日，人固未有知者，何遽而得之因迫而讯之，不应而去。未十余步间，不见矣。唐方甚疑怪，唐素与寺僧法舟善，因言于舟。舟惊曰：“两日前，有一少年见访，怀一碧笺，示我此诗，适方欲言之。”乃出示，唐颇惘然。数日后，唐卒于舍中。知

    Explanation

    130

    Poet Cao Tang, renowned for his poetry in his time, had long failed to pass the imperial examinations. He often resided in the garden of a Buddhist temple in Jiangling, where the scenery was very secluded and beautiful. Whenever he came here to enjoy the view and compose poetry, he would write two lines: "Underwater there is a sky, spring is vast and boundless; on earth there is no path, the moon is vast and mysterious." After reciting these lines for a while, he believed that his usual works were not as good as this one. One day, while sitting by the pond, he was immersed in the pleasure of reciting his poem, when suddenly he saw two women dressed in white clothes, very beautiful and graceful, walking slowly and reciting poetry. It was exactly the two lines he had written. Cao Tang, thinking that he had composed these lines only the previous day, wondered how they could have gotten hold of it so quickly. He asked them about it, but they did not respond and left. Within a dozen steps, they were no longer seen. Cao Tang was greatly puzzled and surprised. He had always been on good terms with the temple monk Fahzhou and told him about the incident. Fahzhou was startled and said, "Two days ago, a young man visited me, holding a greenish paper, and showed me this poem. I was just about to mention it." He then showed the poem to Cao Tang, who felt somewhat bewildered. A few days later, Cao Tang died in his room.

    131

    ○邢群
    　　刑部员外邢群，大中二年，以前歙州刺史居洛中，疾甚。群素与御史朱琯善，时琯自淮海従事罢居伊洛，病卒，而群未知。尝昼卧，忽闻扣门者，令视之，见琯骑而来，群即延入坐。先是，群闻琯病，及见来，甚喜，曰：“向闻君疾，亦无足忧。”琯曰：“某尝病，今则愈矣。然君之疾，亦无足忧，不一二日，当间耳。”言笑久之，方去。琯访群之时，乃琯卒也

    Explanation

    131

    Xing Qun, an official in the Ministry of Justice, lived in Luoyang in the second year of the Dazhong era, suffering from a severe illness. He had always been on good terms with Censor Zhu Guan, who had served as an official in Huaihai and was now retired to Yiluo, where he had died of illness, though Xing Qun did not know this. One day, while resting during the day, Xing Qun suddenly heard someone knocking on the door. He ordered someone to look, and saw Zhu Guan riding his horse. Xing Qun immediately invited him in to sit. Previously, Xing Qun had heard that Zhu Guan was ill, and when he saw him come, he was very pleased, saying, "I heard you were ill before, but there's no need to worry." Zhu Guan replied, "I was once ill, but I have recovered now. Your illness is also nothing to worry about; it will be fine in a day or two." They talked and laughed for a while, and then Zhu Guan left. When Zhu Guan came to visit Xing Qun, he had already died.

    132

    ○李重
    　　太中五年，检校郎中知盐铁河阴院事李重罢职，居河东群。被疾，旬日益甚，沈然在榻。一夕，告其仆曰：“我病不起矣。”即令扃键其门。忽闻庭中窣然有声，重视之，见一人衣绯，乃河西令蔡行己也。又有一人，衣白叠衣，在其后。重与行己善，即惊曰：“蔡侍御来。”因命延上，与白衣者俱坐。倾之，见行己身渐长，手足口鼻，亦随而大焉。细视之，乃非己行也。重心异之，然因以侍御呼焉。重遂觉身稍可举，即负壁而坐，问曰：“某病旬月矣，今愈甚，得不中于此乎？”其人曰：“君之疾当间矣。”即指白衣者：“吾之季弟，善卜。

    Explanation

    132

    In the fifth year of the Tai Zhong era, Li Zhong, the Inspector of the Salt and Iron Department at the Yellow River Institute, was dismissed from office and lived in the region east of the Yellow River. He suffered from an illness that worsened day by day, and he lay quietly on his bed. One night, he told his servant, "I will not recover from this illness." He then ordered the door to be locked. Suddenly, he heard a subtle sound in the courtyard. Li Zhong looked up and saw a man wearing red, who was none other than Cai Xingji, the magistrate of the region west of the Yellow River. There was another man, wearing white layered clothing, following him. Li Zhong was familiar with Cai Xingji and was startled, exclaiming, "Here comes Cai the Imperial Censor!" He then invited them to sit down, and both men sat together. Soon, Li Zhong noticed that Cai Xingji's body gradually grew larger, and his hands, feet, mouth, and nose also enlarged. Upon closer inspection, he realized that this was not Cai Xingji himself. Li Zhong was amazed but continued to address him as the Imperial Censor. Li Zhong then felt that he could slightly move his body and leaned against the wall to sit. He asked, "I have been ill for a month now, and it has worsened. Could it be that I will not recover?" The man replied, "Your illness should soon subside." He then pointed to the man in white clothing and said, "He is my youngest brother, skilled in divination."

    133

    ”乃命卜重。白衣者于中出一小木猿，置榻上，既而其猿左右跳踯，数四而定。白衣者曰：“卦成矣。郎中之病，固无足忧，当至六十二，然亦有灾。”重曰：“侍御饮酒乎？”曰：“安敢不饮。”重遂命酒，以杯置于前。朱衣者曰：“吾自有饮器。”乃于衣中出一杯，初似银，及既酌，而其杯翻翻不定，细视乃纸为者。二人各尽二杯，已而收其杯于衣中。将去，又诫重曰：“君愈之后，慎无饮酒，祸且及矣。”重谢而诺之。良久遂去。至庭中，乃无所见。视其外门，扃键如旧。又见其榻前，酒在地，盖二鬼所饮也。重自是病愈。既而饮酒如初，其年，谪为杭州司马

    Explanation

    133

    Then he ordered the divination for Zhong. The white-clad one emerged from the center with a small wooden monkey, placed it on the couch, and then the monkey jumped around left and right, several times before settling. The white-clad one said, "The divination is complete. The illness of the medical officer is indeed not something to worry about, but he will reach sixty-two, and there will also be disaster." Zhong asked, "Shall the imperial physician drink?" The white-clad one replied, "How dare I not drink?" Zhong then ordered wine and placed the cup in front of him. The red-clad one said, "I have my own drinking vessel." He then took out a cup from his clothing, which at first seemed like silver, but when filled, the cup wobbled, and upon closer inspection, it was made of paper. The two of them finished two cups each. As they were leaving, they warned Zhong again, "After you recover, be careful not to drink alcohol, for disaster will follow." Zhong thanked them and promised to comply. After a long time, they left. In the courtyard, there was nothing to be seen. Looking at the outer door, it was locked as before. He also saw that there was wine on the floor in front of the couch, which the two ghosts had drunk. From then on, Zhong recovered from his illness. Later, he drank as he used to. That year, he was demoted to the position of Prefect of Hangzhou.

    134

    ○王坤
    　　太原王坤，大中四年春为国子博士。有婢轻云，卒数年矣。一夕，忽梦轻云至榻前，坤甚惧，起而讯之。轻云曰：“某自不为人数年矣，尝念平生时，若系而不忘解也。今夕得奉左右，亦幸会耳。”坤懵然若醉，不寤为鬼也。轻云即引坤出门，门已扃钥，隙中导坤而过，曾无碍。行至衢中，步月徘徊久之。坤忽饥，语于轻云，轻云曰：“里中人有与郎善者乎可以诣而求食也。”坤素与太学博士石贯善，又同里居，坤因与偕行至贯门，而门已键闭。轻云叩之，有顷，阍者启扉曰：“向闻扣门，今寂无睹，何也？”因阖扉。轻云又扣之。如是者三。阍者怒曰：“厉鬼安得辄扣吾门！”且唾且骂之。轻白坤云：“石生已寝，固不可诣矣。愿郎更诣他所。”时有国子监小吏，亦同里，每出，常经其门，吏与主月俸及条报除授，坤甚委信之。因与俱至其家，方见启扉，有一人持水缶，注于衢中。

    Explanation

    134

    ○Wang Kun
    In the spring of the fourth year of Da Zhong (850 CE), Wang Kun, a Doctor of the Imperial Academy, was residing in Taiyuan. He had a servant girl named Qingyun who had passed away several years ago. One night, Qingyun suddenly appeared at his bedside. Wang Kun was greatly frightened and asked her about her appearance. Qingyun said, "I have not been among the living for several years now. I often remember my past, and I have not completely forgotten it. Tonight, I am able to visit you, which is a fortunate encounter." Wang Kun was dazed and did not realize that she was a ghost. Qingyun led Wang Kun out of the room. The door was already locked, but she guided him through a gap, and he passed without any hindrance. They walked down the street, strolling under the moonlight for a long time. Wang Kun suddenly felt hungry and told Qingyun. Qingyun said, "Is there anyone in the village who is friendly with you? You can go to their house to find food." Wang Kun had always been close friends with a scholar named Shi Guan, who also lived in the same village. He decided to go to Shi Guan's house. However, when they arrived, the door was locked. Qingyun knocked on the door, but after a while, the gatekeeper opened it and said, "I just heard the knocking, but now I see nothing. What is going on?" He then closed the door. Qingyun knocked again. This happened three times. The gatekeeper became angry and said, "How dare a ghost knock on my door!" He spat and cursed at her. Qingyun explained to Wang Kun, "Shi Guan is already asleep, so we cannot visit him. I suggest you go to another place." At that moment, a minor official from the Imperial Academy, who was also from the same village, was passing by. Wang Kun and Qingyun went to his house. The door was open, and they saw someone pouring water from a container into the street.

    135

    轻云曰：“可偕入。”既入，见小吏与数人会食。初，坤立于庭，以为小吏必降阶迎拜，既而小吏不礼。俄见一婢捧汤饼登阶，轻云即殴婢背，遽仆于阶，汤饼尽覆。小吏与妻奴俱起，惊曰：“中恶。”即急召巫者。巫曰：“有一人朱绂银印，立于庭前。”因祭之。坤与轻云俱就坐，食已而偕去。女巫送至门，焚纸钱于门侧。轻云谓坤曰：“郎可偕某而行。”坤即随出里中，望启夏而去。至郊野数十里，见一墓，轻云曰：“此妾所居，郎可随而入焉。”坤即挽首曲躬而入，墓口曛黑不可辨。忽悸然惊寤，背汗股怵。时天已晓，心恶其梦，不敢语于人。是日，因召石贯，既坐，贯曰：“昨夕有鬼扣吾门者三，遣视之，寂无所睹。”至晓，过小吏，则有焚纸钱迹，即立召小吏讯其事。小吏曰：“某昨夕方会食，忽有婢中恶，巫云‘鬼为崇’。由是设祭于庭，焚纸于此！”尽与坤梦同。坤益惧，因告妻孥。是岁冬，果卒。知

    Explanation

    135

    Light Cloud said, "You may enter with me." After they entered, they saw the small official dining with several others. Initially, Kun stood in the courtyard, thinking that the small official would certainly descend the steps to greet and bow, but the small official did not show respect. Soon, a servant girl carrying a bowl of soup and bread climbed the steps, and Light Cloud immediately struck the girl's back, causing her to suddenly fall on the steps, spilling the soup and bread. The small official and his wife and servants all stood up,惊呼道：“中了邪！”随即急忙召来巫师。巫师说：“有一人穿着红绶带和银印，站在庭院前。”于是进行了祭祀。Kun and Light Cloud both sat down, and after finishing the meal, they left together. The female witch accompanied them to the door, burning paper money at the side of the door. Light Cloud told Kun, "Sir, you can go with me." Kun followed her out of the neighborhood, heading towards Qixia. They reached a remote area of the wilderness, and saw a tomb. Light Cloud said, "This is where I reside. Sir, you can follow me inside." Kun bowed his head and entered the tomb, which was dark and indistinguishable at the entrance. Suddenly, he woke up in a state of fear, sweating on his back and trembling in his thighs. By then, it was already dawn, and he disliked the dream, not daring to tell anyone about it. That day, he summoned Shi Guan, who sat down, and Shi Guan said, "Last night, a ghost knocked on my door three times. I sent someone to look, but nothing was seen." In the morning, when passing by the small official's house, there were traces of burned paper money. He immediately summoned the small official to inquire about the matter. The small official said, "I was having a meal last night when suddenly a servant girl fell ill. The witch said it was due to a ghost causing trouble. Therefore, we set up a sacrifice in the courtyard and burned paper money here!" Everything matched Kun's dream. Kun became even more frightened and informed his family. That winter, he indeed died.

    136

    ○杨慎矜
    　　开元中，杨慎矜为御史中丞。一日，将入朝，家童开其外门。既启锁，其门噤不可解。慎矜且惊且异。洎天将晓，其导従吏自外见慎矜门有夜叉，长丈余，状极异，立于宇下，以左右手噤其门，火吻电眸，盼顾左右。従吏见之，惧惊怵四去。久而衢中舆马人物稍多，其夜叉方南向而去。行者见之，咸辟易仆地。慎矜闻其事，惧甚。后月余，遂为李林甫所诬，弟兄皆诛死
    ○江南吴生
    　　有吴生者，江南人，尝游会稽，娶一刘氏女为妾。后数年，吴生宰县于雁门郡，与刘氏偕之官。刘氏初以柔婉闻，凡数年，其后忽旷烈自恃，不可禁。往往有逆意者，即发怒。殴其婢仆，或啮其肌，血且甚，而怒不可解。吴生始知刘氏悍戾，心稍外之。尝一日，吴与雁门部将数辈猎于野，获狐兔甚多，致庖舍下。明日，吴生出，刘氏即潜入庖舍，取狐兔，生啖之且尽。吴生归，因诘狐兔所在，而刘氏俛然不语。

    Explanation

    136

    ○杨慎矜
    During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, Yang Shenjing served as the Vice President of the Censorate. One day, as he was about to enter the court, his servant opened the outer gate. After unlocking it, the door was tightly sealed and could not be opened. Yang Shenjing was both surprised and puzzled. By dawn, his attendants saw a night demon standing outside his door, about ten feet tall and extremely unusual in appearance. The night demon stood under the eaves, locking the door with its hands, and had a flaming mouth and lightning eyes, glancing left and right. The attendants, frightened and scattered, fled in all directions. After some time, as more people and carriages filled the street, the night demon turned south and departed. Passersby, seeing this, scattered and fell to the ground in fear. Yang Shenjing heard about this event and was greatly alarmed. A month later, he was falsely accused by Li Linfu and his brothers were all executed.

    ○江南吴生
    There was a man named Wu from Jiangnan who once traveled to Kuaiji and married a woman named Liu as a concubine. Several years later, Wu became the magistrate of Yanmen County and took Liu with him to his new post. Initially, Liu was known for her gentleness and submission. However, after several years, she suddenly became reckless and self-willed, beyond control. Whenever someone went against her will, she would become furious. She would beat her maids and servants, sometimes biting their flesh until it bled, and her anger could not be appeased. Wu began to realize that Liu was violent and domineering, and he started to distance himself from her. One day, Wu and several generals from Yanmen went hunting in the wild, capturing many foxes and rabbits, which they brought back to the kitchen. The next day, when Wu returned, Liu secretly entered the kitchen, took the foxes and rabbits, and ate them all raw. Wu asked where the foxes and rabbits were, and Liu remained silent and dejected.

    137

    吴生怒，讯其婢，婢曰：“刘氏食之尽矣。”生始疑刘氏为他怪。旬余，有县吏以一鹿献，吴生命致于庭。已而吴生始言将远适。既出门，即匿身潜伺之。见刘氏散发袒肱，眦皆尽裂，状貌顿异。立庭中，左手执鹿，右手拔其髀而食之。吴生大惧，仆地不能起久之。乃召吏卒十数辈，持兵仗而入。刘氏见吴生来，尽去襦袖，挺然立庭，乃一夜叉耳。目若电光，齿如戟刃，筋骨盘蹙，身尽青色。吏卒俱战怵不敢近。而夜叉四顾，若有所惧。仅食顷，忽东向而走，其势甚疾，竟不知所在
    ○朱岘女
    　　武陵郡有浮屠祠，其高数百寻，下瞰大江。每江水泛扬，则浮屠势若摇动。故里人无敢登其上者。有贾人朱岘，家极赡，有一女，无何失所在。其家寻之，仅旬余，莫穷其适。一日，天雨霁，郡民望见浮屠之颠若有人立者，隐然纹缬衣，郡民且以为他怪。岘闻之，即往观焉。望其衣装，甚类其女。即命人登其上取之，果见女也。岘惊讯其事，女曰：

    Explanation

    137

    Wu was enraged and questioned his maid, who replied, "Liu ate it all." Wu began to suspect that Liu was some kind of monster. A few days later, a county official presented a deer, and Wu ordered it brought to the courtyard. Soon after, Wu started to say he would be going far away. After leaving the house, he hid himself and secretly watched. He saw Liu with loose hair and bare arms, her eyes all torn open, and her appearance suddenly changed. Standing in the courtyard, she held the deer with her left hand and pulled from its thigh to eat with her right. Wu was greatly frightened, fell to the ground, and could not get up for a long time. Then he called for several dozen officials and soldiers, armed with weapons, to enter. When Liu saw Wu coming, she removed her outer garments, stood upright in the courtyard, and transformed into a night fork. Her eyes were like electric light, her teeth like spear blades, her muscles and bones twisted, and her body entirely blue. All the officials and soldiers were terrified and dared not approach. The night fork looked around, as if afraid of something. After a meal, she suddenly turned east and ran, very fast, and eventually disappeared.

    ○ Zhu Xian's Daughter
    There was a pagoda temple in Wuling Commandery, towering hundreds of feet high, overlooking the great river below. Whenever the river waters surged, the pagoda seemed to sway. Thus, no one dared to climb to the top. There was a merchant named Zhu Xian, whose family was extremely wealthy, and he had a daughter who suddenly disappeared without a trace. Her family searched for her, but after a few days, they could not find out where she had gone. One day, after the rain stopped, the townspeople saw someone standing at the top of the pagoda, wearing a patterned robe. They thought it was some kind of monster. Zhu Xian heard about it and went to investigate. Upon seeing her attire, he realized it was very similar to his daughter's. He immediately ordered someone to climb up and retrieve her. Indeed, it was his daughter. Zhu Xian was shocked and questioned her, to which she replied, ...

    138

    “某向者独处，有夜叉，长丈余，甚诡异，自屋上跃而下，入某之室，谓某曰：‘无惧我也。’即揽衣驰去，至浮屠上。既而兀兀然，若甚醉者。凡数日方稍寤，因惧且甚。其夜叉率以将晓则下浮屠，行里中取食饮某。一日，夜叉方去，某下视之，见其行里中，会遇一白衣。夜叉见，辟易退远百步，不敢窃视。及暮归，某因诘之：‘何为惧白衣者乎’夜叉曰：‘向者白衣自小不食太牢，故我不得近也。’某问何故，夜叉曰：‘牛者，所以耕田畴，为生人之本。人不食某肉，则上帝佑之。故我不得而近也。’某默念曰：‘吾人也，去父母与异类为伍，可不悲乎’明日，夜叉去，而祝曰：‘某愿不以太牢为食。’凡三祝，其夜叉忽自郡中来至浮屠下，望某而语曰：‘何为有异志而弃我乎使我终不得近子矣。従此别去。’词毕，即东向走，而竟不知其所往。某喜甚，由浮屠中得以归。”主

    Explanation

    138

    Once, when I was alone, a nightmarish creature, about 10 feet tall and very eerie, leaped from the roof and entered my room. It told me not to be afraid. I quickly put on my clothes and ran to a pagoda. Afterward, I felt dazed and as if drunk. It took several days for me to gradually recover. Because of my fear, I was even more terrified.

    The nightmarish creature usually came down from the pagoda in the early morning to get food and drink for me. One day, as the nightmarish creature was leaving, I looked down and saw it walking through the streets, where it encountered a white-robed figure. The nightmarish creature backed away 100 steps and dared not look at the white-robed figure. When it returned at dusk, I asked it, "Why are you afraid of the white-robed figure?" The nightmarish creature replied, "In the past, the white-robed figure has never eaten the meat of cattle, so I cannot approach." I asked why, and the nightmarish creature explained, "Cattle are used for farming and are essential for human life. If people do not eat their meat, they are protected by the gods. Therefore, I cannot approach."

    I silently reflected, "I am human, and it is sad to associate with creatures of a different kind." The next day, as the nightmarish creature was leaving, I vowed, "I will not eat the meat of cattle." I repeated this vow three times. Then, the nightmarish creature suddenly appeared from the city, came to the pagoda, and spoke to me, "Why have you changed your mind and abandoned me? Now I will never be able to approach you. From now on, we must part ways." After finishing speaking, the nightmarish creature walked east and disappeared without a trace. I was very happy and returned home via the pagoda.

    139

    ○陈越石
    　　颖州陈越石，初名黄石，郊居于王屋山下，有妾张氏者。元和中，越石与张氏俱夜食，忽闻烛影后有呼吸之声，甚异。已而出一手至越石前。其手青黑色，指短，爪甲纤长，有黄毛连臂，似乞食之状。越石深知其怪，恶而且惧。久之，闻烛影下有语：“我病饥，故来奉谒。愿以少肉致掌中。幸无见阻。”越石即以少肉投于地，其手即取之而去。又曰：“此肉味甚美。”食讫，又出手越石前。越石怒骂曰：“妖鬼何为辄来宜疾去，不然，且击之，得无悔耶！”其手即引去，若有所惧。俄顷，又出其手至张氏前，谓张曰：“女郎能以少肉见惠乎？”越石谓张氏曰：“慎无与！”张氏竟不与。

    Explanation

    139

    Tchen Yue Shi, from Yingzhou, originally named Huang Shi, lived in a suburban area near Mount Wangwu. He had a concubine named Zhang. During the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang, in the year 806 AD, Yue Shi and Zhang were eating together at night when they suddenly heard a breathing sound behind the candlelight, which was very unusual. Soon after, a hand appeared in front of Yue Shi. The hand was bluish-black in color, with short fingers and long, slender claws. There were yellow hairs connected to the arm, resembling the appearance of someone begging for food. Yue Shi deeply knew that this was strange, and he felt both disgust and fear. After a while, he heard a voice from behind the candlelight say, "I am hungry, so I have come to pay my respects. I wish to have a little meat placed in my hand. Please do not stop me." Yue Shi immediately threw a small piece of meat on the ground, and the hand took it and left. The hand then said, "This meat tastes very delicious." After finishing the meat, the hand appeared again in front of Yue Shi. Yue Shi angrily shouted, "What are you doing here, you demon? Leave quickly, or I will hit you. Will you not regret it?" The hand then retreated, as if it was afraid. A short while later, the hand appeared again in front of Zhang and said to her, "Madam, can you give me a little meat?" Yue Shi told Zhang, "Be careful not to give it to her!" Zhang refused to give any meat.

    140

    久之，忽于烛影旁出一面，乃一夜叉也。赤发蓬然，两目如电，四牙若锋刃之状，甚可惧。以手击张氏，遽仆于地，冥然不能动。越石有胆勇，即起而逐之，夜叉遂走，不敢回视。明日，穷其迹。于垣上有过踪。越石曰：“此物今将再来矣。”于是至夜持杖立东北垣下以伺之。仅食顷，夜叉果来。既逾墙，足未及地，越石即以杖连击数十。及夜叉去，以烛视其垣下，血甚多，有皮尺余，亦在地，盖击而堕者。自是张氏病愈。至夕，闻数里外有呼者曰：“陈黄石何为不归我皮也？”连呼不止。仅月余，每夕尝闻呼声。越石度不可禁，且恶其见呼，于是迁居以避之。因改名越石。元和十五年，登第进士。至会昌二年，卒于蓝田令。知

    Explanation

    140

    After a long time, suddenly a face appeared beside the candlelight, and it turned out to be a yaksha. The yaksha had red, unkempt hair, eyes like lightning, and four teeth resembling sharp blades, which was extremely frightening. It struck Zhang with its hand, causing him to fall to the ground immediately and become unconscious. Yue Shi, who had courage and bravery, immediately got up and chased it. The yaksha then fled, not daring to look back. The next day, Yue Shi traced its trail and found traces on the wall. He said, "This creature will come again." Therefore, at night, he stood with a staff by the northeast wall to wait for it. Shortly after a meal's time, the yaksha indeed came. Having jumped over the wall, before its feet touched the ground, Yue Shi immediately struck it with his staff dozens of times. When the yaksha left, Yue Shi looked under the wall with a candle and saw a lot of blood. There was also a piece of skin over a foot long, on the ground, which had fallen off from being struck. From then on, Zhang recovered from his illness. At dusk, he heard someone calling from several miles away, saying, "Why hasn't Chen Huangshi given back my skin?" The calling kept going without stopping. Only about a month later, every night, he often heard the calls. Yue Shi thought he could not prevent it and disliked being called out. So, he moved to a new residence to avoid it. He therefore changed his name to Yue Shi. In the 15th year of Yuanhe, he passed the imperial examination as a Jinshi. In the 2nd year of Huichang, he died as the magistrate of Lantian.

    141

    ○郑氏女
    　　通州有王居士者，有道术。会昌中，刺史郑君有幼女，甚念之，而自幼多疾，若神魂不足者。郑君因请居士，居士曰：“此女非疾，乃生魂未归其身。”郑君讯其事，居士曰：“某县令某者，即此女前身也。当死数岁矣，以平生为善，以幽冥佑之得过期。今年九十余矣。今殁之日，此女当愈。”郑君急发人驰访之，其令果九十余矣。后月，其女忽若醉寤，疾愈。郑君又使往验，令果以女疾愈之日无疾卒

    Explanation

    141

    A Girl from the Zheng Family

    In Tongzhou, there was a recluse named Wang who possessed supernatural abilities. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong (841-846), the local governor of Tongzhou, Viceroy Zheng, had a young daughter whom he greatly cherished. She had been ill since childhood, as if her spirit was lacking. Viceroy Zheng then invited Wang to inquire about her condition. Wang said, "This girl is not suffering from an illness, but rather her soul has not returned to her body." Viceroy Zheng asked him to explain further. Wang replied, "A certain magistrate from a certain county, who is the previous incarnation of this girl, should have died several years ago. Due to his lifetime of good deeds, he was protected by the supernatural and thus lived longer than expected. He is now over ninety years old. On the day he passes away, this girl will recover." Viceroy Zheng immediately sent someone to verify this. Indeed, the magistrate was over ninety years old. A month later, the girl suddenly woke up as if from a drunken stupor, and her illness was cured. Viceroy Zheng then sent someone to verify again, and it was confirmed that the magistrate indeed died without illness on the day the girl was cured.

    142

    ○庐江民
    　　贞元中，有庐江郡民，因采樵至山。会日暮，忽见一胡人，长丈余，自山崦中出，衣黑衣，执弓矢。民大恐，遽走匿古木中窥之。胡人伫望良久，忽东向发一矢。民随望之，见百步外有一物，状类人，举体黄毛数寸，蒙乌巾而立，矢中其腹，辄不动。胡人笑曰：“果非吾所及。”遂去。又一胡，亦长丈余，魁伟愈于前者，亦执弧矢，东望而射。中其物之胸，亦不动。胡人又曰：“非将军不可。”又去。俄有胡人数十，衣黑，臂弓腰矢，若前驱者。又见一巨人，长数丈，被紫衣，状貌极异，缓步而来。民见之，不觉懔然。巨胡东望，谓其前驱者曰：“射其喉。

    Explanation

    142

    During the reign of Emperor Dezong, a villager from the Lujiang region was gathering firewood in the mountains. As dusk fell, he suddenly saw a tramp over ten feet tall emerge from a mountain ravine, wearing black clothes and holding a bow and arrow. The villager was greatly frightened and rushed to hide behind an ancient tree, peering out to observe. The tramp stood and gazed for a long time, then suddenly shot an arrow eastward. The villager followed his gaze and saw an object a hundred paces away that looked like a person, covered in several inches of yellow fur, wearing a black headscarf, and standing still. The arrow hit its abdomen, but it did not move. The tramp laughed and said, "Indeed, it is beyond my ability." He then left. Another tramp, also over ten feet tall and even more robust than the first, also held a bow and arrow and looked eastward, shooting. The arrow hit the object's chest, but it still did not move. The tramp said, "Only a general can do it." He then left. Soon, dozens of tramps in black clothes, with bows on their arms and arrows in their belts, arrived, acting as vanguard. Then, a giant several dozen feet tall, wearing purple clothing and with an extremely strange appearance, slowly approached. The villager saw him and unconsciously felt a chill. The giant tramp looked eastward and told the vanguard, "Shoot at its throat."

    143

    ”群胡欲争射之，巨胡诫曰：“非雄舒莫可。”他胡皆退。有一胡前，引满一发，遂中其喉。其物亦不惧，徐以手拔去三矢，持一巨砾西向而来。胡人皆有惧色，前白巨胡：“事迫矣。不如降之。”巨胡即命呼曰：“将军愿降。”其物乃投砾于地，自去其巾，状如妇人，无发。至群胡前，尽收夺所执弓矢，皆折之。遂令巨胡跪于地，以手连掌其颊。胡人哀祈，称死者数四，方释之。诸胡高拱而立，不敢辄动。其物徐以巾蒙首，东望而去。胡人相贺曰：“赖今日甲子耳。不然，吾辈其死乎！”既而俱拜于巨胡前，巨胡颔之，良久，遂导而入山崦，时欲昏黑。民雨汗而归，竟不知其何物也

    Explanation

    143

    A group of Huns wanted to compete to shoot it, and the great Hun warned, saying, "Only Xiong Shu is suitable." The other Huns all stepped back. One Hun approached, drew the bow fully and shot once, and hit its throat. The creature was also not afraid, and slowly pulled out three arrows with its hand, carrying a large stone and coming from the west. The Huns all showed signs of fear, and approached the great Hun, saying, "The situation is urgent. It would be better to surrender to it." The great Hun immediately commanded and called out, "General wishes to surrender." The creature then threw the stone on the ground, removed its own headscarf, and looked like a woman, without hair. It approached the group of Huns, completely took away all the bows and arrows they were holding, and broke them all. Then it ordered the great Hun to kneel on the ground, and struck his cheeks with his hands repeatedly. The Huns begged in a pitiful manner, calling out the number of dead several times, only then released him. The other Huns stood with their hands raised high, and were afraid to move. The creature slowly covered its head with the scarf, looked east, and left. The Huns congratulated each other, saying, "Thankfully today is the day of Jiazi. Otherwise, we would all be dead!" Afterward, they all knelt before the great Hun, and the great Hun nodded. For a long time, then led them into the mountain pass, it was almost dark. The people returned sweating, finally, they did not know what it was.

    144

    ○谢翱
    　　陈郡谢翱者，尝举进士，好为七字诗。其先寓居长安升道里，所居庭中多牡丹。一日晚霁，出其居，南行百步，眺终南峰。伫立久之，见一骑自西驰来，绣缋仿佛，近乃双鬟，高髻靓妆，色甚姝丽。至翱所，因驻谓翱：“郎非见待耶！”翱曰：“步此，徒望山耳。”双鬟笑降，拜曰：“愿郎归所居。”翱不测，即回望其居，见青衣三四人偕立其门外，翱益骇异。入门，青衣俱前拜。既入。见堂中设茵毯，张帷帟，锦绣辉映，异香遍室。翱愕然且惧，不敢问。

    144

    ○谢翱
    　　谢翱，陈郡人，曾经参加过进士考试，喜欢写七言诗。他先祖曾暂时居住在长安升道里，他家的院子里种了许多牡丹。一天傍晚雨后天晴，谢翱走出家门，向南走了百步，远眺终南山的山峰。他久久站立在那里，忽然看见一匹马从西边飞驰而来，马上的女子身着绣花衣服，近看原来是两个鬟髻，高高的发髻，妆容美丽，容貌非常娇艳。那女子来到谢翱面前，停下马说道：“郎君不是来等我吗？”谢翱回答说：“我只是来散步，顺便看看山景而已。”那女子笑着下马，向谢翱行礼说：“希望郎君能回你的住所。”谢翱感到非常困惑，立刻回头望向自己的家，只见几个穿着青衣的人站在门外，谢翱更加惊讶和疑惑。他走进屋内，那些青衣人都上前向他行礼。进入屋内后，只见厅堂中铺设了坐垫和地毯，悬挂着帷幔，锦绣交相辉映，异香弥漫整个房间。谢翱感到非常惊讶和害怕，不敢多问。

    145

    一人前曰：“郎何惧固不为损耳。”顷之，有金车至门。见一美人，年十六七，风貌闲丽，代所未识。降车入门，与翱相见。坐于西轩，谓翱曰：“闻此地有名花，故来与君一醉耳。”翱惧稍解。美人即命设馔同食，其器用物，莫不珍丰。出玉杯，命酒递酌。翱因问曰：“女郎何为者得不为他怪乎？”美人笑不答。固请之，乃曰：“君但知非人则已，安用问耶！”夜阑，谓翱曰：“某家甚远，今将归，不可久留此矣！闻君善为七言诗，愿有所赠。”翱怅然，因命笔赋诗曰：知

    Explanation

    145

    A man stepped forward and said, "Why are you afraid? It won’t harm you." Soon after, a golden carriage arrived at the door. A beautiful woman, about sixteen or seventeen years old, with an elegant and graceful appearance, was seen. She had never been seen before. She got out of the carriage and entered the house, meeting with Aow. They sat in the western pavilion, and she said to Aow, "I heard that there are famous flowers here, so I came to drink with you." Aow's fear gradually eased. The beautiful woman then ordered food to be prepared and ate together. All the utensils and objects were precious and abundant. She took out a jade cup, poured wine, and passed it to drink. Aow then asked, "What kind of person are you? Are you not some kind of strange being?" The beautiful woman smiled and did not answer. Aow persisted in asking her, and she replied, "You just need to know that I am not a human; why ask further?" Late at night, she said to Aow, "My home is very far away, I am going back now, and cannot stay here for long! I heard that you are good at writing seven-character poems, I wish to have one as a gift." Aow felt sad and then wrote a poem, saying:

    146

    “阳台后会杳无期，碧树烟深玉漏迟
    　　半夜香风满庭月，花前竟发楚王悲。”美人览之，泣下数行，曰：“某亦尝学为诗，欲答来赠，幸不见诮。”翱喜而请。美人求绛笺，翱视笥中，唯碧笺一幅，因与之。美人题曰：知
    　　“相思无路莫相思，风里花开只片时
    　　惆怅金闺却归处，晓莺啼断绿杨枝。
    　　其笔札甚工，翱嗟赏良久。美人遂顾左右撒帐帟，命烛登车。翱送至门，挥泪而别。未数十步，车与人马俱亡见矣。翱异其事，因贮美人诗笥中。知

    Explanation

    146

    "The reunion on the Yangtai Terrace is uncertain when, amidst deep green trees and dense mist, the jade-drip clock moves slowly.
    At midnight, a fragrant breeze fills the courtyard under the moonlight, and in front of the flowers, the sorrow of King Chu is unexpectedly evoked." The lady read it, and tears streamed down her face. She said, "I have also learned to write poetry and wish to compose a reply, hoping you will not find fault with it." Aro was pleased and asked her to proceed. The lady requested a red writing sheet, and Aro looked in his box, finding only a green one, so he gave it to her. The lady wrote:
    "There is no way to express longing, so do not long; flowers bloom in the wind, but only for a moment.
    Lamenting the place of return in the golden palace, the morning oriole's song breaks the green willow branches."
    Her writing was very skillful, and Aro admired it for a long time. The lady then looked around and dismissed the curtains, ordered the candle to be lit, and mounted the carriage. Aro saw her off to the door, and they parted with tears. Not even a dozen steps away, the carriage and the horse and rider all vanished from sight. Aro was amazed by the event and stored the lady's poem in his box.

    147

    明年春，下第东归，至新丰，夕舍逆旅。因步月长望，感前事，又为诗曰：
    　　“一纸华笺丽碧云，余香犹在墨犹新
    　　空添满目凄凉事，不见三山缥缈人
    　　斜月照人今夜梦，落花啼鸟去年春。知
    　　红闺更有堪愁处。窗上虫丝镜上尘。”即而朗吟之。忽闻数百步外有车音西来甚急，俄见金闺従数骑，视其従者，乃前时双鬟也。惊问之，双鬟遽前告，即驻车。使谓翱曰：“通衢中，恨不得一见。”翱请其舍逆旅，固不可。又问所适，答曰：“将之弘农。”翱因曰：“某今亦归洛阳，愿偕东，可乎？”曰：“吾行甚迫，不可。”即褰车帘，谓翱曰：“感君意勤厚，故一面耳。”言竟，呜咽不自胜。翱亦为之悲泣，因诵以所制之诗。美人曰：“不意君之不忘如是也，幸何厚焉。”又曰：“愿更酬此一篇。”翱即以纸笔与之，俄顷而成，曰：主

    Explanation

    147

    The following spring, having failed the imperial examinations and returned eastward, I arrived in Xin Feng and stayed at an inn at dusk. Walking and gazing at the moon, I reflected on past events and then composed a poem, saying:

    "A sheet of fine paper like a green cloud, the lingering fragrance and fresh ink remain.
    Only adding to the sight of sorrowful events, I do not see the ethereal people of the Three Mountains.
    The slanting moon illuminates tonight's dream, fallen flowers and crying birds from last spring.
    The red chamber has even more things to grieve over, cobwebs on the window and dust on the mirror."

    I then recited it aloud. Suddenly, I heard the sound of a carriage coming from the west, very urgent. Soon, I saw a carriage accompanied by several horsemen. Looking at the attendants, they were the two maids from before. Surprised, I asked them, and the two maids quickly went forward to report. The carriage stopped immediately. The attendant said to Aow, "In the main street, I could not help but wish to see you." Aow asked her to stay at the inn, but she firmly refused. He then asked where she was going, and she replied, "I am going to Hongnong." Aow said, "I am also returning to Luoyang today, would you like to go east with me?" She replied, "My journey is very urgent, I cannot." She immediately lifted the carriage curtain and said to Aow, "I am moved by your kind intentions, so I only have a brief meeting." After speaking, she wept uncontrollably. Aow also wept with sadness and recited the poem he had composed. The woman said, "I did not expect you to remember me so well, how generous you are." She then said, "I wish to repay you with another poem." Aow immediately gave her paper and a pen, and in a short while, she finished and said:

    [The content of the second poem is not provided in the source text, so it is omitted here.]

    148

    “惆怅佳期一梦中，五陵春色尽成空。知
    　　欲知离别偏堪恨，只为音尘两不通
    　　愁态上眉凝浅绿，泪痕侵脸落轻红
    　　双轮暂与王孙驻，明日西驰又向东。”翱谢之。良久别去，才百余步，又无所见。翱虽知为怪，眷然不能忘。知
    　　及至陕西，遂下道至弘农，留数日，冀一再遇，竟绝影响。及还洛阳，出二诗，话于友人。不数月，以怨结，遂卒

    Explanation

    148

    Melancholy, the beautiful time was but a dream; the spring scenery of the Five Mausoleums has all turned to nothing.
    To understand parting is especially worth hating, only because news cannot reach each other.
    Sorrowful expression congeals on the eyebrows, light green in hue; tear stains stain the face, falling like light red.
    The wheels of the carriage temporarily pause with the noble youth, but tomorrow they will ride westward and then eastward again.
    Aowei thanked him. After a long time, he bid farewell and departed, only to see nothing after a hundred or so steps. Although Aowei knew it was strange, he could not forget it.
    By the time he reached Shaanxi, he took the road to Hongnong, where he stayed for several days, hoping to meet again, but there was no sign of him. When he returned to Luoyang, he recited the two poems to his friends. A few months later, bound by resentment, he died.

    149

    ○僧法长
    　　河南龙门寺僧法长者，郑州原武人。宝历中，尝自龙门归原武。家有田数顷，稔而未刈。一夕因乘马行田间，马忽屹不前，虽鞭抶辄不动，唯瞪目东望，若有所见。时月明，随其望，数百步外有一物，如古木色，兀然而来。长惧，即回马走道左数十步伺之。其物来渐近，乃白气，高六七尺，腥秽甚，愈于鲍肆。有声绵绵如呻吟，西望而去。长策马随其后，常远数十步。行一里余，至里民王氏家，遂突入焉。长驻马伺之，顷之，忽闻其家呼曰：“车宇下牛将死，可偕来视之。”又顷闻呼后舍驴蹶仆地，不可救。又顷，闻惊哭，有出者。长佯过讯之，曰：“主人有子十余岁，忽卒。”语未竟，又闻哭音，或惊叫，联联不已。夜分后，声渐少，迨明而绝。长骇异，即具告其邻，偕来王氏居侦之。其中悄然无闻，因开户，而其家十余人，皆死，鸡犬无存焉

    Explanation

    149

    A monk named Fa Chang from Longmen Temple in Henan, who was from Yuanwu in Zhengzhou. During the Bao Li era, he once returned from Longmen to Yuanwu. His family had several acres of land, and the crops were ripe but not yet harvested. One evening, while riding his horse through the fields, the horse suddenly stopped, even if whipped or struck, it would not move, only staring eastward. At the time, the moon was bright, and following the direction of the horse's gaze, hundreds of steps away, there was an object like the color of an ancient tree, coming abruptly. Fa Chang was frightened and immediately turned his horse to run alongside the path for dozens of steps to observe. The object came closer, and it was white vapor, about six or seven feet tall, very foul-smelling, even more so than a fish market. There was a soft sound like a moan, and it looked westward and then departed. Fa Chang rode his horse to follow behind, always keeping a distance of dozens of steps. After traveling more than a mile, they arrived at the house of a villager named Wang, and the object suddenly entered the house. Fa Chang stopped his horse to wait, and after a while, he suddenly heard the family call out, "There is a cow dying under the carriage, come and see." Soon after, they heard the sound of a donkey falling to the ground, unable to be saved. Then, they heard cries of alarm, and someone came out. Fa Chang pretended to pass by and asked what happened. They said, "The master has a ten-year-old son who suddenly died." Before the sentence was finished, they heard more cries of alarm and exclamations, continuing without end. After midnight, the sounds gradually diminished, and by dawn, they had stopped. Fa Chang was greatly surprised and reported the incident to his neighbors, who came together to investigate the Wang family's house. Inside, everything was silent, and when they opened the door, they found that the Wang family of over ten people had all died, and there were no chickens or dogs left.

    150

    ○郑生
    　　俗传人之死凡数日，当有禽自柩中而出者，曰“杀”。大和中，有郑生者，常于隰川与郡官畋于野，有网得一巨鸟，色苍，高五尺余，主将命解而视之，忽无所见。生惊，即访里中民讯之，民有对者曰：“里中有人死且数日，卜人言今日‘杀’当去，其家伺而视之，有巨鸟色苍，自柩中出。君之所获果是乎？”生异而归天宝中，京兆尹崔光远因游略，常遇一妖鸟，事与此同也

    Explanation

    150

    Zhen Sheng

    According to folk belief, after a few days of death, a bird will emerge from the coffin, and this bird is called "kill" (殺). During the Daxi period, there was a man named Zheng Sheng who was hunting with a local official in Xichuan. They caught a large bird with a grayish color, about five feet tall. The commander ordered it to be released and examined, but suddenly there was nothing to be seen. Zheng Sheng was surprised and immediately went to ask the villagers. One villager replied, "There was someone in our village who had died several days ago. The diviner said that today the ‘kill’ would leave. The family watched for it and saw a large bird with a grayish color emerge from the coffin. Is what you captured indeed that bird?" Zheng Sheng was amazed and returned. During the Tianbao period, the Prefect of Jingzhao, Cui Guangyuan, often encountered a strange bird while traveling, and the incident was similar to this.

    151

    ○清江郡叟
    　　唐开元中，清江郡叟常牧牛于郡南田间，忽闻有异声自地中发。叟与牧童数辈俱惊走辟易，自是叟病热且甚。仅旬余，病少愈，梦一丈夫，衣青襦，顾谓叟曰：“迁我于开元观。”叟惊而寤，然不知其旨。后数日，又适野，复闻之。即以其事白于郡守封君。怒曰：“岂非昏而妄乎！”叱遣之。是夕，叟又梦衣青襦者告曰：“吾委迹于地下久矣，汝速出我，不然得疾。”叟大惧。及晓，与其子偕往郡南，即鉴其地，约丈余，得一锺，色青，乃向所梦丈夫色衣也。遂再白于郡守，郡守置于开元观。是日辰时，不击忽自鸣，声极震响。清江之人俱而惊叹。郡守因其事上闻，玄宗诏宰臣林甫写其钟样，告示天下。知

    Explanation

    151

    The Old Man of Qingjiang Prefecture

    During the Kaiyuan period of the Tang Dynasty, an old man from Qingjiang Prefecture often herded cattle in the fields south of the prefecture. One day, he suddenly heard a strange sound coming from the ground. The old man and several herding boys all fled in fright. After this incident, the old man suffered from a severe fever. After only ten days, his illness somewhat improved, and he dreamed of a man wearing a green robe. The man turned and said to the old man, "Move me to the Kaiyuan Temple." The old man was startled awake but did not understand the meaning. A few days later, he happened to hear the same sound again in the countryside. He immediately reported this matter to Prefect Feng. Prefect Feng said angrily, "Is this not foolish and delusional?" and scolded and sent him away. That night, the old man had another dream in which the man in the green robe told him, "I have been buried underground for a long time. You must quickly dig me up, or else you will fall ill." The old man was greatly frightened. By morning, he went with his son to the southern part of the prefecture, examined the area, which was about a zhang wide, and found a bell, which was green in color, just as the man in the dream had been dressed. He then reported this again to Prefect Feng, who placed the bell in the Kaiyuan Temple. On that day at the third watch, the bell suddenly rang without being struck, and the sound was extremely loud and resonant. The people of Qingjiang all gathered and exclaimed in amazement. Prefect Feng reported this event to Emperor Xuanzong, who ordered Chancellor Linfu to write down the design of the bell and announce it throughout the empire.

    152

    ○东莱客
    　　东莱郡有馆亭，其西轩常有怪异。客有寝其下者，夜常闻有犬吠，声甚微。以独视之，则一无所见。如是者累年矣。其后郡守命扃键为库。尝一夕，月皎，有库更见一犬甚小，苍色，自轩下环庭而走。库更怪其与常犬异，因投石击之，其犬吠而去，入西轩下。明日库吏以其事白于郡守，郡守命于西轩穷其迹。见门上狗有苍毛甚多，果库吏所见苍犬之色，众方悟焉
    ○交城里人
    　　交城县南十数里，常夜有怪见于人，多悸而病且死焉。里人患之久矣。其后里中人有执弧矢夜行者，县南见一魁然若巨人状，衣朱衣，以帛巾蒙其首，缓步而来，欹偃若甚醉者。里人惧，即引满而发，果中焉。其怪遂退。里人惧少解，即北走至旅舍，且语其事。明日抵县城，见郭之西丹桂，有一矢贯其上，果里人之矢。取之以归，镞有血甚多。白于县令，令命焚之。由是县南无患。知

    Explanation

    152

    ○东莱客
    In Donglai County, there was a pavilion where strange occurrences frequently happened in the western pavilion. Guests who slept below it often heard faint barking at night. When they looked alone, they saw nothing. This continued for many years. Later, the county magistrate ordered the pavilion to be locked and secured as a storeroom. One night, when the moon was bright, a storekeeper saw a very small gray dog circling the courtyard and running. The storekeeper found it strange that it was different from ordinary dogs and threw a stone to hit it. The dog barked and ran away, entering the western pavilion. The next day, the storekeeper reported the incident to the county magistrate, who ordered an investigation in the western pavilion. They found many grayish hairs on the door, which matched the color of the gray dog the storekeeper had seen, and everyone finally understood.

    ○交城里人
    Several miles south of Jiaocheng County, strange phenomena often appeared to people at night, causing many to become terrified, fall ill, and even die. The villagers had been troubled by this for a long time. Later, a villager walking with a bow and arrow at night in the southern part of the county saw a tall and large figure like a giant, wearing red clothing and a silk scarf covering his head, walking slowly and leaning as if very drunk. The villager was afraid and immediately drew back and shot, hitting the figure. The strange phenomenon then retreated. The villagers' fear somewhat eased, and the villager walked north to an inn, telling the story. The next day, he reached the county town and saw the red osmanthus tree west of the city wall, with an arrow piercing through it, which indeed belonged to the villager. He took the arrow back, and the arrowhead had a lot of blood. He reported this to the county magistrate, who ordered it to be burned. From then on, there was no more trouble in the southern part of the county.

    153

    ○崔
    　　元和中，博陵崔者，自汝郑来，侨居长安延福里。常一日读书牖下，忽见一童，长不尽尺，露发，衣黄，自北垣下趋至榻前，且谓曰：“幸寄君砚席，可乎？”不应。又曰：“我尚壮，愿备指使，何见拒之深耶！”又不顾。已而上榻，跃然拱立良久，于袖中出一小幅文书致前，乃诗也，细字如粟，历然可辨。诗曰：斋
    　　“昔荷蒙恬惠，寻遭仲叔投
    　　夫君不指使，何处觅银钩”。览讫，笑而谓曰：“既愿相従，无乃后悔耶！”其僮又出一诗，投于几上，诗曰：

    Explanation

    153

    Fengli

    During the Yuanhe era, there was a man named Cui Fengli from Boling, who came from Ruyang and Zheng and lived temporarily in the Yinfu Lane of Chang'an. One day, while reading under the window, he suddenly saw a child, no more than a foot tall, bareheaded and wearing yellow clothes, who came running from the northern wall and approached his bed. The child said to Fengli, "May I entrust myself to your study? Is that acceptable?" Fengli did not respond. The child then said, "I am still strong, and I wish to be at your service. Why do you reject me so strongly?" Fengli still did not pay attention. The child then climbed onto the bed, stood up gracefully for a long time, and took out a small piece of writing from his sleeve and presented it to Fengli. It was a poem, with tiny letters like millet, clearly distinguishable. The poem read:

    "Once I benefited from the kindness of Meng Tian,
    Later I was thrown away by my brothers.
    If you do not give me tasks, where can I find a silver hook?"

    After reading it, Fengli laughed and said, "Since you have agreed to follow, will you not regret it?" The child then took out another poem and placed it on the table, which read:

    154

    “学问従君有，诗书自我传
    　　须知王逸少，名价动千年。”又曰：“吾无逸少之艺，虽得汝，安所用”。俄而又投一篇曰：知
    　　“能令音信通千里，解致龙蛇运八行
    　　惆怅江生不相赏，应缘自负好文章。”戏曰：“恨汝非五色者。”其僮笑而下榻，遂趋北垣，入一穴中。即命仆发其下，得一管文笔。因取书，锋锐如新，用之月余，亦无他怪。知
    ○张秀才
    　　东都陶化里有空宅，大和中，张秀才借得肄业。常忽忽不安，自念为男子，当抱慷慨之志，不宜恇怯以自软，因移入中堂以处之。夜深欹枕，乃见道士与僧徒各十五人従堂中出，形容长短皆相似，排作六行，威仪容止，一一可敬。秀才以为灵仙所集，不敢惕息，因佯寝以窥之。良久，别有二物展转于地，每一物各有二十一眼，内四眼剡剡如火色，相驰逐，而目光眩转，砉剨有声。

    Explanation

    154

    “Knowledge is inherited from you, but poetry and books are passed down through my own efforts.
    　　You must know Wang Xizhi, whose fame and value have endured for a thousand years.” He then said, “I lack the skill of Wang Xizhi, so even if I get you, what good will it do?” Soon after, he threw another piece of writing:知
    　　“It can enable communication over great distances, and it can express complex ideas.
    　　I am sad that Jiang Sheng does not appreciate my good work, but I take pride in my writing.” He playfully said, “I regret that you are not colorful.” His servant laughed and got off the bed, then quickly moved to the northern wall and entered a hole. He immediately ordered his servant to uncover the hole and found a tube of writing brush. He then took the brush, which was as sharp as new, and used it for over a month without any other strange occurrences.知
    ○张秀才
    　　In the Taohua neighborhood of Dongdu, there was an empty house. During the Dazhong era, a scholar named Zhang borrowed it to study. He often felt restless and uneasy, thinking that as a man, he should have a bold and generous spirit, and should not be timid and weak. He then moved into the central hall to stay. Late at night, leaning on his pillow, he saw fifteen Taoist priests and Buddhist monks each emerge from the hall. They all had similar appearances, arranged in six rows, with dignified and respectable demeanor. The scholar thought they were a gathering of immortals and did not dare to breathe a sound, so he pretended to sleep and watched them. After a while, two other objects were moving on the ground, each with twenty-one eyes, four of which were bright like fire, chasing each other, and their eyes were spinning, making a sharp cracking sound.

    155

    逡巡间，僧道三十人，或驰或走，或东或西，或南或北，道士一人独立一处，则被一僧击而去之。其二物周流于僧道之中，未尝暂息。如此争相击抟，或分或聚。一人忽叫云：“卓绝矣。”言竟，僧道皆默然而息。乃见二物相谓曰：“向者群僧与道流妙法绝高，然皆赖我二物成其教行耳，不然，安得称卓绝哉！”秀才乃知必妖怪也，因以枕而掷之。僧道三十人与二物一时惊走，曰：“不速去，吾辈且为措大所使也。”遂皆不见。明日搜寻之，于壁角中得一败囊，中有长行子三十个并骰子一双耳

    Explanation

    155

    In the meantime, thirty monks and Taoist priests were seen moving chaotically, some running, some walking, some going east, some west, some south, and some north. A single Taoist priest stood alone in one place, only to be struck and removed by a monk. The two objects wandered among the monks and Taoists, never taking a moment's rest. In this way, they competed to strike and gather, sometimes separating, sometimes coming together. Suddenly, one person shouted, "Outstanding!" After speaking, the monks and Taoists all fell silent. Then, the two objects spoke to each other, saying, "Earlier, the group of monks and Taoist priests had extremely high and wonderful methods, but they all depended on us two to complete their teachings. Otherwise, how could they be called outstanding?" The scholar then realized that they must be ghosts or spirits, and threw a pillow at them. The thirty monks and Taoists, along with the two objects, fled in fright at once, saying, "Do not delay in leaving, we are almost controlled by a scholar." And so they all disappeared. The next day, upon searching, in the corner of the wall, they found a tattered bag containing thirty longhanging dice and a pair of dice.

    156

    ○河东街吏
    　　开成中，河东郡有吏，常中夜巡惊街路。一夕，天晴月朗，乃至景福寺前，见一人挽而坐交，臂拥膝，身尽黑，居然不动。吏惧，因叱之，其人挽而不顾。叱且久，即扑其首。忽举视，其面貌极异，长数尺，色白而瘦，状甚可惧。吏初惊仆于地，久之，稍能起，因视之，已亡见矣。吏由是惧益甚，即驰归，具语于人。其后因重构景福寺门，发地，得一漆桶，凡深数尺，上有白泥合其首，果街吏所见。知

    Explanation

    156

    During the reign of Emperor Wenzong in the Kai Cheng era, there was an official in Hedong Prefecture who often patrolled the streets at midnight. One night, under clear skies and a bright moon, he reached the front of Jingfu Temple. There, he saw a person sitting cross-legged, arms hugging the knees, with an entirely black body, completely motionless. Frightened, the official scolded the person, but the figure pulled and did not look back. After scolding for a long time, the official struck its head. Suddenly raising his gaze, he saw that the figure had an extremely strange appearance, several feet tall, pale and thin, and its appearance was very frightening. The official was initially so frightened that he fell to the ground, but after a while, he gradually managed to stand up and looked around, but the figure had already disappeared. From then on, the official was even more terrified and hurried back home to tell others about his experience. Later, when the gate of Jingfu Temple was rebuilt and the ground was excavated, a lacquered bucket was found, deep several feet, with white clay sealing its head. Indeed, it matched what the street official had seen.

    157

    ○独孤彦
    　　建中末，有独孤彦者，尝客于淮泗间。会天大风，舟不得进，因泊于岸。一夕，步月登陆，至一佛寺中，寺僧悉赴里民会去，彦步绕于庭。俄有二丈夫来。一人身甚长，衣黑衣，称姓甲，名侵讦，第五；一人身广而短，衣青衣，称姓曾名元。与彦揖而语。其吐论玄微，出于人表。彦素耽奇奥，常与方外士议语，且有年矣。至于玄门释氏，靡不穷其指归。乃遇二人，则自以为不能加也。窃奇之，且将师焉。因再拜请曰：“某好奇者，今日幸遇先生，原为门弟子，其可乎。”二人谢曰：“何敢。”

    Explanation

    157

    During the late Jianzhong era, there was a man named Du Gu Yan who once traveled as a guest in the region between the Huai and Si rivers. One day, a sudden strong wind made it impossible for his boat to proceed, so he anchored it at the shore. Under the moonlight, he walked to the shore and arrived at a Buddhist temple. All the monks had gone to a village gathering, so Yan walked around the courtyard. Soon, two men came. One man was very tall and wore black clothes, calling himself Jia Qinjie, the fifth generation. The other man was broad but short, wearing green clothes, calling himself Zeng Yuan. They bowed to Yan and spoke with him. Their discourse was profound and subtle, beyond the level of ordinary people. Yan, who always sought out strange and profound things, had often discussed with reclusive scholars for many years. As for the esoteric teachings of Daoism and Buddhism, nothing escaped their understanding of the essence. When he met these two men, he felt that he could not surpass them. He secretly admired them and intended to learn from them. Therefore, he bowed twice and said, "I am someone who seeks out the strange. Today I have the good fortune to meet you, sir. I wish to become your disciple. Is it possible?" The two men replied, "How dare we?"

    158

    彦因征其所自。黑衣者曰：“吾之先，本卢氏。吾少以刚劲闻。大凡物有滞而不通者，必侵犯以讦悟之，时皆谓我为侵讦。因名之。其后适野，遇仇家击断。遂易姓甲氏，且逃其患。又吾素精药术，尝侍忝医之职，非不能精熟，而升降上下，即假手于人。后以年老力衰，上欲以我为折腰吏，吾固辞免，退居田间。吾有舅氏，常为同僚，其行止起居，未尝不俱。然我自摈弃，常思吾舅，直以用舍殊，致分不见矣。今夕君子问我，我得以语平生事，幸何甚哉。

    Explanation

    158

    The man inquired about the origin of his lineage. The black-clothed one said, "My ancestors originally belonged to the Lu family. When I was young, I was known for my firmness and strength. Generally, anything that is stuck or not flowing must be provoked to be enlightened, and at the time, everyone called me invasive and accusatory. Because of this, people named me. Later, when I went to the wilderness, I met an enemy who broke me. I then changed my surname to Jia and escaped from my suffering. Additionally, I have always been skilled in medical arts and once held the position of a doctor. It is not that I could not become highly skilled, but when it comes to ascending and descending, I rely on others. Due to old age and weakening strength, the ruler wanted to appoint me as a low-ranking official, but I firmly declined the offer and retired to the countryside. I have an uncle who was my colleague, and his actions and daily routines were never different from mine. But since I was discarded, I often think of my uncle. It is because our usage and placement were different, leading to our separation. Now that the gentleman is asking me, I can talk about my life story, how fortunate I am!"

    159

    语罢，曾元曰：“吾之先，陶唐氏之后也。唯陶唐之官，受姓于姚曾者，与子孙以字为氏，故为曾氏焉。我其后也。吾早従莱侯，居推署之职，职当要热，素以褊躁，又当负气以凌上，由是遭下流沸腾之谤，因而解去，盖吾忠烈之罪。我自弃置，处尘土之间，且有年矣，甘同瓦砾，岂敢他望乎然日昔与吾父遭事，吾父性坚正，虽鼎镬不避其危，赒人之急，必赴汤蹈火，人亦以此重之。今拘于旧职，窘若囚系。余以父弃掷之故，不近于父，迨今亦数岁。足下有问，又安敢默乎？

    Explanation

    159

    After the conversation, Zeng Yuan said: "My ancestors are descendants of the Taotang dynasty. Among those who were granted the surname Yao-Zeng by the officials of Taotang, my family adopted the character '曾' as our surname, hence we are the Zeng clan. I am a descendant of this lineage. I once served under Lord Lai, holding an important position, which required me to handle critical matters. I was naturally narrow-minded and impulsive, and I often acted out of pride or anger against my superiors. As a result, I faced slander and criticism from the lower classes, and eventually resigned from my post. This was due to my sense of loyalty and bravery, which led to my downfall. I have since been living a humble life, almost forgotten, and have been in this state for many years. I am content to be like broken tiles and rubble, and I do not have high aspirations. However, when I recall the events of the past with my father, I remember that he had a strong and upright character. He was willing to face even the most severe punishments, such as being boiled in a cauldron, and he always helped others in times of need, even going through fire and water. People respected him for this. Now, I am confined to my old position, feeling trapped like a prisoner. Because of my father's neglect, I have distanced myself from him, and this has been the case for several years. If you have any questions, how could I remain silent?"

    160

    语未卒，寺僧俱归，二人见之，若有所惧，即驰去。数十步已亡见矣。彦讯僧，僧曰：“吾居此寺且久，未尝见焉。惧为怪耳。”彦奇其才，且异之。因祈其名氏，久而悟曰：“所谓曾元者，岂非甑乎夫文，以瓦附曾，是甑字也。名元者，盖以瓦中之画，致瓦字之上，其义在矣。甲侵讦者，岂非铁杵乎且以午木是杵字。姓甲者，东方甲乙木也。第五者，亦假午字也。推是而辩，其杵字乎名侵讦者，盖反其语为金截。以截附金，是铁字也。总而辩焉，得非甑及铁杵耶！”明日，即命穷其迹。果于朽坏中得一杵而铁者，又一甑自中分，盖用之余者。彦大异之，尽符其解也

    Explanation

    160

    As the words were still uncompleted, the temple monks all returned, and the two people saw them, as if they were afraid of something, and immediately fled away. After dozens of steps, they were no longer seen. Yan questioned the monks, and they said, "We have lived in this temple for a long time, and we have never seen them before. We are afraid they might be strange." Yan found their talent extraordinary and found it strange. Therefore, he asked for their names. After a long while, he understood and said, "So-called Zeng Yuan, isn't it the steamer? The character '文' with '瓦' attached to '曾', is the character for 'steamer'. The name 'Yuan' is because the picture inside the '瓦' is placed above the '瓦' character, which conveys its meaning. The person named Jia Qinjie, isn't it the iron pestle? And '午' wood is the character for 'pestle'. The surname 'Jia' is the wood of the east, 'Jia' and 'Yi'. The fifth, also uses the '午' character. By this reasoning, it is the 'pestle' character. The name 'Qinjie' is because the reverse of the word is 'gold cut'. By attaching 'cut' to 'gold', it is the character for 'iron'. In summary, isn't it the steamer and the iron pestle?" The next day, he ordered to investigate their traces. Indeed, in the decayed remnants, he found an iron pestle, and another steamer that was split in half, which was the remaining part used. Yan was greatly surprised, and everything matched his interpretation.

    161

    ○卢郁
    　　进士卢郁者，河朔人，徙家长安。尝北游燕赵，遂客于内黄，郡守馆郁于廨舍。先是，其舍无居人。及郁至，见一姥，发尽白，身庳而肥，被素衣来，谓郁曰：“妾侨居于此且久矣，故相候谒。”已而告去。是夕，郁独居堂之前，夜潮寒，有风雪，其姥又至，谓郁曰：“贵客独处，何以为欢耶！”命坐语谓。姥曰：“妾姓石氏，家于华阴郡，后随吕御史者至此，且四十年。家苦贫，幸贵客见哀。”于是郁命食，而老姥卒不顾。

    Explanation

    161

    A Scholar-Official Named Lu Yu

    A scholar-official named Lu Yu, originally from the northern region, moved his family to Chang'an. He once traveled north to the states of Yan and Zhao and then stayed as a guest in Neihuang. The local governor housed Lu Yu in his official residence. Previously, the house had no occupants. When Lu Yu arrived, he saw an old woman with completely white hair, short and fat, wearing a plain white robe. She greeted him, saying, "I have been living here for a long time, so I came to greet you." Afterward, she left. That night, Lu Yu was alone in front of the hall, and it was cold with wind and snow. The old woman came again and said to Lu Yu, "How can you, a distinguished guest, entertain yourself alone?" She invited him to sit and talk. The old woman said, "I am surnamed Shi, and I come from Huayin County. I followed a certain Censor Wang here for nearly forty years. Our family is very poor, so please take pity on me, a distinguished guest." Lu Yu then offered her food, but the old woman did not look back as she left.

    162

    郁问之曰：“姑何为不食？”姥曰：“妾甚饥，然不食粟，以故寿而安。”郁好奇，闻之甚善，且以为有道术者。因问曰：“姑既不食粟，何饱其腹耶岂常饵仙药乎？”姥曰：“妾家于华阴，先人好神仙，庐于太华。妾亦常隐于山中，従道士学长生法。道士教妾吞火，自是绝粒。今已年九十矣，未审一日有寒暑之疾。”郁又问曰：“某早岁常遇至人，教吸气之术，自谓其妙。后以奔走名利，従都国之贡，昼趋而夜息。不意今夕遇姑，语及平生之好。

    Explanation

    162

    Yù asked her, "Why don't you eat?" The old woman replied, "I am very hungry, but I do not eat grain, so I live long and am at peace." Yù, being curious, thought it was very good and believed she had some kind of Daoist art or skill. He then asked, "Since you don't eat grain, how do you fill your stomach? Do you often take elixirs of immortality?" The old woman said, "I live in Huayin. My ancestors loved Daoist immortals, and we lived in a hut on Mount Taihua. I also often hid in the mountains, learning the methods of longevity from a Daoist. The Daoist taught me to swallow fire, and since then, I have abstained from eating. Now I am ninety years old, and I wonder if there will be a day when I get sick from cold or heat." Yù then said, "I once met a sage when I was young, who taught me the technique of breathing exercises. I thought it was wonderful. Later, I pursued fame and profit, traveling to the capital to pay tribute. During the day, I hurried; at night, I rested. I did not expect to meet you tonight. We talked about our lifelong interests."

    163

    然不知吞火岂神仙之旨乎？”姥曰：“子不闻至人寒暑不能侵者耶故入火，火不能焚；入水，水不能溺。如是，则吞火固其宜也。”郁曰：“愿观姑吞火，可乎？”姥曰：“有何不可哉！”于是以手采炉中火而吞之。火且尽，其色不动。郁且惊且异，遂起束带再拜，谢曰：“鄙野之人，未尝闻神仙事。今夕遇仙姑，以吞火之异，实平生所未闻者。”姥曰：“此小术尔，何足贵哉。”言讫，且告去。郁因降阶送之。既别，郁前归于寝堂。

    Explanation

    163

    How can one not know that swallowing fire is the essence of immortality?" the fairy maiden said. "Have you not heard that a perfected being is not affected by heat or cold? When entering fire, fire cannot burn them; when entering water, water cannot drown them. If so, then swallowing fire is indeed appropriate." Xu said, "May I watch you swallow fire, please?" The fairy maiden replied, "What is there that is not possible?" She then took fire from the furnace and swallowed it. As the fire was nearly gone, her complexion remained unchanged. Xu was both surprised and amazed, so he stood up, fastened his belt, and bowed twice, thanking her, "A rustic person like me has never heard of immortality. Tonight, I met a fairy maiden and witnessed the extraordinary act of swallowing fire, which is truly something I have never experienced before." The fairy maiden said, "This is just a minor trick, what is there to value in it?" After speaking, she announced her departure. Xu then descended the steps to see her off. After parting, Xu returned to his bedroom.

    164

    既深，有仆者告郁曰：“西庑下有火发！”郁惊起而视之，其西庑舍已焚。于是里中人俱至，竞以水沃之，迨旦方绝。及穷火发之迹，于庑下坎中得一石火通，中有火甚多。先是有败草积其上，故延而至烧。郁方悟老姥乃此火通耳。果所谓姓石氏，居于华山者也。郁因质问吕御史，有郡中老吏谓郁曰：“吕御史，魏之従事也，居此宅，迨今四十年矣。”咸如老姥言也。又青州济南平陵城北石虎，一夜移城东南善石沟上，有狼狐千余迹随之。迹皆成路

    Explanation

    164

    After a while, a servant informed Yu, saying, "There is a fire under the western veranda!" Yu was startled and rushed to look, and indeed, the western veranda was already burning. The people in the neighborhood all arrived and rushed to pour water on it, and it wasn't until dawn that the fire was finally extinguished. Upon investigating the origin of the fire, they found a stone fire starter in a pit under the veranda, with a large amount of firewood inside. There had been piles of rotten grass on top of it, which had caught fire and spread. Yu then realized that the old woman was indeed the source of this fire starter. It was indeed the person known as Mr. Shi, who lived in Mount Hua. Yu then questioned the Prefect of Justice Lu, and an old official from the prefecture told Yu, "Prefect of Justice Lu was an official from Wei, and he has lived in this house for forty years now." Everything matched what the old woman had said. Another stone tiger in the north of Pingling City in Jinan, Qingzhou, one night moved to the east-south side of the Shanzhigou area, and there were traces of over a thousand wolves and foxes following it. The tracks all formed a path.

    165

    ○竹季贞
    　　陈蔡间有民竹季贞，卒十余年矣。后里人赵子和亦卒，数日忽寤，即起驰出门。其妻子惊，前讯之，子和曰：“我，竹季贞也，安识汝今将归吾家。”既而语音非子和矣。妻子遂随之。至季贞家，见子和来，以为狂疾，骂而逐之。子和曰：“我，竹季贞，卒十一年，今乃归。何拒我耶！”其家聆其语，果季贞也；验其事，又季贞也。妻子俱骇异。诘之，季贞曰：“我自去人世，迨今具一纪，居冥途中，思还省妻孥，不一日忘。然冥间每三十年即一逝者再生，使言罪福。昨者吾所请案据，得以名闻冥官，愿为再生者。既而冥官谓我曰：‘汝宅舍坏久矣，如何’案据白曰：‘季贞同里赵子和者，卒数日，愿假其尸与季贞之魂。’冥官许之，即遣使送我于赵氏之舍，我故得归。”因话平昔事，历然可听。妻子方信而纳之。自是季贞不食酒肉，衣短粗衣，行乞陈、蔡、汝、郑间，缗帛随以修佛施贫饿者，不一还家，至今尚存

    Explanation

    165

    A Man Named Zhu Jizhen

    In the region between Chen and Cai, there lived a man named Zhu Jizhen, who had passed away more than ten years ago. Later, a villager named Zhao Zihuo also died, and several days after his death, he suddenly awoke and immediately rushed out of the door. His wife and children were startled and approached him, asking what had happened. Zhao Zihuo said, "I am Zhu Jizhen. How do you know me? I am returning to my home now." However, his voice was no longer that of Zhao Zihuo. His wife and children followed him, and when they arrived at Zhu Jizhen's house, they saw Zhao Zihuo coming and thought he was mad, scolding and driving him away. Zhao Zihuo, now speaking as Zhu Jizhen, said, "I am Zhu Jizhen. I died eleven years ago and have now returned. Why are you rejecting me?" The family listened to his words and realized it was indeed Zhu Jizhen; they verified the circumstances and confirmed it was him. His wife and children were both astonished and bewildered. They questioned him, and Zhu Jizhen explained, "Since I left the mortal world, it has been a full decade. I have been living in the underworld, constantly thinking of returning to see my wife and children, never forgetting for a single day. However, in the underworld, every thirty years, the souls of the deceased are given a chance to be reborn, and their deeds are judged. Last time, I presented my case to the underworld officials and was granted permission to be reborn. The underworld official then asked me, 'Your house has been ruined for a long time. What shall we do?' My case was presented, and it was suggested, 'There is Zhao Zihuo, a villager of the same area, who has just died. He wishes to lend his body to Zhu Jizhen's soul.' The underworld official agreed and sent someone to escort me to Zhao Zihuo's house, which is why I have returned." He then recounted past events, and everything he said was clear and understandable. His wife and children finally believed him and accepted him. From then on, Zhu Jizhen stopped eating meat and alcohol, wore coarse and short clothing, and went begging in the regions of Chen, Cai, Ru, and Zheng. Whenever he received money, he used it to support the poor and hungry, and he never returned home, and he is still alive to this day.

    166

    ○郄惠连
    　　大历中，山阳人郄惠连，始居泗上。以其父尝为河朔官，遂従居清河。父殁，惠连以哀瘠闻。廉使命吏临吊，赠粟帛。既免丧，表授漳南尉。知
    　　岁余，一夕独处于堂，忽见一人，衣紫佩刀，趋至前，谓惠连曰：“上帝有命，拜公为司命主者，以册立阎波罗王。”即以锦纹箱贮书，进于惠连曰：“此上帝命也。”轴用琼钿，标以纹锦。又象笏紫绶、金龟玉带以赐。惠连且喜且惧，心甚惶惑，不暇顾问，遂受之。立于前轩，有相者趋入，赞曰：“驱殿吏卒且至。”已而有数百人，绣衣红额，左右佩兵器趋入，罗为数行，再拜。一人前曰：“某幸得为使之吏，敢以谢。”词竟又拜。拜讫，分立于前。相者又曰：“五岳卫兵主将。

    Explanation

    166

    During the reign of Emperor Daizong of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man from Shan Yang named Qie Huilian, who initially lived in Si. Because his father had once served as an official in the northern regions, he followed him to live in Qing He. After his father's death, Huilian became known for his intense grief and emaciation. The governor sent officials to pay condolences and gave him grain and silk. After completing the mourning period, he was appointed as the magistrate of Zhangnan by imperial edict.
