Many people in fine silk and adorned with jewels and jade surrounded him. Zhang Jian hurried forward and bowed. After bowing, the person gestured for Zhang Jian to ascend the steps and said, "I am the Marquis of Bashu. I have lived here for several decades. Just now, I learned that you have nowhere to stay tonight, so I immediately invited you. Please stay a little longer to enjoy our hospitality." Zhang Jian bowed again to express his gratitude. Soon after, the host ordered the preparation of a feast and placed wine and food. The items used for play and decoration were all luxurious and precious.

    254

    又令左右邀六雄将军、白额侯、沧浪君，又邀五豹将军、钜鹿侯、玄丘校尉。且传教曰：“今日贵客来，愿得尽欢宴，故命奉请。”使者唯而去。久之乃至，前有六人皆黑衣，赑然其状，曰“六雄将军”。巴西侯起而拜，六雄将军亦拜。又一人衣锦衣，戴白冠，貌甚狞，曰“白额侯”也。又起而拜，白额侯亦拜。又一人衣苍，其质魁岸，曰“沧浪君”也。巴西侯又拜，沧浪亦拜。又一人被斑文衣，似白额侯而稍小，曰“五豹将军”也。巴西又拜，五豹将军亦拜。又一人衣褐衣，首有三角，曰“钜鹿侯”也。巴西揖之。又一人衣黑，状类沧浪君，曰“玄丘校尉”也。巴西侯亦揖之。然后延坐。巴西南向坐，鋋北向，六雄、白额、沧浪处于东，五豹、钜鹿、玄丘处于西。既坐，行酒命乐，又美人十数，歌者舞者，丝竹既发，穷极其妙

    Explanation

    254

    The host then ordered his attendants to summon the Six Heroes General, the White-Fronted Marquis, and Lord of Canglang, and also to invite the Five Panthers General, the Marquis of Juxu, and the Commander of Xuanqiu. He also issued instructions, saying: “Esteemed guests have arrived today, and I wish to enjoy a feast in their honor, hence I have ordered their invitation.” The envoy bowed and departed. After a long while, they finally arrived. Before him stood six men clad in black robes, their appearances formidable, and they were called the “Six Heroes General.” The Marquis of Bashui rose to bow, and the Six Heroes General also bowed in return. Another figure wore a brocade robe and a white cap, his appearance very fearsome, and he was called the “White-Fronted Marquis.” The Marquis of Bashui bowed again, and the White-Fronted Marquis also returned the bow. Another man wore dark clothing, his physique tall and imposing, and he was called “Lord of Canglang.” The Marquis of Bashui bowed once more, and Lord of Canglang returned the bow. Another figure wore a robe with a spotted pattern, resembling the White-Fronted Marquis but somewhat smaller, and he was called the “Five Panthers General.” The Marquis of Bashui bowed again, and the Five Panthers General also returned the bow. Another man wore a coarse brown robe, his head adorned with three horns, and he was called the “Marquis of Juxu.” The Marquis of Bashui gave him a respectful bow. Another figure wore black clothing, his appearance resembling that of Lord of Canglang, and he was called the “Commander of Xuanqiu.” The Marquis of Bashui also gave him a respectful bow. Then, they were invited to sit. The Marquis of Bashui sat facing south, while 鋋 sat facing north. The Six Heroes, the White-Fronted Marquis, and Lord of Canglang sat to the east; the Five Panthers General, the Marquis of Juxu, and the Commander of Xuanqiu sat to the west. Once seated, wine was served and music ordered. Dozens of beautiful women were present, singers and dancers, and as the music of silk and bamboo instruments began, their performance reached the height of its artistry.

    255

    白额侯酒酣，顾谓鋋曰：“吾今夜尚食，君能为我致一饱耶！”鋋曰：“末卜君侯所以尚者，愿教之。”白额侯曰：“君之躯可以饱我腹，亦何贵他味乎？”鋋惧，悚然而退。巴西侯曰：“无此理，奈何宴席之上，有忤贵客耶！”白额侯笑曰：“吾之言乃戏耳，安有如是哉！固不然也。”主
    　　久之，有告洞玄先生在门，愿谒白事。言讫，有一人被黑衣，颈长而身甚广。其人拜，巴西侯揖之。与坐，且问曰：“何为而来乎？”对曰：“某善卜者也，知君将有甚忧，故辄奉白。”巴西侯曰：“所忧者何也？”曰：“席上人将有图君，今不除，后必为害。愿君详之。”巴西侯怒曰：“吾欢宴方洽，何处有怪焉？”命杀之。其人曰：“用吾言，皆得安。不用吾言，则吾死，君亦死。将若之何，虽有后悔，其可追乎？”巴西侯遂杀卜者，置于堂下

    Explanation

    255

    The White-Browed Marquis, after drinking, turned to Zhan and said, "I will dine tonight. Can you provide me with a full meal?" Zhan replied, "I have not yet guessed what Your Excellency desires. I wish to be taught." The White-Browed Marquis said, "Your body can satisfy my hunger. What value is there in other flavors?" Zhan was afraid and left in a state of alarm. The Marquis of Bashu said, "There is no such thing. How can there be a strange occurrence at a feast?" The White-Browed Marquis laughed and said, "My words were just a jest. How could it be like that? It is not so." After a long while, there was a report that Mr. Dongxuan was at the door, wishing to report important matters. Upon hearing this, a man appeared, wearing black clothes, with a long neck and a broad body. He bowed, and the Marquis of Bashu greeted him. They sat down, and the Marquis asked, "Why have you come?" The man replied, "I am a skilled diviner. I know that you will have great troubles, so I have come to report." The Marquis of Bashu asked, "What troubles will I have?" The man said, "There is a person at the feast who intends to harm you. If you do not act now, you will surely suffer later. I hope you will consider it carefully." The Marquis of Bashu was angry and said, "My feast is just getting started. Where is there anything strange?" He ordered the man to be killed. The man said, "If you use my words, all will be well. If you do not use my words, I will die, and so will you. What should be done? Even if you regret it later, it cannot be undone." The Marquis of Bashu then killed the diviner and placed him on the ground.

    256

    时夜将半，众尽醉而皆卧于榻，鋋亦假寐焉。天将晓，忽悸而寤，见己身卧于大石龛中。其中设绣帷，旁列珠矶犀象。有一巨猿状如人，醉卧于地，盖所谓巴西侯也；又见巨熊卧于前者，盖所谓六雄将军也；又一虎顶白，亦卧于前，所谓白额侯也；又一狼，所谓沧浪君也；又有文豹，所谓五豹将军也；又一巨鹿，一狐，皆卧于前，盖所谓钜鹿侯、玄丘校尉也。而皆冥然若醉状。又一龟，形甚异，死于龛前，乃向所杀洞玄先生也。主

    Explanation

    256

    Around midnight, everyone was drunk and all lay on the bed, while Zhan also pretended to sleep. Just before dawn, he suddenly felt a startle and woke up, seeing himself lying in a large stone niche. Inside was set up with embroidered curtains, and pearls, coral, rhinoceros horn, and ivory were arranged around. There was a giant ape looking like a human, lying drunk on the ground, which is called the Lord of Bashu; then he saw a giant bear lying in front, which is called the Six Heroes General; another tiger with a white forehead, also lying in front, which is called the Lord of White Forehead; another wolf, which is called the Lord of Canglang; there was also a striped leopard, which is called the Five Leopards General; another giant deer and a fox, all lying in front, which are called the Lord of Juxu and the Colonel of Xuanqiu. And all were in a state of deep sleep, as if drunk. Another turtle, with a very unusual appearance, was dead in front of the niche, which was the recently killed Master Dongxuan.

    257

    鋋既见，大惊，即出山迳，驰告里中人。里人相集得百数，遂执弓挟矢入山中。至其处，其后猿忽惊而起，且曰：“不听洞玄先生言，今日果如是矣。”遂围其龛，尽杀之。其所陈器玩，莫非珍丽。乃具事以告太守。先是人有持真珠缯帛，涂至此者，俱无何而失，且有年矣。自后绝其患也
    ○杨叟
    　　乾元初，会稽民有杨叟者，家以资产丰赡闻于郡中。一日，叟将死，卧而呻吟，且仅数月。叟有子曰宗素，以孝行称于里人。迨其父病，罄其产以求医术。后得陈生者，究其原：“是翁之病心也。盖以财产既多，其心为利所运。故心已离去其身。非食生人心，不可以补之。而天下生人之心，焉可致耶如是，则非吾之所知也。”宗素既闻之，以为生心，故不可得也，独修浮图氏法，庶可以间其疾。即召僧转经，命工图铸其像，已而自赍食，诣郡中佛寺饭僧

    Explanation

    257

    A mysterious creature was seen, and upon seeing it, the people were greatly startled. They immediately went out through the mountain path and rushed to inform the villagers. The villagers gathered in large numbers, numbering around a hundred, and then armed with bows and arrows, they entered the mountain. Upon reaching the spot, later a monkey suddenly started and said, "Not listening to the words of the Master of Profound Mystery, today it is indeed as such." They then surrounded the niche and killed all of them. The objects displayed were all precious and beautiful. They prepared the details and reported to the magistrate. Before this, people who carried pearls and fine fabrics came to this place and all lost them without reason, and this had been going on for several years. From then on, the problem was eliminated.

    ○ Eldest Man Yang

    During the early years of the Qianyuan era, there was an elderly man named Yang in Kuaiji who was known throughout the county for his abundant wealth. One day, the old man was about to die, lying in bed and moaning, and this continued for several months. The old man had a son named Zong Su, who was known among the villagers for his filial piety. When his father fell ill, he used up all his wealth to seek medical treatment. Later, he obtained a man named Chen, who investigated the cause: "The old man's illness is a heart ailment. Because his wealth was abundant, his heart was driven by profit. Therefore, his heart had left his body. Unless one eats the heart of a living person, it cannot be healed. But how can the hearts of all living people be obtained? As such, it is beyond my knowledge." Zong Su, having heard this, thought that the heart ailment could not be obtained, and so he solely practiced Buddhist practices, hoping to alleviate his father's illness. He immediately called monks to recite sutras, ordered craftsmen to cast his father's image, and afterward brought food himself, going to a Buddhist temple in the county to feed the monks.

    258

    一日，因挈食去，误入一山迳中，见山下有石龛，龛有胡僧，貌甚老而枯瘠，衣褐毛缕成袈裟，踞于磐石上。宗素以为异人，即礼而问曰：“师，何人也独处穷谷，以人迹不到之地为家，又无侍者，不惧山野之兽，有害于师乎不然，是得释氏之术者耶！”僧曰：“吾本是袁氏。祖世居巴山，其后子孙，或在弋阳，散游诸山谷中，尽能绍修祖业，为林泉逸士，极得吟啸。人好为诗者，多称其善吟啸，于是稍闻于天下。有孙氏，亦族也，则多游豪贵之门。亦以善谈谑，故又以之游于市肆间，每一戏，能使人获其利焉。独吾好浮图氏，脱尘俗，栖心岩谷中不动，而在此且有年矣。常慕歌利王割截身体及菩提投崖以伺饿虎，故吾啖橡栗，饮流泉，恨未有虎狼噬吾。吾亦甘受之。”宗素因告曰：“师真至人，能舍其身而不顾，将以饲山兽，可谓仁勇俱极矣。虽然，弟子父有疾已数月，进而不瘳，某夙夜忧迫，计无所出。

    Explanation

    258

    One day, as I took food with me and went out, I mistakenly entered a mountain path. Below the mountain, I saw a stone niche, and inside the niche was a foreign monk. The monk appeared very old and emaciated, wearing a robe made of brown wool threads. He sat on a large, flat rock. Master Zongsu thought him a remarkable person and immediately bowed and asked, "Master, who are you? Why do you live alone in a remote valley, making a home in a place where human footprints are rare? You have no attendants, yet you do not fear the wild animals of the mountains. Is there any harm to you? Or is it because you have mastered the teachings of Buddhism?" The monk replied, "I am originally from the Yuan family. My ancestors lived in Bashan. Later generations of our family, some live in Yiyang, scattered and living in various valleys. All can continue and maintain our ancestral business, becoming reclusive scholars in forests and springs, extremely skilled in singing and whistling. People who love poetry often praise their skill in singing and whistling, and thus they gradually became known throughout the world. There is the Sun family, also a branch of our family, who often visit the homes of the rich and powerful. They are also skilled in jesting and joking, so they also visit marketplaces. Every time they play, they can bring profit to others. Only I like the Buddhist way, detaching from worldly affairs, dwelling in the mountains and valleys without moving, and I have been here for many years. I often admire King Goriputra's self-mutilation and Bodhidharma throwing himself off a cliff to wait for hungry tigers. Therefore, I eat acorns and chestnuts, drink flowing water, and regret that there are no tigers or wolves to devour me. I am also willing to accept it." Master Zongsu then said, "Master, you are truly a sage, able to give up your body without hesitation, intending to feed the mountain beasts. You can be called both compassionate and courageous. However, my father has been ill for several months, despite treatment, he has not recovered. I am worried day and night, and I have no idea what to do."

    259

    有医者云，是心之病也，非食生人之心，固不可得而愈矣。今师能弃身于豺虎以救其馁，岂若舍命于人以惠其生乎愿师详之。”僧曰：“诚如是，果吾之志也。檀越为父而求吾，吾岂有不可之意。且吾以身委于野兽，曷若惠人之生乎然今日尚未食，愿致一饭而后死也。”宗素且喜且谢，即以所挈食置于前。僧食之立尽，而又曰：“吾既食矣，当亦奉教，然俟吾礼四方之圣也。”于是整其衣，出龛而礼。礼东方已毕，忽跃而腾上一高树。宗素以为神通变化，殆不可测。俄召宗素，厉而问曰：“檀越向者所求何也？”宗素曰：“愿得生人心，以疗吾父疾。”僧曰：“檀越所愿者，吾已许焉。今欲先说《金刚经》之奥义，且闻乎？”宗素曰：“某素尚浮图氏，今日获遇吾师，安敢不听乎？”僧曰：“《金刚经》云：‘过去心不可得，见在心不可得，未来心不可得。’檀越若要取吾心，亦不可得矣。”言已，忽跳跃大呼，化为一猿而去。宗素惊异，惶骇而归。知

    Explanation

    259

    A physician said, "This is a disease of the heart. To cure it, one must eat the heart of a living person, which is indeed impossible." Now, if the teacher can sacrifice himself to the wild beasts to save his father's hunger, how much more virtuous is it to sacrifice one's life for the benefit of others? I hope the teacher will consider this carefully." The monk replied, "If that is true, then it aligns with my intentions. The layperson seeks me for his father, and I have no objections. Moreover, sacrificing myself to wild beasts is less virtuous than helping others. However, I have not yet eaten today, so I wish to have one meal before I die." Zong Su was both joyful and grateful, and immediately placed the food he had brought in front of the monk. The monk ate it quickly and then said, "I have eaten, and now I will follow your instructions, but first I must pay respects to the four directions." He then straightened his clothes, stepped out of the niche, and began to pay respects. After completing his respects to the east, he suddenly leaped and climbed a tall tree. Zong Su thought the monk had supernatural powers and found it difficult to understand. Soon, the monk called out to Zong Su in a stern voice and asked, "What did you request earlier?" Zong Su replied, "I wished to obtain a human heart to cure my father's illness." The monk said, "What you wish for, I have already agreed to. Now, I wish to first discuss the profound meaning of the Diamond Sutra. Would you like to listen?" Zong Su said, "I have always respected Buddhism. Today, honored to meet my teacher, how could I not listen?" The monk said, "The Diamond Sutra states, 'Past thoughts cannot be attained, present thoughts cannot be attained, and future thoughts cannot be attained.' If you wish to take my heart, it too cannot be attained." After saying this, he suddenly leaped, shouted, and transformed into a monkey and left. Zong Su was surprised and frightened, and returned home.

    260

    ○林景玄
    　　唐林景玄者，京兆人，侨居雁门，以骑射畋猎为己任。郡守悦其能，因募为衙门将。尝与其徒十数辈，驰健马，执弓矢兵杖，臂隼牵犬，俱骋于田野间，得麋鹿狐兔甚多。由是郡守纵其所往，不使亲吏事。尝一日，畋于郡城之高岗，忽起一兔榛莽中，景玄鞭马逐之，仅十里余，兔匿一墓穴。景玄下马，即命二卒守穴傍，自解鞍而憩。忽闻墓中有语者曰：“吾命，土也，克土者木，日次于乙，辰居卯，二木俱王，吾其死乎。”已而咨嗟者久之。又曰：“有自东而来者，我将不免。”景玄闻其语，且异之。因视穴中，见一翁，衣素衣，髯白而长，手执一轴书，前有死鸟鹊甚多。景玄即问之，其人惊曰：“果然！祸我者且至矣。”即诟骂。景玄默而计之曰：“此穴甚小，而翁居其中，岂非鬼乎不然，是盗而匿此。”即毁其穴，翁遂化为老狐，帖然俯地，景玄因射之而毙。视其所执之书，点画甚异，似梵书而非梵字，用素缣为幅，仅数十尺。景玄焚之。知

    Explanation

    260

    Lín Jǐngxuán of the Tang Dynasty was from Jingzhao but lived as a guest in Yānmén. He took hunting with horse and bow as his duty. The prefect was pleased with his ability and thus recruited him as a military officer. He often went hunting with his followers, riding strong horses, holding bows, arrows, and weapons, with a falcon on his arm and a dog in front, all galloping across the fields, obtaining many deer, hares, foxes, and rabbits. Because of this, the prefect allowed him to go wherever he pleased and did not make him handle administrative tasks. Once, while hunting on a high hill outside the city, a rabbit suddenly appeared from the bushes. Lín Jǐngxuán whipped his horse to chase it, covering about ten miles, when the rabbit hid in a grave. Lín Jǐngxuán dismounted, immediately ordered two soldiers to guard beside the grave, tied up his horse, and rested. Suddenly, he heard someone speaking from the grave, saying, "My fate is earth, and the element that overcomes earth is wood. The day is on the second position, and the hour is at the卯position. Both woods are dominant. Am I going to die?" Afterward, he sighed for a long time. He then said, "Someone coming from the east, I will not escape." Lín Jǐngxuán heard the words and found them strange. He looked into the grave and saw an old man, wearing white clothes, with a long white beard, holding a scroll of books, with many dead birds and crows in front. Lín Jǐngxuán immediately asked him, and the old man was surprised and said, "Indeed! The one who will bring disaster to me is coming." He then began to curse and scold. Lín Jǐngxuán silently thought about it and said, "This grave is very small, yet the old man lives in it. Is he not a ghost? Otherwise, he is a thief hiding here." He immediately destroyed the grave, and the old man then transformed into an old fox, lay down quietly on the ground. Lín Jǐngxuán then shot him and killed him. He looked at the book he held, the strokes were very unusual, seeming like Sanskrit but not Sanskrit characters, made of white silk, only several dozen feet long. Lín Jǐngxuán burned it.

    261

    ○祁县民
    　　唐祁县有村民，因辇地征刍粟至太原府。及归，途中日暮，有一白衣妇人，立路旁谓村民：曰“妾今日都城而来，困且甚，愿寄载车中可乎？”村民许之，乃升车。行未三四里，因脂辖，忽见一孤尾在车之隙，中垂于车辕下。村民即以镰断之。其妇人化为无尾白狐，鸣嗥而去
    ○李林甫（二）
    　　唐李林甫方居相位，尝退朝坐于堂之前轩。见一玄狐，其质甚大，若牛马而毛色黯黑有光，自堂中出，驰至庭，顾望左右。林甫命弧矢将射之，未及，已亡见矣。自是凡数日，每昼坐，辄有一玄狐出焉。其岁林甫籍没
    ○李揆（一）
    　　唐丞相李揆，乾元初为中书舍人。尝一日退朝归，见一白狐在庭中捣练石上，命侍僮逐之，已亡见矣。时有客于揆门者，因话其事。客曰：“此祥符也。某敢贺。”至明日，果选礼部侍郎

    Explanation

    261

    ○Qixian Villager
    In the Tang Dynasty, there was a villager from Qixian who transported fodder and grain to Taiyuan by cart. On his return journey, as evening approached, he saw a white-clad woman standing by the roadside and addressing him: "I have come from the capital today and am very exhausted. May I be allowed to ride in your cart?" The villager agreed, and she climbed aboard. They had not traveled three or four miles when, due to the need to grease the wheel hubs, the villager noticed a solitary tail in the gap of the cart, hanging down from the front. The villager immediately cut it off with a sickle. The woman then transformed into a tailless white fox and howled as it disappeared.

    ○Li Linfu (II)
    During the reign of Li Linfu as chancellor, he once sat in the front hall after returning from court. He saw a black fox, which was very large, with fur as dark and lustrous as that of a cow or horse, emerging from the hall and galloping to the courtyard, looking around. Li Linfu ordered an arrow to shoot it, but before he could, the fox had already vanished. For several days thereafter, every time he sat during the day, a black fox would appear. That year, Li Linfu's estate was confiscated and distributed.

    ○Li Kui (I)
    In the early period of the Tang Dynasty, when Li Kui served as a secretary in the imperial chancellery, he once returned home after a day of court. He saw a white fox in the courtyard, standing on a stone used for pounding silk, and ordered his servant to chase it. The fox had already disappeared. At that time, a guest at Li Kui's door heard about the incident and said, "This is an auspicious omen. I dare to congratulate you." The next day, Li Kui was indeed selected as a deputy minister of the Board of Rites.

    262

    ○裴少尹
    　　唐贞元中，江陵少尹裴君者，亡其名。有子十余岁，聪敏，有文学，风貌明秀，裴君深念之。后被病，旬日益甚，医药无及。裴君方求道术士，用呵禁之，冀瘳其苦。有叩门者，自称高氏子，以符术为业。裴即延入，令视其子。生曰：“此子非他疾，乃妖狐所为耳。然某有术能愈之。”即谢而祈焉。生遂以符术考召。近食顷，其子忽起曰：“某病念愈。”裴君大喜，谓高生为真术士。具食饮，已而厚赠缗帛，谢遣之。生曰：“自此当日日来候耳。”遂去。其子他疾虽愈，而神魂不足，往往狂语，或笑哭不可禁。高生每至，裴君即以此且祈之。生曰：“此子精魄，已为妖魅所击，今尚未还耳，不旬日当间，幸无以忧。”裴信之。居数日，又有王生者。自言有神符，能以呵禁除去妖魅疾。来谒，裴与语。谓裴曰：“闻君爱子被病，且未瘳。

    Explanation

    262

    During the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang, there was a local official in Jiangling named Péi, whose name is not recorded. He had a son over ten years old who was intelligent, well-versed in literature, and had a bright and refined appearance. Péi deeply cared for him. Later, the son became ill, and his condition worsened day by day, with medicine proving ineffective. Péi then sought a Taoist magician to use exorcism, hoping to alleviate his son's suffering. One day, a man knocked on the door and introduced himself as a son of the Gao family, whose profession was magic. Péi invited him in and asked him to examine his son. The magician said, "This child is not suffering from a common illness, but is bewitched by a fox spirit. However, I have a method that can cure him." Péi immediately thanked him and prayed for help. The magician then performed his rituals. After a meal, the son suddenly sat up and said, "My illness seems to have subsided." Péi was greatly pleased and called the magician a true practitioner of magic. He prepared food and drink, then heavily rewarded him with money and silk, and thanked and dismissed him. The magician said, "From now on, I will come every day to visit." He then left. Although the son's physical illness had subsided, his spirit was weak, and he often spoke wildly, sometimes laughing or crying uncontrollably. Every time the magician came, Péi prayed to him for help. The magician explained, "This child's spirit has been struck by a demon, and it has not yet returned. In no more than ten days, he will recover, please do not worry." Péi believed him. After several days, another man named Wang arrived. He claimed to have a divine talisman that could banish demons and cure their diseases. He came to pay respects, and Péi spoke with him. Wang said, "Hearing that your beloved son is ill, and the illness has not yet healed, I offer my assistance."

    263

    愿得一见矣。”裴即使见其子，生大惊曰：“此郎君病狐也。不速治，当加甚耳。”裴君因话高生，王笑曰：“安知高生不为狐”，乃坐。方设席为呵禁，高生忽至。既入大骂曰：“奈何此子病愈，而乃延一狐于室内耶即为病者耳。”王见高来，又骂曰：“果然妖狐，今果至。安用为他术考召哉？”二人纷然，相诟辱不已。裴氏家方大骇异，忽有一道士至门，私谓家僮曰：“闻裴公有子病狐，吾善视鬼，汝但告，请入谒。”家僮驰白裴君，出话其事，道士曰：“易与耳。”入见二人，二人又诟曰：“此亦妖狐，安得为道士惑人。”道士亦骂之曰：“狐当还郊野墟墓中，何为挠人乎？”既而闭户相斗殴。数食顷，裴君益恐。其家僮惶惑，计无所出。及暮，阒然不闻声。开视，三狐皆仆地而喘，不能动矣。裴君尽鞭杀之。其子后旬月乃愈矣

    Explanation

    263

    I wish to see him once." Mr. Péi immediately had his son brought in, and the young man was greatly surprised and said, "This young master is afflicted by a fox spirit. If it is not treated quickly, it will worsen." Mr. Péi then mentioned Gāo, and Wáng laughed and said, "How can you be sure Gāo is not a fox himself?" and sat down. Just as he was setting up a seat and preparing to forbid the fox, Gāo suddenly arrived. After entering, he shouted angrily, "How can this son of yours have recovered, yet you invite a fox into the house? He is the one who is sick!" Wáng, upon seeing Gāo, again shouted, "Indeed, it is an evil fox! It has indeed arrived. Why use other methods to summon it?" The two of them were in disarray, continuously insulting each other. The Péi family was greatly shocked. Suddenly, a Taoist priest arrived at the door, privately telling the servant, "I have heard that Mr. Péi has a son afflicted by a fox. I am skilled at seeing ghosts. Just tell him, and I will come in to pay respects." The servant rushed to tell Mr. Péi, who went out to speak about the matter. The Taoist priest said, "It is easy to deal with." Upon entering, he saw the two men, who again insulted him, saying, "This is also an evil fox! How can a Taoist priest deceive people?" The Taoist priest also scolded them, saying, "The fox should return to the countryside among tombs. Why trouble people?" They then closed the door and began to fight. After a few meals' time, Mr. Péi became even more afraid. His servant was panicked and confused, with no solution. By evening, there was complete silence, and no sound was heard. When they opened the door to look, the three foxes were lying on the ground, panting and unable to move. Mr. Péi beat them to death with a whip. His son recovered after a few months.

    264

    ○计真
    　　唐元和中，有计真，家侨青齐间。尝西游长安，至陕。真与陕従事善，是日将告去，従事留饮酒，至暮方与别。及行未十里，遂兀然堕马，而二仆驱其衣囊前去矣。及真醉寤，已曛黑，马亦先去，因顾道佐小迳有马溺，即往寻之。不觉数里，忽见朱门甚高，槐柳森然。真既亡仆马，怅然，遂叩其门。已扃键，有小童出视，真即问曰：“此谁氏居？”曰：“李外郎别墅。”真请入谒。僮遽以告之。顷之，令人请客入息于宾馆。

    Explanation

    264

    During the middle of the Yuanhe period of the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Jì Zhēn, who originally hailed from the region of Qing and Qi. He once traveled westward to Chang'an and arrived in Shaanxi. Jì Zhēn was on good terms with an official there, and on the day he was about to depart, the official invited him to drink. They did not part ways until late in the evening. After traveling for just ten miles, Jì Zhēn suddenly fell off his horse, and his two servants drove away his belongings. By the time Jì Zhēn woke up from his drunken stupor, it was already dusk, and his horse had also left. He looked back along the path and saw horse urine on a small trail, so he followed it. Unaware of how far he had walked, he suddenly came upon a grand entrance with a very high red gate, surrounded by lush locust and willow trees. Jì Zhēn, having lost his servants and horse, felt despondent and proceeded to knock on the door. It was already locked, and a small child came out to see him. Jì Zhēn immediately asked, "Whose residence is this?" The child replied, "It is the villa of Officer Li." Jì Zhēn then requested to enter and pay his respects. The child hastily informed the owner. Shortly after, someone was sent to invite Jì Zhēn to rest in the guesthouse.

    265

    即引入门，其左有宾位，甚清敞，所设屏障，皆古山水及名画图、经籍、茵榻之类，率洁而不华。真坐久之，小僮出曰：“主君且至。”俄有一丈夫，年约五十，朱绂银章，仪状甚伟，与生相见，揖让而坐。生因具述：“従事故人留饮酒，道中沈醉，不觉曛黑，仆马俱失，原寓此一夕，可乎？”李曰：“但虑此卑隘，不可安贵客，宁有间耶！”真愧谢之。李又曰：“某尝従事于蜀，寻以疾罢去，今则归休于是矣。”因与议语，甚敏博。真颇慕之。又命家僮访真仆马，俄而皆至，即舍之。既而设馔共食。食竟，饮酒数杯而寐。明日，真晨起告去，李曰：“愿更得一日，侍欢笑。”生感其意，即留。明日乃别。知

    Explanation

    265

    Immediately led into the house, to the left was a guest seat, which was very clean and spacious. The screens and partitions set up were adorned with ancient landscapes, famous paintings, books, and cushions, generally clean but not overly ornate. Zhen sat there for a long time. A young servant came out and said, "The master is about to arrive." Soon, a man appeared, about fifty years old, wearing a red sash and silver seal, with a very dignified and imposing appearance. He met Zhen, and after exchanging bows, they sat down. Zhen then explained, "I stayed behind to drink with an old friend and got so drunk on the way that I didn't notice it was getting dark, and both my servant and horse were lost. May I stay here for one night, please?" Li said, "I only worry that this place is humble and small, unsuitable for a distinguished guest. Is there any other room?" Zhen felt ashamed and thanked him. Li added, "I once served in Shu, but soon resigned due to illness. Now I have retired here." They then engaged in conversation, and Li was very intelligent and knowledgeable. Zhen admired him greatly. Li then ordered the servant to find Zhen's servant and horse, and soon they all arrived. He left them there. Afterward, they prepared a meal and ate together. After finishing the meal, they drank several cups of wine and fell asleep. The next morning, Zhen got up early and bid farewell. Li said, "I wish to stay one more day, to enjoy your company." Zhen was moved by his kindness and stayed. The next day they parted.

    266

    及至京师，居月余，有款其门者，自称进士独孤沼。真延坐与语，甚聪辩，且谓曰：“某家于陕，昨西来，过李外郎。谈君之美不暇，且欲与君为姻好，故令某奉谒话此意。君以为何如？”喜而诺之。沼曰：“某今还陕。君东归，当更访外郎，且谢其意也。”遂别去。后旬月，生还，诣外郎别墅。李见真至，大喜。生即话独孤沼之言，因谢之。李遂留生，卜日就礼。妻色甚姝，且聪敏柔婉。生留旬月，乃挈妻孥归青齐。自是，李君音耗不绝
    　　生奉道，每晨起阅《黄庭内景经》，李氏常止之曰：“君好道，宁如秦皇汉武乎求仙之力，又孰若秦皇汉武乎彼二人贵为天子，富有四海，竭天下之财以学神仙，尚崩于沙丘，葬于茂陵。况君一布衣，而乃惑于求仙耶！”真叱之，乃终卷。意其知道者，亦不疑为他类也。后岁余，真挈家调选，至陕郊，李君留其女而遣生来京师。明年秋，授兖州参军，李氏随之官。数年，罢秩归齐鲁

    Explanation

    266

    When he arrived in the capital city, he lived there for more than a month. One day, someone knocked on his door and introduced himself as the scholar-official Dugu Zhao. Zhen invited him to sit and talk. Dugu Zhao was very intelligent and eloquent, and he said to Zhen, "I come from Shaanxi. I came from the west yesterday and visited Li, the scholar-official. He spoke of your virtues without pause and wishes to make a marital alliance with you. So he sent me to convey this intention. What do you think about it?" Zhen was pleased and agreed. Dugu Zhao said, "I am returning to Shaanxi today. You are heading east. You should visit Li again and thank him for his intentions." He then left.

    After ten days or so, Zhen returned and visited Li's villa. Li saw Zhen arrive and was very happy. Zhen immediately told Li what Dugu Zhao had said and thanked him for it. Li then kept Zhen and chose a date for the ceremony. His wife was very beautiful and was also intelligent, sensitive, and gentle. Zhen stayed for ten days or so, then took his wife and children back to Qing and Qi. From then on, Li's news and letters did not cease.

    Zhen practiced Daoism and would read the Huangting Neijing every morning. Li often stopped him and said, "You like Daoism. Would you be like Emperor Qin or Emperor Han? The effort to seek immortality. Who could match the efforts of Emperor Qin or Emperor Han? Those two were emperors, rich with all the seas, and spent all their wealth to learn immortality. They still died at Shaoqiu and were buried at Maoling. As for you, a commoner, you are still confused by seeking immortality?" Zhen scolded him and then finished reading. Thinking he was knowledgeable, he did not suspect him to be of another kind. After a year or so, Zhen took his family to participate in the imperial examination and reached the outskirts of Shaanxi. Li kept his daughter and sent Zhen to the capital city. The following autumn, he was appointed as a military officer in Yan Prefecture, and Li followed him to his post. After several years, he retired and returned to Qing and Qi.

    267

    又十余年，李有七子二女，才质姿貌，皆居众人先，而李容色端丽，无姝少年时。生益锺念之。无何，被疾且甚，生奔走医巫，无所不至，终不愈。一旦，屏人握生手，呜咽流涕，自言曰：“妾自知死至，然忍羞以心曲告君，幸君宽罪宥戾，使得尽言。”已，嘘欷不自胜。生亦为之泣，因慰之。乃曰：“一言诚自知受责于君，顾九稚子犹在，以为君累，尚敢一发口。且妾非人间人，天命当与君偶，得以狐狸贱质奉箕帚二十年，未尝纤芥获罪，惧以他类贻君忧。一女子血诚自谓竭尽。今日求去，不敢以妖幻余气托君，念稚弱满眼，皆世间人，为嗣续。及某气尽，愿少念弱子心，无以枯骨为仇，得全支体，埋之土中，乃百生之赐也。”言终，又悲恸，泪百行下。生惊悦伤感，咽不能语。相对泣良久，以被蒙首，背壁卧，食顷无声。生遂发被，见一狐死被中。生特感悼之，为之敛，葬之，制皆如人

    Explanation

    267

    Another ten years passed, and Li had seven sons and two daughters, all of whom surpassed the common people in talent, appearance, and demeanor. Yet, Li herself retained a dignified and beautiful appearance, though not as youthful as before. The man grew even more attached to her. Soon after, Li fell gravely ill, and the man sought out doctors and sorcerers, trying every possible remedy, but she did not recover. One day, she privately held the man's hand, sobbing and weeping, and said to herself, "I know death is near, but I endure shame to reveal my innermost thoughts to you. I hope you will forgive my faults and allow me to speak my mind fully." Afterward, she sobbed uncontrollably. The man was also moved to tears and comforted her. She then said, "I know I will be blamed for speaking up, but considering that nine young children are still alive, they are a burden to you, and I dare to speak once more. And I am not an ordinary human; fate has destined me to be your companion. For twenty years, I have served you with my humble fox form, and I have never caused any offense. I fear that other creatures might bring you sorrow. A woman's sincere heart believes it has been fully devoted. Today, I seek to depart, and I dare not leave behind my remaining supernatural essence with you. Considering the many young ones, all are ordinary humans, to continue the lineage. When I am gone, I hope you will remember the young ones, do not view my bones as an enemy, and allow my body to be buried in the earth. It will be a great gift for a hundred lifetimes." After finishing her words, she sobbed again, with tears streaming down her face. The man was shocked, pleased, and saddened, unable to speak. They wept together for a long time. She then covered her head with a blanket and lay facing the wall, and after a while, there was no sound. The man then uncovered the blanket and saw a dead fox in it. He was particularly moved and saddened, and he buried the fox, performing the burial rites as if for a human.

    268

    礼讫，生径至陕，访李氏居，墟墓荆棘，阒无所见，惆怅还家。居岁余，七子二女相次而卒，视其骸，皆人也。而终无恶心。知
    ○尹瑗
    　　尹瑗者，尝举进士，不中第，为太原晋阳尉。既罢秩，退居郊野，以文墨自适。忽一日，有白衣丈夫来谒，自称：“吴兴朱氏子，早岁嗜学，窃闻明公以文业自负，愿质疑于执事，无见拒。”瑗即延入与语，且徵其说。云：“家侨岚川，早岁与御史王君皆至北门，今者寓迹于王氏别业累年。”自此，每四日辄一来。甚敏辩纵横，词意典雅。瑗深爱之。瑗因谓曰：“吾子机辩玄奥，可以従郡国之游，为公侯高客。何乃自取沈滞，隐迹丛莽？”生曰：“余非不原谒公侯，且惧旦夕有不虞之祸。

    Explanation

    268

    The ceremony concluded, the man went straight to Shan, visiting the residence of the Li family. The burial grounds were overgrown with brambles, silent and empty, with nothing to be seen, and he returned home with a feeling of melancholy. After living there for over a year, seven sons and two daughters died one after another. When he looked at their remains, they were all human, and ultimately, they had no evil intentions.

    ○ Yin Yuan

    Yin Yuan, once having taken the imperial examination but not passing, served as the magistrate of Jinyang in Taiyuan. After retiring from office, he retired to the countryside, finding pleasure in literature and writing. One day, a plain-clothed man came to visit him, introducing himself as: "A son of the Zhu family from Wu County, who loved learning since early years. I have heard that the respected scholar takes pride in his literary achievements and wish to ask questions of you, please do not reject me." Yin Yuan invited him in to talk and inquired about his views. He said: "I lived in Lan River, and in early years, I visited the northern gate with Inspector Wang. Now, I have been living in Wang's subsidiary estate for many years." From then on, he came every four days. He was sharp and profound in argument, with elegant and dignified speech. Yin Yuan deeply admired him. Yin Yuan then said: "Your sharp and profound arguments can make you travel to various regions, becoming a distinguished guest of princes and dukes. Why do you choose to remain obscure, hiding in the wilderness?" The man replied: "I am not unwilling to visit princes and dukes, but I fear unforeseen dangers every day."

    269

    ”瑗曰：“何为发不祥之言乎？”朱曰：“某自今岁来，梦卜有穷尽之兆。”瑗即以词慰谕之。生颇有愧也。后至重阳日，有人以浓酝一瓶遗瑗，朱生亦至，因以酒饮之。初辞以疾，不敢饮，已而又曰：“佳节相遇，岂敢不尽主人之欢耶！”即引满而饮，食顷，大醉，告去。未行数十步，忽仆于地，化为一老狐，酩酊不能动矣。瑗即杀之。因访王御史别墅，有老农谓瑗曰：“王御史，并之裨将，往岁戍于岚川，为狐媚病而卒，已累年矣。”墓于村北数十步。即命家僮寻御史墓，果有穴。瑗后为御史，窃话其事，时唐太和初也

    Explanation

    269

    Aun said, "Why speak of ill omens?" Zhu replied, "Since this year, my dreams and divinations have shown signs of an end." Aun then comforted him with words. The student felt somewhat ashamed. Later, on the Double Ninth Festival, someone gave Aun a bottle of strong wine. Zhu also came, and Aun offered him the wine. Initially, Zhu declined, saying he was unwell and dared not drink. But then he said, "On such a good festival, how could I not enjoy the host's hospitality?" He took a full drink and, after a short while, became drunk and left. Not having walked more than a dozen steps, he suddenly fell to the ground and transformed into an old fox, drunk and immobile. Aun immediately killed it. He then visited Wang the magistrate's former residence, where an old farmer told him, "Wang the magistrate was a deputy general who was stationed in Lan Prefecture years ago and died from a fox spirit illness. It has been many years since then." The tomb was located several steps north of the village. Aun then ordered his servant to find the magistrate's tomb, and indeed, there was a grave. Later, Aun became a magistrate and secretly recounted this event. It was at the beginning of the Taiping era of the Tang dynasty.

    270

    ○韦氏子
    　　杜陵韦氏子，家于韩城，有别墅在邑北十余里。开成十年秋，自邑中游焉。日暮，见一妇人，素衣，挈一瓢，自北而来，谓韦曰：“妾居邑北里中有年矣，家甚贫，今为里胥所辱，将讼于官。幸吾子纸笔书其事，妾得以执诣邑，冀雪其耻。”韦诺之。妇人即揖韦坐田野，衣出一酒卮，曰：“瓢中有酒，愿与吾于尽醉。”于是注酒一饮韦。韦方举卮，会有猎骑従西来，引数犬。妇人望见，即东走数十步，化为一狐。韦大恐，视手中卮，乃一髑髅，酒若牛溺之状。韦因病热，月余方瘳

    Explanation

    270

    ○Wei's Son
    　　The son of the Wei family from Dulings lived in Hancheng and had a villa about ten miles north of the town. In the autumn of the tenth year of the Kai Cheng era, he went from the town to visit his villa. As evening fell, he saw a woman dressed in white, carrying a gourd, coming from the north. She said to Wei, "I have lived in this village for many years, and my family is very poor. Today, I have been insulted by the village headman and am planning to sue him in court. I hope you, sir, will write down my story with paper and ink, so I can present it to the town officials and hope to clear my name." Wei agreed. The woman then bowed to Wei and invited him to sit in the fields, and she produced a wine cup from her clothes, saying, "There is wine in the gourd. I wish to drink to our hearts' content." She then poured the wine and offered it to Wei. As Wei was about to raise the cup, a hunting party came from the west, leading several dogs. The woman saw them and immediately ran eastward for several dozen steps, transforming into a fox. Wei was greatly frightened and looked at the cup in his hand, which turned out to be a skull, and the wine was like cow urine. Wei became ill with a fever and recovered after more than a month.

    271

    ○兴福寺
    　　长安兴福寺有十光佛院，其院宇极壮丽，云是隋所制。贞观中，寺僧以其年纪绵远，虑有摧圯，即经费计工，且欲新其土木。乃将毁撤，既启户，见有蛇万数，连贯在地。蛇蟠绕如积，摇首呿喙，若吞噬之状。寺僧大惧，以为天悯重劳，故假灵变，于是不敢除毁
    ○李林甫（三）
    　　李林甫宅即李靖宅。有泓师者，以道术闻于睿宗时，尝与过其宅，谓人曰：“后之人有能居此者，贵不可言。”其后久无居人。开元初，林甫官为奉御，遂従而居焉。人有告于泓师，曰：“异乎哉，吾言果验。是十有九年居相位，称豪贵于天下者，此人也。虽然，吾惧其易制中门，则祸且及矣。”林甫果相玄宗，恃权贵，为人觖望者久之。及末年，有人献良马甚高，而其门稍庳，不可乘以过，遂易而制。既毁其檐，忽有蛇千万数在屋瓦中。林甫恶之，即罢而不能毁焉。未几，林甫竟籍没。其始相至籍没，果十九年矣

    Explanation

    271

    ○兴福寺
    The Xingfu Temple in Chang'an has a Ten-Light Buddha Hall, whose buildings are extremely grand and beautiful, said to have been built during the Sui dynasty. During the Zhenguan era, the temple monks, considering its age, feared it might collapse, so they immediately calculated the expenses and labor and planned to renovate the wooden structures. As they were about to demolish and rebuild, when they opened the door, they saw thousands of snakes lined up on the ground. The snakes coiled and piled up like a heap, shaking their heads and opening their mouths, as if they were about to devour something. The monks were greatly afraid, thinking that heaven pitied the heavy labor and had temporarily manifested as a miracle, so they dared not remove or destroy the snakes.

    ○李林甫（三）
    Li Linfu's residence was originally Li Jing's residence. There was a Daoist master named Hong Shi, famous for his Daoist arts during the reign of Emperor Ruizong. He once passed by the residence and told someone, "Those who live here in the future will be extremely honored." For a long time afterward, no one lived there. In the early Kaiyuan era, Li Linfu held the position of Fengyu (Imperial Attendant) and moved in. Someone reported to Hong Shi, saying, "How strange! My words have indeed come true. He will hold the position of chancellor for nineteen years and be renowned as a powerful and honored person throughout the world. However, I fear that if he changes the middle gate, disaster will befall him." Li Linfu indeed became chancellor to Emperor Xuanzong, relying on his power and status, and people had been disappointed by him for a long time. By the end of his reign, someone presented a very tall good horse, but the gate was slightly low and the horse could not pass through. So he changed and rebuilt it. After demolishing the eaves, suddenly there were thousands of snakes in the roof tiles. Li Linfu disliked this and stopped but could not destroy them. Not long after, Li Linfu was eventually dispossessed. From the beginning of his chancellorship to his dispossesion, it was indeed for nineteen years.

    272

    ○韦于春
    　　临淮郡有馆亭，滨泅水上。亭有大木，周数十栱，突然劲拔，阴合百步。往往有甚风迅雷，夕发其中。人望见亭有二光，对而上下，赫然若电。风既息，其光亦闭。开元中，有韦子春，以勇力闻。会子春客于临淮，有人语其事者。子春曰：“吾能伺之。”于是挈衣橐止于亭中以伺焉。后一夕，遂有大风雷震于地，亭屋摇撼，果见二光照跃亭宇。子春乃敛衣而下，忽觉有物蟠绕其身，冷如冰冻，束不可解。回视见二光在其身后。子春即奋身挥臂，■然有声，其缚亦解。遂归亭中。未几而风雨霁，闻亭中腥若鲍肆，明日视之，见一巨蛇中断而毙，血遍其地。里人相与来观，谓子春且死矣，乃见之大惊。自是其亭无风雷患。知

    Explanation

    272

    The Pavilion of Wei Yu Chun

    In the region of Linhuai, there was a pavilion by the side of the Sui River. The pavilion was surrounded by a large tree, its circumference spanning dozens of gong, suddenly growing strong and tall, casting shade over a hundred steps. Often, there were fierce winds and thunderstorms that would erupt within the pavilion. People would see two lights within the pavilion, rising and falling, shining brightly like lightning. When the wind subsided, the lights would also disappear. During the Kaiyuan era, there was a man named Wei Yu Chun, known for his bravery and strength. One day, while traveling in Linhuai, Wei Yu Chun heard of this phenomenon. He said, "I can observe it." Thus, he carried his clothes and bag and stayed in the pavilion to wait. One evening, a fierce storm with thunder and lightning suddenly struck the ground, shaking the pavilion. Indeed, he saw two lights moving within the pavilion. Wei Yu Chun then gathered his clothes and descended, suddenly feeling something coiled around him, cold as ice, tightly bound and unable to be untied. Looking back, he saw the two lights behind him. Wei Yu Chun then struggled and swung his arms, making a sound, and the binding was released. He returned to the pavilion. Shortly after, the storm cleared up, and he smelled a fishy odor within the pavilion, like that of a fish market. The next day, he saw a huge snake cut in half and dead, with blood covering the ground. The locals came to see, thinking Wei Yu Chun was going to die, but they were greatly surprised to see him alive. From then on, the pavilion no longer suffered from storms.

    273

    ○无畏师
    　　天宝中，无畏师在洛。是时有巨蛇，状甚异，高丈余，围五十尺，魁魁若盘绕，出于山下，洛民咸见之。于是无畏曰：“后此蛇决水潴洛城。”即说佛书义甚精。蛇至夕则驾风露来，若倾听状。无畏乃责之曰：“尔蛇也，营居深山中固安，其所何为，将欲肆毒于世即速去，无患生人。”其蛇闻之，遂俯于地，若有惭色，须臾而死焉。其后禄山据洛阳，尽毁宫庙，果无畏所谓决洛水潴城之应
    ○利州李录事
    　　开成中，有陇西李生为利州录事参军，居于官舍中。尝晓起见蛇数百在庭，生大惧，尽命弃于郊野外。其明旦，群蛇又集于庭。生益惧之，且异也，亦命弃去。后一日，群蛇又至，李生惊曰：“岂天将祸我乎？”戚其容者且久。后旬余，生以脏罪闻于刺史，遣吏至门，将按其罪，且闻于天子。生惶骇无以自安，缢于庭树，绝脰而死。生有妻，感生不得其死，亦自缢焉。于是其家僮震慴，委身于井者且数辈。果符蛇见之祸。刺史即李行枢也

    Explanation

    273

    ○ Fearless Master

    During the Tianbao era, the Fearless Master was in Luoyang. At that time, there was a giant snake, its appearance very unusual, over ten feet tall, with a circumference of fifty feet, coiling like a massive serpent, emerging from the mountains. The people of Luoyang all saw it. The Fearless Master then said, "After this, this snake will break the water and flood the city of Luoyang." He immediately expounded on the profound meanings of Buddhist scriptures. At dusk, the snake would come riding the wind and mist, as if listening attentively. The Fearless Master then rebuked it, saying, "You, the snake, dwell securely in the deep mountains; what are you doing here? If you intend to spread poison in the world, then go quickly; do not harm the living." Upon hearing this, the snake bowed its head to the ground, as if showing shame, and died shortly thereafter. Later, An Lushan occupied Luoyang, destroying all the palaces and temples, fulfilling the prophecy of the Fearless Master that the waters of the Luo River would flood the city.

    ○ Li the Recorder of Lishui

    During the Kai Cheng era, there was a man named Li from Longxi who served as the Recorder of Lishui, residing in the official residence. One morning, he saw hundreds of snakes in the courtyard and was greatly frightened, ordering them to be thrown into the outskirts. The next morning, the snakes gathered again in the courtyard. Li was even more afraid and found this strange, so he also ordered them to be thrown away. The following day, the snakes returned again. Li was shocked and said, "Is it that heaven is bringing disaster upon me?" His expression was sad for a long time. After more than ten days, Li was accused of a crime and reported to the governor, who sent officials to his door to investigate his guilt and report to the emperor. Li was terrified and unable to find peace, so he hanged himself from a tree in the courtyard and died by cutting off his neck. Li had a wife who felt that her husband did not die a natural death and also hanged herself. As a result, the household servants were terrified and several of them threw themselves into the well. It truly matched the disaster predicted by the appearance of the snakes. The governor was Li Xingshu.

    274

    ○睢阳凤
    　　贞元十四年秋，有异鸟，其色青，状类鸠鹊，翔于睢阳之郊，止丛木中。有群鸟千类，俱率其类，列于左右前后，而又朝夕各衔蜚虫稻梁以献焉。是鸟每飞，则群鸟咸噪而导其前，咸翼其旁，咸拥其后，若传唤惊卫之状。止则环而向焉，虽人臣侍天子之礼，无以加矣。睢阳人咸适野纵观，以为羽族之灵者。然其状不类鸾凤，由是益奇之。时李翱客于睢阳，翱曰：“此真凤鸟也。”于是作《知凤》一章，备书其事。知

    Explanation

    274

    In the autumn of the fourteenth year of the Zhenyuan era, an unusual bird with a green color and a shape resembling a magpie or jay flew over the outskirts of Suizhou and landed in a thicket of trees. A multitude of birds of various kinds, all leading their own species, arranged themselves around the central bird, both in front and behind. Every morning and evening, the birds would each carry insects and grains to offer to the central bird. Whenever this bird flew, the surrounding birds would all chirp and lead the way in front, flanking it on the sides, and following behind, as if guarding or protecting it. When the birds stopped, they would circle around and face the central bird, their behavior resembling the respectful etiquette of ministers serving the emperor, which is unparalleled in dignity. The people of Suizhou all went out to the fields to watch the spectacle, considering it a manifestation of the most auspicious of all birds. However, its appearance did not resemble that of a phoenix, which made it even more extraordinary. At the time, Li Ao was a guest in Suizhou. Li Ao said, "This is indeed a phoenix." He then composed a chapter titled "Knowledge of the Phoenix," fully recording the event.

    275

    ○邺郡人
    　　薛嵩镇魏时，邺郡人有好育鹰隼者。一日，有人持鹰来，告于邺人，人遂市之。其鹰甚神俊，邺人家所育鹰隼极多，皆莫能比，常臂以玩不去手。后有东夷人见者，请以缯百余段为直。曰：“吾方念此不知其所用。”其人曰：“此海鹞也，善辟蛟螭患，君宜于邺城南放之，可以见其用矣。”先是，邺城南陂蛟常为人患，郡民苦之有年矣。邺人遂持往。海鹞忽投陂水中，顷之乃出，得一小蛟。既出，食之且尽。自是邺民免其患。有告于嵩，乃命邺人讯其事，邺人遂以海鹞献焉。知

    Explanation

    275

    A Man from Yecheng

    During the time when Xue Song was stationed in Wei, there was a man from Yecheng who enjoyed raising falcons and hawks. One day, someone brought a falcon to him and informed the man from Yecheng, who then bought it. The falcon was extremely spirited and agile, and the man from Yecheng had raised many falcons and hawks, none of which could compare to this one. He would often carry it on his arm for play, never letting it out of his hand. Later, an Eastern barbarian saw it and offered to pay more than a hundred pieces of silk for it. The man from Yecheng replied, "I am just thinking about this and don't know what use it will have." The Eastern barbarian said, "This is a sea hawk, good at warding off the dangers of dragons and chimeras. You should release it in the south of Yecheng to see its use." Before this, the dragon in the southern pond of Yecheng had been a constant nuisance to the people of the city for many years. The man from Yecheng then took the falcon there. The sea hawk suddenly dove into the pond, and after a while, it emerged with a small dragon in its beak. After emerging, it finished eating the dragon. From then on, the people of Yecheng were free from the dragon's troubles. Someone reported this to Xue Song, and he ordered the man from Yecheng to investigate the matter. The man from Yecheng then presented the sea hawk to Xue Song.

    276

    ○周氏子
    　　汝南周氏子，吴郡人也，亡其名，家于昆山县。元和中，以明经上第。调选，得尉昆山。既之官，未至邑数十里，舍于逆旅中。夜梦一丈夫，衣白，仪状甚秀，而血濡衣襟，若伤其臆者。既拜而泣，谓周生曰：“吾家于林泉者也。以不尚尘俗，故得安其所，有年矣。今以偶行田野间，不幸值君之家僮，有系吾者。吾本逸人也，既为所系，心甚不乐。又纵狂犬噬吾臆，不胜其愤。愿君子悯而宥之。不然，则死在朝夕矣。”周生曰：“谨受教，不敢忘。”言讫忽寤，心窃异之。明日，至其家。是夕，又梦白衣来，曰：“吾前以事诉君，幸君怜而诺之，然今尚为所系。顾君不易仁人之心，疾为我解其缚，使不为君家囚，幸矣。”周即问曰：“然则尔之名氏可得闻乎？”其人曰：“我，鸟也。”言已遂去。又明日，周生乃以梦语家僮，且以事讯之。乃家人因适野，遂获一鹅，乃笼归。前夕，有犬伤其臆。周生即命放之。是夕，又梦白衣人辞谢而去。知

    Explanation

    276

    A Man from the Zhou Family

    A man from the Zhou family in Runan, originally from Wu County, but having lost his name, lived in Kunshan County. During the Yuanhe era, he passed the Mingjing examination with distinction and was appointed as a magistrate of Kunshan. After setting out for his post, he stayed at an inn not far from the town. That night, he dreamed of a man dressed in white, with a very handsome appearance, and blood staining his collar, as if he had been injured in the chest. The man bowed and wept, saying to the Zhou man, "I am a recluse who lives in the mountains and forests. Because I do not value worldly affairs, I have been able to live in peace for many years. Now, while walking in the fields, I unfortunately encountered one of your household servants, who tied me up. I am a recluse by nature, and being bound causes me great distress. Moreover, a mad dog bit my chest, which filled me with anger. I hope you will pity me and pardon me. Otherwise, I will die soon." The Zhou man replied, "I will follow your advice and never forget it." After speaking, he woke up suddenly, feeling quite surprised. The next day, he arrived at his home. That evening, he dreamed again of the white-clad man, who said, "I previously told you about my situation, and you kindly promised to help me. However, I am still bound. Please do not change your kind heart. Quickly free me from my bonds so that I am not kept as a prisoner in your household. That would be fortunate." The Zhou man then asked, "Then, may I know your name and surname?" The man replied, "I am a bird." He said this and then left. The next day, the Zhou man told his servants about the dream and asked them about the incident. It turned out that one of the family members had gone into the fields and caught a goose, which they brought back in a cage. The night before, a dog had bitten the goose in the chest. The Zhou man immediately ordered the goose to be released. That evening, he dreamed again of the white-clad man, who thanked him and then left.

    277

    ○吕生妻
    　　东平吕生，鲁国人，家于郑。其妻黄氏，病将死，告于姑曰：“妾病且死。然闻人死当为鬼，妾常恨人鬼不相通，使存者益哀。今姑念妾深，妾死，必能以梦告于姑矣。”及其死，姑梦见黄氏来，泣而言曰：“妾平生时无状，今为异类，生于郑之东野丛木中，黰其翼、嗷其鸣者，当是也。后七日，当来谒姑。愿姑念平生时，无以异类见阻。”言讫，遂去。后七日，果一鸟自东来，至吕氏家，止于庭树，哀鸣久之。其姑泣而言曰：“果吾之梦矣。汝无昧平素，直来吾之居也。”其鸟即飞入堂中，回翔哀唳，仅食顷，方东向而去。知

    Explanation

    277

    吕生的妻子

    东平的吕生，是鲁国人，居住在郑地。他的妻子黄氏，病重将死，对她的婆婆说：“我病重将死。然而我听说人死后会变成鬼，我常常遗憾人鬼之间不能相通，使得活着的人更加悲伤。现在婆婆您对我如此疼爱，我死后，一定能通过梦境告诉您。”等到她死后，婆婆梦见黄氏前来，哭泣着说：“我一生中行为不端，现在成了异类，生在郑地东边的荒野中，有黑色的翅膀、发出哀鸣的鸟，就是我。七天后，我会来拜访您。希望婆婆您能记得我生前的情谊，不要因为我现在是异类而拒绝我。”说完，便离开了。七天后，果然有一只鸟从东方飞来，到了吕家，停在庭院的树上，哀鸣了很久。婆婆哭泣着说：“果然是我的梦啊。你不要隐藏你的本性，直接到我家来。”那只鸟随即飞进堂中，盘旋哀鸣，只过了片刻，便向东飞走了。

    278

    ○韦氏子
    　　汧阳郡有张女郎庙。上元中，有韦氏子容于汧阳，途至其庙，遂解鞍以憩。忽见庙宇中有二屐子在地上。生视之，乃结草成者，文理甚细，色白而制度极妙。韦生乃收贮于橐中，既而别去。及至郡，郡守舍韦生于馆亭中。是夕，生以所得屐致于前而寐。明日，已亡所在，莫穷其处。仅食顷，乃于馆亭瓦屋上得焉。仆者惊愕，告于韦生，生即命升屋而取之。既得，又致于前。明日，又失其所，复于瓦屋上得之。如是者三。韦生窃谓仆曰：“此其怪乎可潜伺之。”是夕，其仆乃窃于隙中伺之。夜将半，其屐忽化为白鸟，飞于屋上。韦生命取焚之，乃飞去。知

    Explanation

    278

    ○Wei Shizi Rong
    In Qianyang County, there was a temple dedicated to Lady Zhang. During the Shangyuan era, Wei Shizi Rong of the Wei clan was traveling through Qianyang and came to the temple, where he untied his saddle and rested. Suddenly, he saw two sandals made of grass on the ground inside the temple. He examined them and found that they were intricately crafted, with fine texture, white in color, and of exquisite design. Wei Shizi Rong then stored them in his bag and left. Upon reaching the county, the governor housed him in a pavilion. That evening, he placed the sandals in front of him and went to sleep. The next day, they had disappeared, and he could not find them. After only a meal's time, they reappeared on the roof of the pavilion. The servant was startled and informed Wei Shizi Rong, who immediately ordered him to climb the roof and retrieve them. After retrieving them, he placed them in front of him again. The next day, they disappeared once more and reappeared on the roof of the pavilion. This happened three times. Wei Shizi Rong whispered to his servant, "This must be a supernatural occurrence; we should secretly observe it." That evening, the servant secretly watched from a crack. As the night grew late, the sandals suddenly transformed into a white bird and flew onto the roof. Wei Shizi Rong ordered it to be burned, and the bird flew away.

    279

    ○韩愈（三）
    　　唐吏部侍郎韩文公愈，自刑部侍郎贬潮阳守。先是，郡西有大湫，湫有鳄鱼，约百余尺。每一怒，则湫水腾荡，林岭如震。民之马牛有滨其水者，辄吸而噬之，不瞬而尽。为所害者，莫可胜计。民患之有年矣。及愈刺郡，既至之三日，问民不便事，俱曰：“郡西湫中之鳄鱼也。”愈曰：“吾闻至诚感神：昔鲁恭宰中牟，雉驯而蝗避；黄霸治九江，虎皆遁去。是知政之所感，故能化禽兽矣。”即命庭掾以牢醴陈于湫之旁，且祝曰：“汝，水族也，无为生人患。”既而沃以酒。是夕，郡西有风雷声，动山野，迨夜分霁焉。明日，里民视其湫，水已竭。公命使穷其迹，至湫西六十里易地为湫，巨鳄亦随而徙焉。自是郡民获免其患。故工部郎中皇甫湜撰愈“神道碑”，叙曰：“刑部为潮阳守，云‘洞獠海夷，陶然皆化；鳄鱼稻蟹，不暴民物’。”盖谓此矣

    Explanation

    279

    Han Yu, a high-ranking official of the Tang Dynasty, was demoted from his position as Vice Minister of Justice to the governor of Chaozhou. Prior to his arrival, there was a large marsh on the west side of the city, inhabited by a crocodile about a hundred feet long. Whenever the crocodile became angry, the water in the marsh would surge violently, and the surrounding mountains and fields would shake. Any livestock that came near the water would be sucked up and eaten in an instant. The number of victims was countless, and the people had suffered from this for many years. Upon arriving in the region, Han Yu asked about the people's grievances, and they all replied, "It is the crocodile in the marsh on the west side of the city." Han Yu said, "I have heard that sincere devotion can move the spirits: in ancient times, Lu Gong, as the governor of Zhongmu, tamed wild pheasants and caused locusts to flee; Huang Ba, as the governor of Jiuzhou, drove away tigers. This shows that the influence of good governance can even transform wild animals." He then ordered the court officials to set up offerings of meat and wine near the marsh and prayed, saying, "You, a creature of the water, should not harm the people." Afterward, he poured wine over the offerings. That night, there was a wind and thunder in the west of the city, shaking the mountains and fields, and it cleared up by midnight. The next day, the local people saw that the marsh had dried up. The governor ordered someone to investigate the situation, and they found that sixty miles west, a new marsh had formed, and the large crocodile had moved there. From then on, the people of the region were free from this danger. The official of the Ministry of Works, Huang Fu Shi, wrote a Shendao stele for Han Yu, which recorded, "As the governor of Chaozhou, he said, 'The mountain tribes and sea barbarians were all transformed; the crocodiles, rice, and crabs no longer harmed the people's property.'" This is what is referred to here.

    280

    ○柳宗元
    　　唐柳州刺史河东柳宗元，常自省郎出为永州司马，途至荆门，舍驿亭中。是夕，梦一妇人，衣黄衣，再拜而泣曰：“某，家楚水者也。今不幸，死在朝夕，非君不能活之。傥获其生，不独戴恩而已，兼能假君禄，益君为将为相，且无难矣。幸明君子一图焉。”公谢而许之。既寤，嘿自异之。及再寐，又梦妇人且祈且谢，久而方去。明晨，有吏来，称荆帅命，将宴宗元。宗元既命驾，以天色尚早，因假寐焉。

    Explanation

    280

    ○ Liu Zongyuan
    Liu Zongyuan, the Tang dynasty prefect of Liuzhou from Hedong, often served as a provincial administrator in Yongzhou after being demoted from his post as a court official. On his journey, he arrived at Jingmen and stayed in the station pavilion. That evening, he dreamed of a woman dressed in yellow, who bowed twice and wept, saying: “I am one who dwells by the Chushui. Alas, I am now on the brink of death, and only you can save me. If you save my life, I will not only be grateful, but I can also bestow upon you official rank, helping you become a general or chancellor, and it will be no difficulty at all. I humbly request your noble gentleman to take this matter into consideration.” The official bowed in thanks and agreed. After waking, he silently found the dream strange. When he fell asleep again, he dreamed of the woman once more, who both prayed and thanked him, and only after a long while did she depart. The next morning, an official came and announced that the commander of Jingzhou had ordered a banquet for Liu Zongyuan. Liu Zongyuan had already ordered his carriage, but since the morning was still early, he pretended to sleep.

    281

    既而又梦妇人，颦然其容，忧惶不暇，顾谓宗元曰：“某之命，今若败缕之悬甚风，危危将断且飘矣。而君不能念其事之急耶幸疾为计。不尔，亦与败缕皆断矣。愿君子许之。”言已，又祈拜。既告去，心亦未悟焉，即俛而念曰：“吾一夕三梦妇人告我，辞甚恳。岂吾之吏有不平于人者耶抑将宴者以鱼为我膳耶得而活之，亦吾事也。”即命驾诣郡宴。既而以梦话荆帅，且召吏讯之。吏曰：“前一日，渔人网获一巨黄鳞鱼，将为膳，今已断其首。”宗元惊曰：“果其夕之梦。”遂命挈而投江中，然而其鱼已死矣。是夕，又梦妇人来，亡其首。宗元益异之

    Explanation

    281

    Not long after, he dreamed again of a woman, her face furrowed with sorrow, her expression filled with anxiety and fear, leaving no time for hesitation. She turned to Liu Zongyuan and said, “My fate is now as fragile as a thread about to be severed by the wind—dangerously close to breaking and drifting away. Will you not recognize the urgency of this matter? Please act swiftly. Otherwise, you too will be cut off, like the thread. I beg the gentleman to grant this.” After speaking, she again prayed and bowed. When she departed, his heart still had not grasped the meaning. He then lowered his head and mused, “I have dreamed of this woman three times in one night, and her words were very earnest. Is it that one of my officials has been unjust to someone? Or is it that the host of the banquet is serving me fish? To save it is also my duty.” He immediately ordered his carriage to go to the county banquet. Later, he spoke of his dream to the governor of Jing, and summoned officials to question them. The officials replied, “The day before, a fisherman had caught a large yellow-scaled fish, which he was about to serve as food, but now its head has been severed.” Zongyuan was startled and said, “Indeed, it was the dream of that night.” He then ordered that the fish be taken and cast into the river, but the fish was already dead. That same night, he dreamed again of the woman, who now lacked a head. Zongyuan was even more amazed by this.

    282

    ○柳沂
    　　唐河东柳沂者，侨居洛阳。因乘春钓伊水，得巨鱼，挈而归，致于盆水中。先是，沂有婴儿，始六七岁。是夕，沂梦鱼以喙啮婴儿臆。沂悸然而寤，果闻婴儿啼，曰向梦一大鱼啮其臆，痛不可忍，故啼焉。与沂梦同。沂异之，乃视婴儿之臆，果有疮而血。沂益惧。明旦，以鱼投伊水中，且命僧转经画像。仅旬余，婴儿疮愈。沂自后不复钓也。主

    Explanation

    282

    A Woman Named Liuyi

    During the Tang Dynasty, there was a man named Liuyi from Hedong who was a guest in Luoyang. One spring, he went fishing on the Yi River and caught a huge fish. He carried it back home and placed it in a basin of water. Before this event, Liuyi had a child who was six or seven years old. That night, Liuyi dreamed that the fish was biting the child's chest with its mouth. Liuyi woke up startled and indeed heard the child crying, saying that he had dreamed of a large fish biting his chest, causing unbearable pain, and thus he was crying. The child's dream was the same as Liuyi's. Liuyi found it strange and then looked at the child's chest and indeed found a wound with blood. Liuyi became even more afraid. The next morning, he threw the fish back into the Yi River and also ordered a monk to recite scriptures and draw a picture. After only ten days or so, the child's wound healed. Liuyi never fished again after that.

    283

    ○刘成
    　　宣城郡当涂民有刘成者、李晖者，俱不识农事，尝用巨舫载鱼蟹，鬻于吴越间。唐天宝十三年春三月，皆自新安江载往丹阳郡，行至下查浦，去宣城四十里，会天暮，泊舟，二人俱登陆。时李晖往浦岩村舍中，独刘成在江上。四顾云岛，阒无人迹，忽闻舫中有连呼阿弥陀佛者，声甚厉。成惊而视之，见一大鱼自航中振须摇首，人声而呼阿弥陀佛焉。成且惧且悚，毛发尽劲，即匿身芦中以伺之。俄而舫中万鱼，俱跳跃呼佛，声动地。成大恐，遽登舫，尽投群鱼于江中。有顷而李晖至，成具以告晖，晖怒曰：“坚子安得为妖妄乎？”唾而骂言且久。成无以自白，即用衣资酬其直。既而余百钱，易荻草十余束，致于岸。明日迁于舫中，忽觉重不可举，解而视之，得缗十五千，签题云：“归汝鱼直。”成益奇之。是日，于瓜洲会群僧食，并以缗施焉。时有万庄者，自泾阳令退居瓜洲，备得其事，传于纪述

    Explanation

    283

    Deng Cheng
    In the Dangtu County of Xuancheng Prefecture, there were two men named Deng Cheng and Li Hui, both of whom were ignorant of farming. They often used a large boat to carry fish and crabs, selling them in Wu and Yue. In the spring of the thirteenth year of the Tianbao era during the Tang Dynasty, they both set out from the Xin'an River, heading to DanYang Prefecture. They traveled to Xia Cha Pu, forty li from Xuancheng, when it got dark and they anchored their boat. Both men then disembarked. At that time, Li Hui went to a village in Pu Yan, while Deng Cheng remained on the shore. Looking around at the cloud-shrouded islands, there was no sign of human activity. Suddenly, Deng Cheng heard someone shouting "Amitabha" from the boat, the voice very loud. Startled, he looked and saw a large fish in the boat wagging its whiskers and shaking its head, calling out "Amitabha" in a human voice. Deng Cheng was both afraid and terrified, his hair standing on end, and he hid in the reeds to watch. Soon, all the fish in the boat jumped and called out to Buddha, the sound shaking the ground. Deng Cheng was greatly frightened and quickly climbed onto the boat, throwing all the fish into the river. After a while, Li Hui arrived, and Deng Cheng told him everything. Li Hui angrily said, "You fool, how could you be so foolish?" He spat and scolded him for a long time. Deng Cheng had no way to prove himself, so he used his clothes and money to pay for the fish. Later, he had some leftover money, which he used to buy more than ten bundles of reeds, bringing them to the shore. The next day, he moved the reeds into the boat, and suddenly felt it was too heavy to lift. He untied it and saw fifteen thousand strings of cash, with a note saying, "This is your payment for the fish." Deng Cheng was even more amazed. That day, at Guazhou, he met with a group of monks and ate with them, giving them the money as alms. At that time, there was a man named Wan Zhuang, who had retired from his position as magistrate of Jingyang and lived in Guazhou. He heard about the entire incident and recorded it.

    284

    ○李揆（二）
    　　唐李揆乾天中为礼部侍郎，尝一日昼坐于堂之前轩。忽闻堂中有声极震，若墙圮。揆惊入视之，见一虾蟆俯于地，高数尺，魅然殊状。揆且惊且异，莫穷其来。即命家童以巨缶盖焉。有解曰：“夫虾蟆者，月中之虫，亦天使也。今天使来公堂，岂非上帝以密命付公乎？”其明，启而视之，已亡见矣。后数日，果拜中书侍郎平章事
    ○石宪
    　　有石宪者，其籍编太原，以商为业，常货于代北。长庆二年夏中，雁门关行道中，时暑方盛，因偃大木下。忽梦一僧，蜂目，披褐衲，其状奇异，来宪前，谓宪曰：“我庐于五台山之南，有穷林积水，出尘俗甚远，实群僧清暑之地。檀越幸偕我而游乎即不能，吾见檀越病热且死，得无悔其心耶！”宪以时暑方盛，僧且以祸福语相动，因谓僧曰：“愿与师偕去。”于是其僧引宪西去，且数里，果有穷林积水，见群僧在水中。宪怪而问之，僧曰：“此玄阴池。故我徒浴于中，且以荡炎燠。”于是引宪环池行。

    Explanation

    284

    ○ Li Kui (Part II)
    　　During the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, Li Kui served as a Counselor of the Ministry of Rites. One day, he was sitting in the daytime in the front hall. Suddenly, he heard a sound in the hall that was extremely loud, as if a wall had collapsed. Li Kui was shocked and went in to see what it was. He saw a toad lying on the ground, several feet tall, with a strange and eerie appearance. Li Kui was both shocked and amazed, unable to understand where it had come from. He immediately ordered a servant to cover it with a large jar. Someone explained, "The toad is an insect from the moon, also a messenger of heaven. Today, the messenger has come to your hall; could it not be that the Heavenly Emperor has entrusted you with a secret command?" The next morning, when he opened the jar to look, the toad had already disappeared. A few days later, Li Kui was indeed appointed as a Counselor of the Secretariat and Chancellor of the State Affairs.

    ○ Shi Xian
    　　There was a person named Shi Xian, whose register was compiled in Taiyuan, and who engaged in commerce, often trading in Dai North. In the summer of the second year of the Changqing era, on the road through Yanmen Pass, the heat was intense, and Shi Xian happened to be resting under a large tree. Suddenly, he dreamed of a monk with bee-like eyes, wearing a simple robe, with a strange and unusual appearance. The monk approached Shi Xian and said, "I reside south of Wutai Mountain, where there is a dense forest and a pool of water, far removed from the mundane world, truly a place where monks can cool off in the heat. Venerable one, would you like to accompany me on a journey? If not, I foresee that you will fall ill with the heat and die; will you not regret your heart?" Shi Xian, thinking that the heat was intense, and the monk was trying to sway him with words of fortune and misfortune, replied, "I wish to accompany you, Master." The monk then led Shi Xian westward, and after walking several miles, there indeed was a dense forest and a pool of water, where he saw a group of monks in the water. Shi Xian was surprised and asked, "What is this Xuan Yin Pool? Why do your disciples bathe in it to cool off the heat?" The monk replied, "This is the Xuan Yin Pool. We bathe in it to cool off the heat." The monk then led Shi Xian to walk around the pool.

    285

    宪独怪群僧在水中，又其状貌无一异者。已而天暮，有一僧曰：“檀越可听吾徒之梵音也。”于是宪立池上，群僧即于水中合声而噪。仅食顷，有一僧挈手曰：“檀越与吾偕浴于玄阴池，慎无畏。”宪即随僧入池中，忽觉一身尽冷，噤而战。由是惊悟。见己卧于大木下，衣尽湿，而寒怵且甚。时已日暮，即抵村舍中。至明日，病稍愈。因行于道，闻道中有蛙鸣，甚类群僧之梵音。于是径往寻之，行数里，穷林积水，有蛙甚多。其水果谓玄阴池者，其僧乃群蛙耳。宪曰：“此蛙能易形以感于人，岂非怪尤者乎？”于是尽杀之。知

    Explanation

    285

    The author found it strange that the monks were in the water, and they all looked the same. As night fell, one monk said, "Patron, you may listen to our chanting." The author stood by the pool, and the monks chanted together in the water. After about an hour, one monk took his hand and said, "Patron, come with me to bathe in the Dark Pool. Please do not be afraid." The author followed the monk into the pool and suddenly felt extremely cold, shivering uncontrollably. He then woke up with a start. He found himself lying under a large tree, his clothes soaking wet, and he was extremely cold and frightened. By then, it was already evening, so he went to a nearby village. The next day, he felt slightly better. As he walked along the road, he heard frogs croaking, which sounded like the monks' chanting. He went directly to search for them and found them after walking several miles. There were many frogs in the dense forest and standing water, and these were indeed the Dark Pool. The author thought, "These frogs can change their form to influence people. Are they not especially strange or monstrous beings?" He then killed them all.

    286

    ○王叟
    　　宝历初，长沙有民王叟者，家贫，力田为业。一日耕于野，为蚯蚓螫其臂，痛楚甚。遂驰以归，其痛益不可忍，夜呻而晓，昼吟而夕，如是者凡旬余。有医者云：“此毒之甚者也。病之始，庶药有及。状且深矣，则吾不得而知也。”后数日，病益甚，忽闻臂有声，幽然而微，若蚯蚓者。又数日，其声益大，如合千万音，其痛亦随而多焉。是夕乃卒

    Explanation

    286

    During the early years of the Baoli era, there was a poor farmer named Wang Sǒu in Changsha. One day, while working in the fields, he was stung by an earthworm on his arm, and the pain was severe. He rushed back home, but the pain became increasingly unbearable. He moaned at night and groaned during the day, and this continued for about ten days. A traditional Chinese physician said, "This is a very severe poison. At the beginning of the illness, perhaps medicine could help. But now the condition is deep, and I cannot know what to do." After several more days, his illness worsened. Suddenly, he heard a faint sound from his arm, as if it were the sound of an earthworm. A few days later, the sound grew louder, as if combining thousands of sounds, and the pain increased accordingly. That night, he died.

    287

    ○韦君
    　　有御史韦君，尝従事江夏。后以奉使至京，既还，道次商于馆亭中。忽见亭柱有白蜘蛛曳而下，状甚微。韦君曰：“是人之患也。吾闻汝虽小，螫人，良药无及。”因以指杀焉。俄又有一白者下，如前所杀之。且观其上，有网为窟，韦乃命左右挈帚尽扫去，且曰：“为人患者，吾已除矣。”明日将去，因以手抚其柱，忽觉指痛不可忍之，乃是有一白蜘蛛螫其上。韦君惊，即拂去。俄遂肿延，不数日而尽一臂。由是肩舁至江夏。医药无及，竟以左臂溃为血，血尽而终。先是，韦君先夫人在江夏，梦一白衣人谓曰：“我弟兄三人，其二人为汝子所杀。吾告上帝，帝用悯其冤，且遂吾请。”言毕，夫人惊寤。甚异之，恶不能言。后旬余而韦君至，具得其状，方寤所梦，觉为梦日，果其馆亭时也。夫人泣曰：“其能久乎？”数日而韦君终矣

    Explanation

    287

    ○Wei Jun
    　　There was a magistrate named Wei Jun, who once served in Jiangxia. Later, on a mission to the capital, he returned and stayed at an inn in Shangyu along the way. Suddenly, he saw a white spider crawling down from the pillar, very small in size. Wei Jun said, "This is a trouble for people. I heard that even though you are small, you can sting people, and good medicine will be too late." He then killed it with his finger. Soon another white one came down, just like the one he had killed. Looking up, there was a web as a den. Wei then ordered his attendants to sweep it all away with brooms and said, "The troublemaker, I have already eliminated." The next day, when he was leaving, he touched the pillar with his hand and suddenly felt his finger painfully unbearable. It turned out that a white spider had stung him. Wei was startled and immediately brushed it off. Soon it swelled and spread, within a few days, the entire arm was affected. As a result, he was carried to Jiangxia on a stretcher. Medicine could not help, and eventually, his left arm became sores filled with blood, and he died after the blood ran out. Before this, Wei's wife was in Jiangxia and dreamed of a white-clad person who said, "We three brothers, two of us were killed by your son. I told the Heavenly Emperor, who pitied our injustice and granted my request." After saying this, the wife woke up in shock. She found it very strange and could not express her feelings. A few weeks later, Wei arrived, and she learned the details, realizing what she had dreamed. Realizing that the day of the dream was the same as the day he stayed at the inn, she wept and said, "How long can he last?" A few days later, Wei died.

    288

    ○陆颙
    　　吴郡陆颙，家于长城，其世以明经仕。颙自幼嗜面，为食愈多而质愈瘦。及长，従本郡贡于礼部，既下第，遂为生太学中。知

    Explanation

    288

    A Man Named Lu Yong

    Lu Yong from Wu Prefecture lived in Changcheng, and his family had been officials through the imperial examinations on the Confucian classics. From a young age, Lu Yong enjoyed eating, and the more he ate, the thinner he became. As he grew older, he was selected from his prefecture to submit to the Ministry of Rites for the imperial examinations, but he failed and subsequently became a student at the Imperial Academy.

    289

    后数月，有胡人数辈挈酒食诣其门。既坐，顾谓颙曰：“吾南越人，长蛮貊中，闻唐天子庠罗天下英俊，且欲以文物化动四夷，故我航海梯山来中华，将观太学文物之光。唯吾子峨焉其冠，襜焉其裾，庄然其容，肃然其仪，真唐朝儒生也。故我愿与子交欢。”颙谢曰：“颙幸得籍于太学，然无他才能，何足下见爱之深也？”于是相与酣宴，极欢而去。颙，信士也，以为群胡不我欺。旬余，群胡又至，持金缯为颙寿。颙至疑其有他，即固拒之。胡人曰：“吾子居长安中，惶惶然有饥寒色，故持金缯为子仆马一日之费，所以交吾子欢耳。岂有他哉幸勿疑我也。”颙不得已，受金缯。及胡人去，太学中诸生闻之，偕来谓颙曰：“彼胡率爱利不顾其身，争盐米之微，尚致相贼杀者，宁肯弃金缯为朋友寿乎且太学中诸生甚多，何为烛厚君耶君匿身郊野间，以避再来也。”颙遂侨居于渭水上，杜门不出

    Explanation

    289

    Several months later, a group of several barbarians brought wine and food to Yu's door. After sitting down, they turned and said to Yu, "I am a person from Nanyue, growing up among the barbarians. I have heard that the Emperor of Tang gathers the talented people from all over the world, and wishes to use culture and civilization to influence the four barbarian tribes. Therefore, I have sailed across the sea and climbed over the mountains to come to China, to see the brilliance of the Taixue's culture and civilization. Only your son has a towering hat, a flowing robe, a dignified appearance, and a solemn demeanor. Truly, he is a scholar of the Tang Dynasty. Therefore, I wish to befriend you." Yu thanked them, saying, "I am fortunate to be registered at the Taixue, but I have no other talents. Why do you love me so much?" They then drank together, had a great feast, and left happily. Yu, a trustworthy man, believed that the group of barbarians did not deceive him. After several days, the group of barbarians came again, bringing gold and silk to wish Yu a long life. Yu suspected that they had another intention, so he firmly refused them. The barbarians said, "Your son lives in Chang'an, looking hungry and cold. Therefore, we brought gold and silk to cover your expenses for a day, so that we could be friends with you. We have no other intentions. Please do not doubt us." Yu had no choice but to accept the gold and silk. When the barbarians left, the other students at the Taixue heard about it and came together to tell Yu, "Those barbarians are greedy for profit and do not care about themselves. They fight over small things like salt and rice, even to the point of killing each other. How could they give up gold and silk to wish a friend a long life? Moreover, there are many students at the Taixue. Why did you treat me so favorably? You hid yourself in the suburbs to avoid them coming again." Yu then moved to live by the Wei River and closed his door, not going out.

    290

    仅月余，群胡又诣其门。颙大惊，胡人喜曰：“比君在太学中，我未得尽言，今君退居郊野，果吾心也。”既坐，胡人挈颙手曰：“我之来，非偶然也。盖有求于君耳，幸望许之。且我所祈，于君固无害，于我则大惠也。”而言曰：“谨受教。”胡人曰：“吾子好食面乎？”曰：“然。”又曰：“食面者，非君也，乃君肚中一虫耳。今我欲以一粒药进君，君饵之，当吐出虫。则我以厚价従君易之，其可乎？”颙曰：“若诚之，又安有不可耶！”已而胡人出一粒药，其色光紫，命饵之。有顷，遂吐出一虫，长二寸许，色青，状如蛙。胡人曰：“此名‘消面虫’，实天下之奇宝也。

    Explanation

    290

    Only a month or so later, a group of barbarians came to his door. You (the addressee) were greatly surprised, but the barbarian was pleased and said, "When you were still at the national university, I could not fully express myself. Now that you have retreated to the outskirts, this is exactly what I hoped for." After they sat down, the barbarian took You's hand and said, "My coming here was not by chance. I have a request for you, and I hope you will agree. Moreover, what I wish for will cause you no harm, but it will be a great benefit for me." You replied, "I will follow your advice." The barbarian then asked, "Do you like eating noodles?" You answered, "Yes." The barbarian continued, "The one who eats the noodles is not you, but a worm in your stomach. I wish to give you a single pill. If you take it, you will vomit out the worm. Then I will exchange it for a high price. Would that be acceptable?" You said, "If you truly believe this, how can there be anything wrong?" Soon after, the barbarian produced a pill that shone a bright purple color and instructed You to take it. After a short while, You vomited out a worm about two inches long, green in color, and shaped like a frog. The barbarian said, "This is called the 'noodle-eating worm,' and it is indeed a rare treasure in the world."

    291

    ”颙曰：“何以识之？”胡人曰：“吾每旦见宝气亘天，在太学中，故我访君而取之。然自一月余，清旦望之，见其气移于渭水上，果君迁居焉。夫此虫禀天地中和之气而结，故好食面，盖以麦自秋始种，至来年夏季方始成实，受天地四时之全气，故嗜其味焉。君宜以面食之，可见矣。”颙即以面斗余致其前，虫乃食之立尽。颙又问曰：“此虫安使用也！”胡人曰：“夫天下之奇宝，俱禀中和之气。此虫乃中和之粹也。执其本而取其末，其远乎哉！”既而以筒盛其虫，又金函扃之，命颙致于寝室。谓颙曰：“明日当再来。”及明旦，胡人以十辆重辇，金玉缯帛约数万献于颙，共持金函而去。颙自此大富，致园屋为治生具，日食粱肉，衣鲜衣，游于长安中，号豪士

    Explanation

    291

    Zheng asked, "How do you know this?" The trapper replied, "Every morning I see the aura of treasure spanning the sky, located in the Imperial Academy, so I came to visit you to obtain it. However, for more than a month, when I looked at it early in the morning, I saw the aura had moved to the Wei River. Indeed, you have moved there. This insect is formed by the harmonious essence of heaven and earth, so it likes to eat noodles. Because wheat is sown in autumn and only ripens in summer of the following year, it receives the complete essence of the four seasons from heaven and earth, therefore it loves its taste. You should feed it with noodles, and you will see." Zheng immediately brought a basket of noodles to the insect, and it ate them all at once. Zheng then asked, "What use is this insect?" The trapper said, "All the rare treasures in the world share the harmonious essence. This insect embodies the essence of harmony. To grasp the root and take the branch, is it far from that?" Afterward, he put the insect in a tube, locked it in a golden box, and instructed Zheng to place it in his bedroom. He told Zheng, "Tomorrow I will come again." By the next morning, the trapper arrived with ten heavily loaded carts, carrying a large amount of gold, jade, silk, and cloth, and offered them to Zheng. Together, they carried the golden box away. From then on, Zheng became very wealthy, building gardens and houses as means of livelihood, eating fine food every day, wearing fine clothes, and wandering around Chang'an, being called a wealthy and influential man.

    292

    仅岁余，群胡又来，谓颙曰：“吾子能与我偕游海中乎我欲探海中之奇宝以耀天下，而吾子岂非好奇之士耶！”颙既以甚富，又素享闲逸自遂，即与群胡俱至海上。胡人结宇而居，于是置油膏于银鼎中，构火其下，投虫于鼎中，炼之，七日不绝燎。忽有一童，分发，衣青襦，自海水中出，捧月盘，盘中有径寸珠甚多，来献胡人。胡人大声叱之。其童色惧，捧盘而去。僮去食顷，又有一玉女，貌极冶，衣雾绡之衣，佩王珥珠，翩翩自海中而出，捧紫玉盘，中有珠数十，来献胡人。胡人骂之，玉女捧盘而去。

    Explanation

    292

    After only a year or so, the group of barbarians came again and said to Yong, "Can you, my son, accompany me on a journey to the sea? I wish to explore the sea for its wondrous treasures to dazzle the world, and aren't you, my son, a man of curiosity?" Yong, having become very wealthy and having always enjoyed a life of leisure and self-satisfaction, then accompanied the group of barbarians to the sea. The barbarians built a dwelling and settled there. They placed oil and fat in a silver cauldron, set fire beneath it, and threw insects into the cauldron. They refined them, the fire burning continuously for seven days. Suddenly, a child appeared, with disheveled hair and wearing a green tunic, emerging from the seawater, holding a moon-shaped tray. The tray contained many pearls, each about an inch in diameter, and the child came to offer the pearls to the barbarians. The barbarians shouted at him angrily. The child, frightened, took the tray and left. After the child had left for a meal's worth of time, another jade maiden appeared, extremely beautiful, wearing a robe made of misty gauze and adorned with a royal diadem and pearl earrings. She gracefully emerged from the sea, holding a purple jade tray containing dozens of pearls, and came to offer the pearls to the barbarians. The barbarians cursed at her, and the jade maiden took the tray and left.

    293

    俄有一仙人，戴瑶碧冠，帔霞衣，捧绛帕籍，籍中有一珠，径三寸许，奇光泛空，照数十步。仙人以珠献胡人，胡人笑而授之。喜谓颙曰：“至宝来矣。”即命绝燎。自鼎中收虫，置金函中。其虫虽炼之且久，而跳跃如初。胡人吞其珠，谓颙曰：“子随我入海中，慎无惧。”颙即执胡人佩带，従而入焉。其海水皆豁开数十步，鳞介之族，俱辟易回去。游龙宫，入蛟室，珍珠怪宝，惟意所择。才一夕，而获甚多。胡人谓颙曰：“此可以致亿万之货矣。”已而又以珍贝数品遗于颙。货于南越，获金千镒，由是益富。其后竟不仕，老于闽越中也。

    Explanation

    293

    Soon there appeared a fairy, wearing a jade-green crown and a robe of cloud-like silk, holding a red silk cloth. Inside the cloth was a pearl, about three inches in diameter, its strange light spreading through the air and illuminating several dozen steps. The fairy presented the pearl to the barbarian, who smiled and handed it to him. Delighted, the barbarian said to You, "The treasure has come!" He immediately ordered the fire to be extinguished. He collected the insect from the cauldron and placed it in a golden box. Though the insect had been refined for a long time, it still jumped as before. The barbarian swallowed the pearl and told You, "You shall follow me into the sea; be careful not to be afraid." You took hold of the barbarian's pendant and followed him in. The sea water parted for several dozen steps, and the fish and shellfish all retreated. They toured the dragon palace and entered the cave of the water dragon, where pearls and strange treasures were to be chosen at will. Only one night, and they gained a great deal. The barbarian said to You, "This can bring in millions of goods." Later, he gave several kinds of precious shells to You. They sold them in the South Yue region and gained a thousand yi of gold, from then on becoming even richer. Afterward, he never took office and grew old in the Min Yue region.
